Headline: Nigeria: Ensuring Road Safety
Body_Text:
Kayode Olagunju, Sector Commander of the Lagos State Federal Road
Safety Corps, says road users must imbibe a disciplined road culture
The Lagos State Sector Commander of the Federal Road Safety Corp,
FRSC, Kayode Olagunju, can be said to have a passion for souls. Like
someone who has entered into a vow, he is determined to ensure that
road users in the country stay alive even if it means infringing on
their fundamental human rights, as he maintains that "nobody has the
right to kill himself." Olagunju cuts the picture of a man of great
resolve.
He waxes resolute on issues that are fundamental to the operations of
the road safety body. This will be evident, for instance, when you
listen to him talk about the need for drivers to make use of the seat
belt: "We've also observed that people know the importance of seat
belt when driving but they choose to ignore it ... By the time we
begin to enforce its use next year, the people will know we can bark
and bite." The FRSC will begin to enforce the compulsory use of seat
belt on the road in January 2003. Seat belts properly fastened have
proved to save the lives of many vehicle occupants, especially the
driver, when accidents occurred. And often when a vehicle
somersaults, properly fastened seat belts reduce the risk of
injuries, Olagunju emphasized.
His determination can also be glimpsed from his on-going battle with
touts over new vehicle number plates and driver's licensing scheme.
It is widely believed that it is this duel that accounted for the 13
July 2002 attempted murder on the life of the FRSC boss.
It was interesting watching steely resolve plastered on Olagunju's
face when he, in company of two of his officers, paid a courtesy
visit on the Independent Communications Network Limited, ICNL,
publishers of TheNEWS, TEMPO and PMNews. During the visit, on 28
November 2002, he urged Nigerians to view road safety as a collective
responsibility so as to save more lives from untimely death. He
revealed that current statistics showed that between 1992 to 2002, a
period of 10 years, 170,964 road accidents were recorded on Nigerian
roads with 75,292 deaths and 204,240 injuries. Majority of the
injured persons, Olagunju lamented, are permanently bed-ridden at the
National Orthopedic Hospital, Igbobi, Lagos, and have been conferred
with the sobriquet, 'landlords.' So frightening is the accident rate
in the country that the FRSC boss calculated that 20 lives are lost
in road accidents daily with an average of 55 persons sustaining
injuries. He revealed that in Europe, America and Asia, especially in
Japan, there are just about five vehicle accidents per 10,000
registered vehicles. These contrast sharply with Nigeria's record of
150 deaths per 10,000 registered vehicles.
The Sector Commander blamed the increasing rate of accidents in
Nigeria on the careless attitude of motorists to driving. He noted
that most drivers have misinterpreted 'democracy' for "total freedom
to commit manslaughter on the road" and go scot free. He went on:
"You'll find somebody cruising at 140 kilometre per hour and when you
stop such an individual, he will say you are infringing on his
fundamental human rights." Olagunju wondered if democracy grants to
people the right to commit suicide.
Also, bad roads that dot the nation's landscape and an increase in
vehicles that are not roadworthy have contributed to the frightening
rate of accidents in the country.
The FRSC waits impatiently for the National Assembly to pass into law
the ban on the use of mobile phone while driving. Though the
introduction of mobile phones in the country has facilitated business
development, Olagunju said it would be dangerous to continue to allow
motorists use the device while on the wheel. "It is an offence to
drive with one hand using the other to receive a phone call,
especially if you realize that driving requires 100 per cent
concentration." For those who say there is no law that bans phoning
while driving, Olagunju has this to say: " We can also say there is
no law that says you cannot eat pounded yam while driving. And we all
know too well that you cannot lap a woman when driving. The
fundamental issue is that if you are using one hand to phone, there
are certain actions of yours that are violating traffic regulations."
So, as the Commission awaits the legislation, it would ensure drivers
save their own lives by using both hands when driving.
Already, about 2,500 offenders in Lagos have been arrested and
penalized this year for phoning while driving. Even though fines are
minimal, Olagunju enjoined motorists to avoid being arrested as the
procedure for remitting the fines are 'deliberately made cumbersome
and frustrating.' This, he said, is to discourage road users from
endangering lives. "Some people have often complained about our fines
being low. Yes, it is low because we are not a money-generating
agency. We are created to enforce and restore order on the road. But
beyond the fines, our task is to instill the spirit of compliance and
orderliness on our roads," the FRSC boss maintained.

Headline: Nigeria: So Long, 'Egbon' Fatoyinbo
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bukola Olatunji pays tribute to the creator and co-ordinator of the Communication for Education and Development (COMED) programme and Senior Communication Specialist with the World Bank, Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo, who passed on suddenly on Sunday, December 1, 2002 at 59
'Friendships are of many kinds
As through our lives we go
Some are short but very sweet
For parting makes them so...'
This may be an unusual piece for this page. But then, it is a short story of an unusual man. Executive Secretary of the Association for the Development of Education in Africa (ADEA), Mr. Mamadou Ndoye described Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo as "an exceptional man, so generous, so available for everybody."
President of the Nigerian Guild of Editors, Mrs. Remi Oyo said at his graveside last Saturday in Ilesha, that Akin Fatoyinbo 'was a mentor and valuable friend of Nigerian Editors and journalists. He was a wonderful friend."
Indeed he was a great friend to everyone who crossed his path and I must be one of the newest friends he made. I met him for the first time on Sunday, September 1, 2002. Thirteen short Sundays later, and by a cruel coincidence, on December 1, 2002, he was gone. It is hard for me to talk about Fatoyinbo in the past tense, but what can I do now?
He died in Dar-es-Salam, Tanzania where he had gone, as usual, to prepare Tanzanian and other African journalists for the coverage of the African Ministers of Education (MINEDAF) conference, which was held between December 2, and 6.
To say Fatoyinbo's death was a surprise or a shock is saying the least. Mrs. Oyo, who was with him in Dar-es-Salam when he passed on said 'I wish Akin lived forever. Akin would have loved to live forever."
Mr. Wale Ajao of the Vanguard Newspaper who was also at the conference and who saw Fatoyinbo the day before he died told me "even the man himself would have been surprised by his death."
Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo, who was a staff of the World Bank for over 20 years, designed the Communication for Education and Development (COMED) programme. He was also a prime mover of the ADEA African Education Journalism Award, which I won.
Part of the prize was a two-week study tour of major media organisations which have departments devoted to education in Paris, London and Washington, beginning with seminars in Paris, and ending with visits to the World Bank Headquarters in Washington. The Washington leg was not in the original schedule. My Fatoyinbo had been instrumental to the addition.
Editor of The Comet newspaper, Mr. Gbenga Omotosho and I had arrived Paris, France early morning on Sunday, September 1 2002; to begin the study tour. My story that won the award ('How Far can a Meal Go?) was published in the paper and ADEA had invited the Editors of the publication in which the articles (Two Anglophone and two Francophone) were published to also come on the study tour.
A couple of hours after checking into Hotel Nicolo I went into the small Dinning Room for breakfast and found three people already having theirs. I greeted them and made to sit down when the oldest of them, wearing a beautiful adire shirt, said, "this must be Bukola Olatunji, the award winner. I am Akin Fatoyinbo." I went, "Oh! I am so pleased to meet you sir. I have heard so much about you, but somehow we had never met."
Indeed, I had heard his name mentioned in discussions about training of African journalists, education in Africa and so on. But had neither met him nor seen his photograph.
If I knew that "Egbon (Big Brother)", as Omotosho fondly addressed Fatoyinbo throughout the exciting trip, had exactly three months to live from that day I met him, perhaps I would have prayed fervently, like Jesus Christ did, that the cup pass over him. I might have also asked him to tell me what must have been a very exciting story of his life.
I do not know how many languages he spoke. When we got to the UNESCO headquarters in Paris and Fatoyinbo discovered that the lady who conducted us round the premises was Spanish, he spoke her language to her. Of course he spoke English, French, German and not to say, Yoruba languages, including his 'Ijesha' dialect. How he came about all these languages would have made an interesting read.
When he learnt of his passing, Mr. John Eremu of the New Vision of Uganda, who came second in the ADEA contest wrote, "surely this world is so cruel, but God has his own plans. If it was humanly possible, God should have given Fatoyinbo a few more years. Such a nice man going so quickly when he had just come into our lives."
Ms. Rose Akakpo of Le Point au Quotidien of Benin Republic, who came first in the French Category of the competition did not say a word at the funeral on Saturday. We just hugged and wept, lamenting our common loss.
I did not set out to study him during the tour, but I just got drawn to him form the first seminar on the first day. Mr. Fatoyinbo was a damn good speaker. His voice had this childlike ring that made me want to hear him all the time. But he did not say much per time. He preferred to listen to others speak. He was also down to earth. Though way ahead of us on many counts, he did not show it off. "You speak Spanish too?" I had asked him. "Just a little to get by", he replied with a smile.
Maybe it does not make any difference now, but looking back, I am so glad I told Mr. Fatoyinbo how I felt about him. We parted at the end of the tour on September 15 when I left Washington for Los Angeles. On my third day in LA, I had sent him a long e-mail telling him where I was and what I was doing. I am also glad that I saved a draft of that mail, just as I have kept every copy of his mails to me.
I checked my mailbox this past week to copy the last two paragraphs of that long mail:
"I am sorry if I bore you, but you deserve these details because I would not be here in the first place but for the way God has used you to extend the study visit here."
"I want to add that I thoroughly enjoyed your company. You remind me of my father, a very simple, fair, caring and intelligent man who treats everyone with respect and understanding. I will tell him about you when I go to see him in Kwara State in November by God's grace. Bye for now and God bless, Bukola."
Apart from the qualities listed above, Mr. Fatoyinbo also had my father's build - slim, tall and straight. That is not to talk of that brisk walk of someone rushing to keep an appointment, and the hard work.
At over 70, my father still stands tall and straight. I did not get to see him in November as planned because I postponed it for a family meeting scheduled for December 7 by which time Fatoyinbo was no more. With so much happiness at seeing my siblings and me and planning for a brother's wedding, I could not tell my father about Fatoyinbo anymore. It will still take a while from now to talk about him without forcing back tears.
How many of those kind gestures can I list? As the days of the study tour rolled by, our different weaknesses began to manifest. But Mr. Fatoyinbo was never impatient with any of us. He in fact made excuses for our inadequacies, made everyone of us feel special. He came down to our level and befriended us.
Our last day in Paris featured the Award Night aboard the boat 'Montebello' on the River Seine. At the end of the ceremony, Mr. Fatoyinbo saw Prof. Michael Omolewa, Nigeria's Ambassador and Permanent Delegate to UNESCO to his car and opened the door for him. The humble man that Omolewa is, He refused, saying, "No Egbon. You get in and let me take you to your hotel." Fatoyinbo said he had to make sure everyone left before he did. Omolewa also insisted that he should be the one to open a car door for Fatoyinbo. So the latter had to give up.
We all thought Mr. Fatoyinbo was very English, but he was not as English as we imagined. On our second day in Paris, Mr. Omotoso told us of his visit, with his friend who is lives in the city, to a Nigerian eatery (Miliki Spot) run by a man that preferred to be called "Baba Miliki" and his wife "Iya Miliki". The pounded yam he ate, he said, was excellent. An 'Ijesha' man that he was, Mr. Fatoyinbo said we must check it out later that day. As we left Nicolo, it began to drizzle. I thought that was the end of the outing. But Mr. Fatoyinbo said, "we can't allow this rain to stop us. Come, there is a Monoprix (a departmental store) somewhere down the road." We went in and he bought me a beautiful raincoat (now a memento of great value) and took an umbrella for himself.
The taxi that took us went could not locate the address after going round and round. That cost a fortune. I said we should take the nearest metro back to the hotel, but he said, "never mind, we will find it." We eventually had to call Baba Miliki who came to pick us where we were. It turned out that Baba Miliki and Fatoyinbo were from the same town. Then I heard him speak his Ijesha dialect for the first time. He wanted to know when the couple last visited Nigeria and advised them to do so more often. When the steaming hot pounded yam and fresh fish came, Fatoyinbo ate it with his hands and like President Obasanjo once said, licked his fingers.
Although we were all adults on the trip, Mr. Fatoyinbo and Mrs. Thanh Hoa Desruelles, ADEA's Information and Communication Officer looked after us like their children and were referred to as 'papa' and 'mama'. They cared about our needs, what we ate, where we ate, if we slept well and what have you, without breathing down our backs. Mr. Fatoyinbo even helped us to get the best value for our par diem. he suggested what kind of phone card to buy and where, where to get the best bargain to dry clean our clothes and where to change money. He was available to answer any question. He could read our mood, knew who was uncomfortable and acted swiftly.
On arrival at the Waterloo Station in London, we needed some pounds. He looked at the exchange rate, did a little arithmetic and concluded that we would get more if we changed the money as a group. At the end of the day, Mr. Fatoyinbo over paid some of us. He would rather have it that way than for us to individually change our money and have less.
Listening to others pay their tribute at Fatoyinbo's graveside last Saturday, I say with all humility that my assessment of him was dead accurate.
President of ADEA, Mr. Ahlin-Byll Cataria, who could not hold back the tears as the casket was lowered into the grave said on behalf of the Association, "the story of Akin's life left a strong mark on the circle of people who were close to him: the members of his family and his friends. Akin always displayed noble-heartedness and steadfast caring for his loved ones. Gifted with an outstanding feel for human relationships, he was able to give backing when and where needed and his dealings with one and all were congenial.
"Akin's passing has left an emptiness which cannot be filled,. But our faith, conveyed in our prayers, is that Akin was a good man and that God will recognise one of his own."
The African Education Journalism Award, Cataria said, is now to be known as the Akin Fatoyinbo Award.
The Communications and Partnership staff of the World Bank said, "Akin was great source of inspiration to those he worked with, especially those of us who joined the World bank many years after he did. He very readily shared his experiences and was a mentor to so many of us who work in the communication field in the Bank. He was very much an original."
His friend, Mr. Sunny Aminaghan said "I have known Akin Fatoyinbo for over 25 years. He was a very kind hearted, loving and caring friend and brother." Ndoye had added in a grave voice during my discussion with him, "talking about Akin is very difficult. This is a great loss for me because he was not just a colleague, he was also my friend."
Country Director of the World Bank, Mr. Mark Tomlinson who also attended Saturday's burial ceremonies said, "Akin Fatoyinbo was a wonderful person, a great colleague, a very powerful intellectual and a very talented communicator. We will miss him in the bank; we feel emptiness now that he's left us. It is very sad."
As soon as we arrived in Washington, Mr. Fatoyinbo muttered more than once, "I hope my daughter will be able to come. I have to call my daughter." He did call his daughter, Lola, 21-year-old final year student of Biology at the University of Virginia in the United States, and she came to the Wyndham City Centre where we were lodged. No point stressing how they related. One should not expect any less from a father and his daughter. Mr. Omotosho had to ask if Lola was Fatoyinbo's first child to which he answered "she is the one and only." As Lola confirmed to me last Saturday, it turned out to be the last time of father and daughter being together.
How can I forget the Early Childhood conference in Asmara, Eritrea? How Mr. Fatoyinbo handled the issue of a lady agent who wanted to fleece some of us of our hard earned money. Fatoyinbo was indeed a diplomat.
Then our last meeting at the High level Group meeting on Education for All (EFA) held in Abuja. Mr. Fatoyinbo had written for me three e - mail addresses (another memento) through which I could contact Mrs. Remi Oyo for daily update on MINEDAF, which he did not live to participate in.
Fatoyinbo was simple a to the very end. his burial place was under a big tree on the serene 'Hill Top' ('man pass man', as it was named by the former ownner), where birds make melody all the time, along the Ido Ijesha/Ife Road. Lola told me that her father bought the place from an English man whose name she did not remember, and who was a gold miner.
May his soul rest in perfect peace.

Headline: Nigeria: Kayode Fayemi - Still Fighting
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Dr. Kayode Fayemi says five years of struggle for democracy have
only opened doors for more challenges.
For Dr. Kayode Fayemi, director, Centre for Democracy and Development
(CDD), the struggle to enthrone democracy in Nigeria has just begun.  As a
matter of fact, the battle has shifted from the struggle for transition to
that of consolidation of democracy. "We have moved from the struggle to
remove the military from political authority in Nigeria. But that in itself
has opened up another phase in the struggle."
Kayode Fayemi is not operating in a strange land. For him, activism is
not just pastime but a full-time job. He grew up in a home where his
curiosity was encouraged and developed by parents who noticed early enough a
special trait in their child. At the Christ School, Ado-Ekiti, where his
love for books was honed, he was the secretary of the school's press club
and he represented the school in several schools' debate during the period,
a task which earned him the nickname, Current Affairs.
He blossomed during his undergraduate days at the University of
Lagos. He was editor of the campus newspaper, The Watch. He was also the
editor of Alpha, a newspaper published by the Eni-Njoku Hall.
He would however come into national consciousness with the roles he
played during the heyday of the despotic rule of Sani Abacha, when it became
too dangerous for pro-democratic elements to remain within the borders of
Nigeria. Fayemi joined forces with other members of the famed National
Democratic Coalition (NADECO) to wrest Nigeria from the grip of military
rule. He was one of the pillars behind Radio Kudirat, whose duty it was to
counter the false stories Nigerians were being fed.
But three and half years into democratic rule in Nigeria, the battle,
according to Fayemi, is far from being over. With the level of corruption in
the system, as displayed at all the levels of governance, which has
consigned the country to one of the 20 poorest nations in the world, the
battle is definitely not yet over. This, however, is not enough for
Nigerians to lose hope in their nation.
"With the performance of civil rule in the last three and a half years,
Nigerians, now, more than ever, need to be vigilant. For those of us who
were cut in the earlier struggle, it is important that in spite of all the
imperfections of the last three and half years, we cannot afford to revert
to military rule."
The CDD director believes the basic problem confronting the Nigerian
state is the lack of sustainable structures, which has made it virtually
impossible for any physical gain to be made in any sphere of human endeavour
in Nigeria. Ironically, however, Nigerians have continued to excel in
different fields of endeavours outside the country. This, according to
Dr. Fayemi, is principally because Nigerians have for long placed much
premium on personalities instead of on issues and structures.
Though he argues that both personalities and issues cannot be separated
from issues of state governance, he believes that the right structures will
bring about good leaders.
Another problem which he believes has hindered the progress of Nigeria
as a nation is corruption. Though the problem had been with the country from
the time of independence, it however took a new dimension during the eight
years of Babangida's rule. "Babangida it was who institutionalised
corruption in Nigeria."
Fayemi's Centre for Democracy and Development (CDD) is positioning
itself to meet the needs of Nigeria and indeed the West African sub-region
for democratic solutions and consolidation, which, he says, are fundamental
to political stability and economic growth.
The CDD has in the last five years of its creation been involved in
several projects with many governments in the region. Of particular interest
was its involvement with the Nigerian government in the formation of the
Human Rights Violations and Investigation Commission (HRVIC). The fact that
past leaders could be brought before Nigerians to account for their misdeeds
was enough victory for Fayemi.
In spite of his very tight schedules, which see him travelling round the
world in search of peace for mankind, Fayemi, in his spare time, which of
course is rare, still finds enough time for his foremost hobby of reading
history and biographies. "I read a lot of history and biography, not to copy
those people, but to lean from them." One other thing which the struggle had
not taken away from him is his love for pounded yam and palaver soup.

Headline: Nigeria: Akintola Fatoyinbo: It is Well
Body_Text:
Lagos — A lovely painting of two peacocks hangs at the reception of the Peacock Hotel in Dar-es-Salaam. On the painting these words are inscribed: "Joy is not in things but in us".
Peacock Hotel was the last abode of Akintola Fatoyinbo before he died in a nearby hospital on Sunday, December 1 2002.
These words epitomized the life and times of Akintola Fatoyinbo, who I first met in 1983 when I was a Desk Editor in the News Agency of Nigeria. I was then in charge of the Foreign Desk and it was in this capacity that I became first associated with Akin.
Because he was older than me, culture demanded, and I was willing to so bow, to call him 'Uncle' or 'Bros' or be formal and address him as Mr. He was not formal and insisted that I call him by his first name.
Akin was then on secondment from the World Bank and helping to set up the Africa operations of Inter Press Service News Agency headquartered in Harare, Zimbabwe.
That chance meeting marked the beginning of a privileged friendship that has imparted positively not only on my professional work, in my personal life but on that of my family, colleagues and friends.
To know Akin was to love him. He was warm, considerate, selfless and tireless in the pursuit of his work. A polyglot, he tried hard and failed to make me one. Akin was passionate about his family, his sisters, his work, his country and our continent.
His sterling qualities, especially an unparalleled commitment to the noble profession of journalism shone in his tireless work at building the capacities of journalists in Africa.
In the three years that I have served so far as President of the Nigerian Guild of Editors, members and other journalists have benefited from no less than four capacity building workshops through the indefatigable efforts of this wonderful gentleman and quintessential journalist. He was the backbone and supporter of the Guild and its programmes.
Having served as Senior Editor of Deutsche Welle, the German Radio and written while living in Germany for many Nigerian newspapers, Akin was keen on ensuring that those who practice journalism did so without fear or let but with the goal of service to humanity, to the country and continent.
He often prefaced the beginning of our many conversations with "You know what" and then wonderful ideas would follow. Brilliant ideas that have resulted in the establishment of COMED the Communication for Education and Development Programme as a working group of the Association for the Development and Education in Africa (ADEA), a programme sponsored by the World Bank and other donors.
Akin believed that reward should be given for excellence. He believed the lowly could rise to the top of the world. There was no "can't do" in his lexicon. "That is a possibility", he would say, with such an infectious smile.
Akin had such a way with people. He would easily forgive a slight or any let down. He was willing to provide opportunities for any one. He harboured no ill will and to the chagrin of us lesser mortals, Akin will make excuses for those who had exhibited human foibles and thus fallen short of expectations. His forgiveness was genuine and quick, his advice free and unsolicited.
Although Akin had risen to the world stage, he was so self-effacing. I remember on the eve of his death, I teased him about a front-page photograph in a Nigerian newspaper which caught him in the shadows of President Olusegun Obasanjo meeting his Burkina Faso colleague and the Director-General of UNESCO in Abuja.
"Fame at last", I said and he just smiled and proceeded to tell me about his fresh ideas of capacity building efforts for the Nigerian media and plans for the 2003 African Education Journalism Award.
Akin was already famous, many many years before that photograph. Although he had dined with kings, queens, Presidents Ministers, movers and shakers, he had no qualms about sharing a bowl of pounded yam and vegetable soup with the lowly. Akin was an Ijesha man you see, and proud to be one too.
The love and generousity that Akin so freely shared came to full bloom after his demise. Many agreed that a huge void has been created in the firmament but all admitted that we must celebrate Akin and rededicate ourselves to the ideals he lived and died for
I guess it will be correct to admit that Akin died doing what he loved best. Getting journalists and policy makers to communicate the message of development through education properly.
On the eve of his death, he concluded a training programme for Tanzanian journalists in collaboration with Prof. Alfred Opubor, one of Africa's and Nigeria's leading lights in mass communication and my humble self.
Akin did not get a chance to see if his trainees did well. I can confirm that they did along with their colleagues from Nigeria, Ghana, Uganda and Mauritius, as well as colleagues from PANA.
A minute's silence at the plenary of the African Education Ministers' conference formally announced Akin's death. What followed were tears from men and women, powerful but who together with Akin shared love, love for development, love for humanity.
Akin belonged to the world, he belonged to Africa, he belonged to Nigeria and he belonged to us all who loved him, who held him in awe and admiration and were privileged to benefit from his unparalleled loyalty and genuine friendship. For all you have done for us all, we are all indebted to you, Akin, my friend, big brother and mentor.
Akintola Fatoyinbo, 1943-2002. It is well.
Oyo is president of the Nigerian Guild of Editors.

Headline: Uganda: Hotel Naigara is Ambitious
Body_Text:
Kampala — One should not mistake Naigara, as in the hotel, with the famous Niagara Falls of North America. From our understanding, this hotel was named in memory of Nayagala, the late mother of its owner.
Less than 10 years ago, nobody would have ventured to set up a hotel complex in the areas of Najanankumbi, Zanna and the like.
In the case of Zanna, the area where Hotel Millennium stands was nothing but swamp. Conventional wisdom would have dictated that such an undertaking was too risky.
Wrong. The area from Kibuye
Round-about all the way to Seguku along Entebbe Road has grown in leaps and bounds, the latest development being the Naigara Hotel. Off the beaten path, it is nestled in a little cul-de-sac, making it into a sort of hideaway; ideal for those who desire seclusion coupled with a feel of country air.
I am full of praise for those who think beyond and plan for the future, as is the case of the owners of this new property.
Though not yet fully complete, it is habitable, with a conference facility that can accommodate about 200 people. It has 16 complete rooms. Last month, Naigara Hotel opened without any fan fare or much publicity. We decided to check out the food. I must say that the al fresco was perfect for a hotel of this size. The owners wisely decided to woo the population who live within the vicinity rather than rely on city-dwellers.
Accordingly, the food has been priced with the locals in mind. Suburbian living is relatively cheaper than life in the inner city. If one is going to attract these people, one must give them a worthwhile incentive. Low price has always been an attraction.
The menu has been chosen as a la carte, but I honestly feel that it is simply too ambitious and unworkable. Whoever is behind this long-winded document would be well advised to go back to the drawing board and come up with something more suited to an out of town joint.
To wit, the lunch or dinner menu, while going for a very inexpensive sh3,000, failed to live up to its expectations. It was wonderful to see that one could get matooke, yams, Irish and sweet potatoes, rice, chapatis, pumpkin, posho, millet et al. These were served with afro-fish stew, beef stew, brown goat stew, chicken stew, luwombo... the list is endless.
In all fairness, the above would rightly never be called a la carte. Here, I blame the chef for getting carried away with what one can only term as his sense of limited understanding of menu engineering. Somebody once remarked that a little knowledge was dangerous. I am afraid that this was all too evident in the form of the menu card that was presented. We would have thought that more than 100 items for a place this size is just sheer overkill.
Matters are not helped by the numerous spelling gaffes one finds on the menu. Sandwitches, persley, becon, omolette, jacked (potatoes), humbergers (sic), just to mention a few. The management has assured us that they are going to correct these aberrations. This is good news and can only help the hotel.
We had some fried fish that was nothing remarkable, served with tepid rice and a sauce of some sort. I have to admit that there is room for vast improvement in the kitchen and I have no doubt that the powers that be shall act accordingly. The owner insisted on picking up the tab, a generous offer that would nevertheless have set us back a mere sh5,000 for the two of us; chump change!
Ratings
Excellent: xxx
Very good: xx
Worth a visit: x
Not worth a visit: o

Original_Search_Title: American Notes (8)- Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe
Headline: Nigeria: American Notes (8)- Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe
Body_Text:
The conspiracy of my friends was total. Frank Aigbogun, Amuzie Akpaka,
Ikeddy Isiguzo, Ely Obasi, Taiwo Obe and others. They set upon me with this
diktat: Get married! It was 1983. I was 32. We were all on the staff of the
newly established newspaper, The Guardian, and we lived, bachelors all, in
this old one-storey building in Shomolu, Lagos that belonged to the Ibru
family.
I pleaded with my friends to show some understanding, pointing out that
32 years was not dismal for one to still remain unmarried. But they
disagreed. "Get married!" My problem was compounded by the fact that my
friends had a formidable ally in my mother. She let whoever paid any
attention know her attitude in the matter. Had a prophet prophesied that her
own son would be without a wife at 30 years, she would have retorted by
branding the fellow a charlatan and advising them to swap jobs. But, now,
her worst fears had come to pass! And the problem itself was incapable of
explanation, seeing that there were all over the place eligible spinsters
waiting to be carried across the threshold! In the last analysis, did it
indicate that she would not have the joyous privilege of carrying in her
arms a grandchild? When I asked whether the brood of Nnenna, my elder
sister, did not count as grandchildren, she put me in my place: Let's hear
word!
It was in this state of two-sided pressure that, during 1984, I
accompanied a friend to an address in Surulere, Lagos. She had a message for
a Chief Akukwe. When we got to the place, a lady in her mid-20s served us
drinks and engaged in small talk before disappearing. She was Chief Akukwe's
daughter. In my eyes, she did not look bad. Later I sent word to my
mother. I had freshly looked at all sides of everything on the marriage
spectrum, I told her. And it now seemed to me that she had a point all
along. Would it not make some sense if we asked a few questions on the
Akukwe lady? People around when my mother got the message later revealed
that she immediately invited neighbours over for instant celebration. My
mother knew the home of the Akukwes very well. Even though it is in another
town, it cost you only 16 minutes or so to get there from my own father's
compound.
She found out the next time Chief Akukwe was expected from Lagos and
paid a visit to the man of presence and stately bearing. And she decided,
the Matriach, that we were on. The man's daughter would be a knockout in her
own right. Ultimately, we tied the knot and I signed on the dotted line!
Chinweizu was there, his views on marriage notwithstanding. That was how I
got into the world of Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe. His Surulere residence
is a stone's throw from Nduka Obaigbena's ThisWeek magazine. And I often
took my friends from The Guardian and ThisWeek to visit my father-in-law, a
man who told beautiful stories connected to the time Dr. Azikiwe and others
fought for independence wielding nothing but oratorical quintessence. The
late Ely Obasi would often ask that we paid yet another visit. What
heightened our encounters with the man was the bacchanal ecstasies that
framed them, for Chief Akukwe did not hate a tipple!
Often our visits coincided with those of patriarchs like my
father-in-law. He would often let them into the secret of what a
distinguished writer I was, you know, the young boy that "decisioned" the
white man in his own tongue! Sometimes he added that I was level headed, so
well behaved. Needless to say that I enjoyed all the flattery. But it was at
a stage in my life when I did not believe that hot water could kill the
tortoise, and I often wondered whether Chief Akukwe really knew his
son-in-law.
Time passed. On August 22, I went to see him. He was sitting in his
usual position in front of the house, carrying in his arms one of the
grandchildren. I was traveling to the United States the following day. I sat
next to him and we talked some. He was pleased to learn that Ijeoma, my
daughter, was on her way to medical school. "The offspring of the snake
cannot but be long", he observed. "You will have a safe flight." When I rose
to go, he said, "You will see Godwin, I am sure. Tell him we are fine."
Godwin is his eldest son. As my mother-in-law saw me to my jalopy, I turned
to wave. Instead he asked me back. "Tell Godwin that we are fine", he said
again.
Radcliff, Kentucky. On Wednesday October 16, I returned from Cincinnati,
to warm up for Los Angeles. Godwin had developed an idea. He had been having
this feeling impossible to place. He thought his family in Lagos should get
together for thanksgiving at the end of November. He had $500 to burn. All
the Akukwes in Lagos would go to their local church, St. Anthony's Gbaja on
a Sunday morning for a high mass offered on their behalf. Then they would
retire to the father's house for feasting. How did we get the money through?
I telephoned my brother Charles, hitting his GSM. I asked him to leave the
Naira equivalence of $500 with Chief Akukwe. The details of what the money
was for would be sent by e-mail. Charles was visiting Onitsha but would
return to Lagos the weekend and go straight to Surulere to deliver Godwin's
message.
On the morning of Saturday October 19, Godwin had a story. He hadn't
been fair to me, he said. Since I was visiting, he should have given me a
treat well before now. He knew this place in Lexington. They offered choice
food and drinks in assorted variety. We would make the hour-long journey on
the night so he could give me "a real treat". I didn't see why I should not
support his scheme. After launch that day, he had to go to Elizabethtown,
ten minutes away. In his absence, I accessed my e-mails. And there it stood
starkly before me, the news I knew would come before long but which we
thought would tarry. "Dad", wrote my daughter Ijeoma. "Papa is dead. He died
last night in his sleep. Uncle Benjamin came this morning to tell Mummy."
The message was copied to Godwin's e-mail address.
I telephoned Lagos immediately and confirmed the terrible news. Part of
the message I left with Chief Akukwe household on August 22 was that Godwin
would be home in time for Christmas. It meant that the father could not
wait. When Godwin came back, he said he required some sleep. When he woke,
we would set out for Lexington. I remembered that when I first arrived, he
had looked me in the face and asked, "How is my father?" I had replied that
the man was fine but that the trot downhill had become all descent. For some
time, it had of course been clear to many that Chief Akukwe was on the way
to joining his ancestors. He had slowed down considerably; in fact he was
frail. I allowed Godwin a few minutes rest and requested to have a word. He
joined me in the living room and turned down the TV, which was saying
something about the Beltway sniper.
"I have news from Lagos", I said. "And t is not the best."
"What is it?
"Don't be alarmed", I told him. "But your father is dead." Godwin felt
like someone pole axed. He is a spitting image of his father, and they were
close. He placed his right palm on his temple, closed his eyes and reclined
on the chair. Two minutes later he sat up. "How did you get the news?"
The money sent through my brother became handy for organising the first
things about the Chief Akukwe's last rites. Godwin's anticipated
thanksgiving will take place, after all, from November 27. For if a Nigerian
lived to be 86, it called for thanks and praises to the One with the knife
and the yam. We, kindred spirits, would no longer sit in his porch and
sip. But the memory of our encounters that lasted for nearly 20 years would
linger. My one regret is that my hands would not be among those to carry his
casket through those elaborately carved gates that gave into his compound,
the ancestral home of his father Chief Ngwuakpu, who was the traditional
ruler of Eziowelle. But my mother would be there, 84 and vibrant. Do not be
surprised if she raised an arm of salutation and the place resonated with a
ditty to the deceased

Original_Search_Title: How This Teenager Escaped From a Kidnapper
Headline: Nigeria: How This Teenager Escaped From a Kidnapper
Body_Text:
A 13-year-old boy who lives with his elder cousin at Asaba, the Delta State capital, escaped from a man who had earlier on kidnapped him at gun point. Ugwoke and his aunt narrated their experiences to Okey Ndiribe who was at Asaba recently.
Okwudili Ugwoke had just walked down Celestial Church Street, Asaba, to buy some chewing gum. Afterwards, he decided to take a walk to the intersection of a major road in that area of the city. As he was walking along the road, a Peugeot 504 station wagon car with tinted glasses suddenly stopped beside him. The man behind the wheel instantly pointed a gun at Okwudili and ordered him to get into the car or get shot. The youngster froze. His eyes darted to the left and right looking for an escape route. But the kidnapper who had a fierce glint in his eyes, guessed what was going on in Okuwdili's mind and repeated his order in a very harsh voice. His legs wobbling, Okwudili climbed into the car and the kidnapper sped off with him.
Speaking to our reporter about his experience, Okwudili said the kidnapper held the gun with his right hand while he held the steering with the left. He continued: "As the man (his captor) drove the car towards the River Niger bridge, it had a fault. He then drove into a mechanic's workshop to repair the car."
Okwudili summoned a rare courage for a child of his age after he got over the initial shock of being kidnapped in broad daylight. He began to plot his escape. Said he: "While he was repairing the car, he had an argument with the mechanics. It was then that I quietly opened the car door, sneaked out from the car and ran away." And after his miraculous escape at the mechanic's workshop, the teenager ran as fast as his legs could carry him. "As I was running away, I saw some yam sellers near the bridge. As I was there, I saw the kidnapper again when he drove past towards Onitsha at top speed. I shouted that this was the man who attempted to take me and that he's carrying a gun."
Still terrified, the yam sellers then told the young Okwudili to stay with the night guards in that area for the night. They also told him that in the morning, he would be taken away from there. That night, he slept at a yam seller's shed. According to him, "they fed me and also rubbed a local medicine on me. They told me that it was to prevent anybody from kidnapping me again."
The following morning, the yam sellers made good their promise and took Okwudili to Onitsha. According to the teenager, "in the morning, they came and took me in a car to Onitsha. I met somebody in the car who asked me where I hailed from. I told him that I am a native of Nsukka. The man asked me to describe the section of Nsukka I came from. I did. He told me that he knew that area but that he wasn't going to Nsukka at that time."
But the man promised to take him to the studio of Anambra Broadcasting Service (ABS) at Onitsha. "This man and the first person who accompanied me from the yam sellers area at Asaba, took me to the studio of ABS," he said. When this young kidnap victim got to ABS and on enquiry, established his identity, he also disclosed the profession of his elder cousin and guardian. "I told them that he works in a bank. They asked me the name of the bank. I could not remember the name of the bank, but I told them what I knew is that, he works in a bank whose vehicles are painted in red stripes. The man said that he knew the bank."
After this initial interrogation, Okwudili was then taken into another section of the studio where he was shown to the viewing public on the screen. Radio announcements were also broadcast. And shortly after the broadcast, his elder cousin arrived the studio and took him away. "I was very happy to meet my people again. I thank God for saving me from that man who wanted to take me away by force. I don't know what he would have done to me and that's why I thank God for saving me."
For Okwudili's auntie, Mrs Chinweuba Ugwoke, it was a traumatic experience while Okwudili's sudden disappearance lasted. When the young boy didn't return home the night he was kidnapped, his aunt initially thought that it was a bad dream that would soon be over. She had heard stories of how children were kidnapped but never imagined she would face a similar situation. So, when she came back from her office to charge her car battery at a nearby battery charger's place, and didn't meet her young brother-in-law, she thought that he may have gone to play in the neighbourhood. According to her, "what happened was that on Wednesday, September 4, 2002, when I returned home from the office at about 11.30 in the morning, I observed that the entrance door to our apartment was open and I couldn't find my husband's small cousin that stays with us. In anger I locked up the entire place and returned to the office to continue my work for the rest of the day.
"We thank God that he's been found through radio announcement," Chinweuba says.

Headline: Nigeria: Don Cautions Against Eating 'Fast Foods'
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Refrain from food that has colourants, seasonings and flavourants which are especially common with "fast foods", has been recommended as panacea to longevity. The Dean of the Students Affairs of the University of Ilorin, Prof. Musbau Aderoumi Akanji, has said.
In a 53-page inaugural lecture at the University of Ilorin, Akanji said that the presence of additives makes digestion difficult and diminishes human metabolism. "We should refrain from food additive, that do not add our value to our food other than fancy. Examples are colourant, seasoning flavourants, dyes etc. it is very difficult for the cells to metablise and get rid of them. They stay for long periods in the cellular system and cause a lot of havoc. They are the matagens and carcinogens that eventually destroy the cells and kill you by little."
In the lecture titled; "Eat and Die by Little", the professor of Bio-chemistry stated that fasting or and eating once in every 12 hours is the best way to extend long life. Quoting Holy Prophet Mohammed who advised that "do not eat while you are not hungry," and "when you are hungry and you want to eat, divide your stomach into three portions; fill one with food fill one with water; and have third empty so that you can refect deeply."
While recommending the 1-0-1 eating formula, he asserted that taking fast food is the shortest way to "eat and die by little." He added that its avoidance is the beginning of metabolic wisdom". We should eat foods that are as barren of endogenous materials as possible. Like eba, pounded yam, rice, beans, meat etc.

Headline: Nigeria: Tinubu Plans Ethnic Justice Groups
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lagos State Governor, Bola Ahmed Tinubu said that his administration had concluded plans to inaugurate Justice Group in the major ethnic groups in the state.
Tinubu made the disclosure at the weekend through the Secretary to the State Government, Alhaji Aro Lambo, at the 2002 annual Igbo New Yam Festival organised by the Eze Igbo of Lagos State, Eze H. N. Ohazurike, at the State Grammar School, Ifako-Ijaiye, Local Government in the state.
The essence of inaugurating the justice group, Tinubu said, was to explore opportunities of interactions with the ethnic groups to ensure peace, unity and progress, adding that the meeting with the Igbo "today" had been the much needed opportunity he had long nurtured.
He described Ohazurike's address, which centred on peace, justice and equity as timely.
He informed Ndigbo that the suspended breakfast meeting with the governor at the Lagos House, Marina Lagos would soon resume.
He promised that there would be more progress next year in the country and urged all Nigerians to denounce violence at all levels.
The festival witnessed the conferment chieftaincy titles on 12 Igbo ladies and six men in the presence of Chief M.C.K. Ajuluchukwu and Dr Ozuo Okoye, the Traditional Prime Minister of Igboland who represented Ikemba Nnewi, Chief Odimegwu Ojukwu.

Original_Search_Title: Tinubu to Inaugurate Justice Group Soon
Headline: Nigeria: Tinubu to Inaugurate Justice Group Soon
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lagos State Governor, Bola Ahmed Tinubu, said that his government has concluded plans to inaugurate Justice Group among the major ethnic groups in the state.
Tinubu disclosed this over the weekend through the Secretary to the State Government, Alhaji Aro Lambo, who represented the governor at this year's annual Igbo New Yam Festival organised by the Eze Igbo of Lagos State, Eze H. N. Ohazurike, held at the State Grammar School, at Ijaiye area of the state.
The essence of inaugurating the Justice Group, Tinubu said, was to explore opportunities of interactions with the ethnic groups to forestall peace, unity and progress, adding that the opportunity of meeting with the Igbo "today" had been a much needed opportunity he has long been nurtured. He described Eze Ohazurike's address which centred on peace and justice among one another as timely.
He also informed Ndigbo in the state that the suspended breakfast meeting would soon resume with the governor at Marina Lagos.
He promised that there would be more progress come next year in the country and urged all Nigerians to denounce violence at all levels.
The festival was used to confer chieftaincy titles on 12 deserving Igbo women and six deserving men, with Chief M.C.K. Ajuluchukwu and Dr Ozuo Okoye, the traditional prime minister of Igboland, who represented Chief Odimegwu Ojukwu.

Headline: Nigeria: The Comeback King
Body_Text:
Everyone in England is thrilled by the return to form of Arsenal and
Super Eagles striker, Nwankwo Kanu. Except, of course, opposing
defences and goalkeepers.
Arsene Wenger, coach of Arsenal F.C. in the English Premiership, is
having a swell time. So is Nwankwo Kanu, his rangy striker, whose
five goals have contributed in no small way to Arsenal's unbeaten run
in the current season.
Wenger, Kanu and Arsenal have good reasons to feel good. They have
been winning matches and in style. The result is the pole position in
the league and superlative performances on the European circuit.
Kanu is the one hugging the headlines, and justifiably so.
The leggy forward appears to be enjoying a new lease of life at his
London club. Rising from the substitute bench to emerge as one of the
leading goal scorers at Highbury, he has once again defied the odds
against him.
At the beginning of the 2002/2003 season, manager, Arsene Wenger,
wanted to off-load him to any willing club; the English tabloid
labelled him a player collecting $40,000 weekly to sit on the bench.
But, despite having an indifferent year in Arsenal's domination of
the soccer scene last season, Kanu took everything in his stride
saying, "When I want to leave, I'll tell everyone that it's time to
go, but I still want to win the champions league with the team."
Kanu got his chance in the match against Jay Jay Okocha's Bolton
Wanderers where he scored not only his first goal of the season, but
the all important winning goal on away soil.
He followed this feat with a brace, yet another 4-2 away victory
against Leeds United. He was on song for both the first and last
goals.
And just like last week where he featured prominently in Arsenal's
1-0 away victory over Auxerre in the prestigious UEFA Champions
League, the two-time African Footballer of the Year was in action
throughout the match in which French international, Patrick Viera,
drew the last blood.
The gangling striker, who has been consistently linked with a move to
several European clubsides, the most recent being the Kevin
Keegan-tutored Manchester City, over the weekend, was on song twice
under ten minutes in a 3-1 whitewash of shapeless Sunderland.
Perhaps Kanu's strikes against the relegation-threatened side played
a part in the club's management's decision to sack its coach, Peter
Reid.
His goals not only took Arsenal's unbeaten run to 48 consecutive
games, but, his second strike was adjudged as Goal of the Week by the
British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).
According to the BBC, "Kanu's goal was quite simple in its execution,
but the making of the goal, Kanu's fifth in five games, made it the
standout strike of the week." The radio station described it as a
'golden goal' in a special piece entitled 22 Steps to Heaven.
In a volte-face, Arsene Wenger, who had wanted to off-load the
gazelle-limbed Nigerian export, has been pouring encomiums on him for
taking his chance.
"The most important thing is that we have 20 quality players, it
doesn't matter who comes in. He has always shown a great attitude and
I have got a lot of respect for that. It's not been easy for him as
he's got a world-class player fighting for his position."
Kanu's footsteps had always been dodged with career-threatening
issues. In 1996, he was diagnosed as having a faulty valve in his
heart, went through surgery and came back to hit the headlines as one
of Africa's soccer greats.
His misfortune then, he turned into a fortune for other Nigerian kids
suffering heart defects with the establishment of the Kanu Heart
Foundation.
The foundation has catered for scores of Nigerian youths, who have
been flown to Israel for corrective surgery.
Though Kanu is a big hit in Europe, his biggest achievements so far
in Nigeria are his gold medals of Japan '93 U-17 championship and the
gold medal run at the 1996 Olympic Games in Atlanta, before the heart
problem was discovered.
However, Arsenal's 3-1 win over Sunderland at Highbury was the team's
30th consecutive Premiership result without a loss, a record
previously set at 29 by Manchester United.
Now that they have the Premiership record, the Gunners, with Kanu in
tow, are eyeing the all-time top flight run of 42 games held by Brian
Clough's legendary Forest side of the late 70s.
Arsenal's unbeaten record, according to Arsene Wenger, is a pointer
to the fact that the team is consistent and wants to win every game.
"The record we have broken is a big one and we are proud of that. We
gave the right ambition and spirit. We'll try to keep the run going
and do what we want to do," Wenger disclosed.
Interestingly, Dennis Bergkham, whose injury gave Kanu the needed
opportunity to showcase his potentiality after being written off,
might have to sit it out on the sideline until he is fully fit to
break into the team with Kanu's graceful run and record of notching
up five goals in five matches.
The smile is off the face of Austin Otoku, former skipper of the
national soccer team and coach of Union Bank FC of Lagos, as he
wastes away from an attack of stroke and without help from his former
employers. BABS OYETORO met him
The smile is off the face of Austin Otoku, former skipper of the
national soccer team and coach of Union Bank FC of Lagos, as he
wastes away from an attack of stroke and without help from his former
employers. BABS OYETORO met him
y problem started on 6 December 2001. Before this problem began, I
was looking ahead to the new season with a great expectation. It was
this problem that shattered my dreams for that year.
As the season was about to start then, I decided that my boys should
play a pre-season friendly match against a Lagos-based team - the
Athletico F.C. - as a tune-up.
The match started on a high tempo and before we could settle down,
the Athletico boys had scored. We were one goal down.
Not quite long after, a through pass was floated into our vital area.
As my goal keeper went for the ball in the air - all this while I was
on my feet watching the proceedings from the bench - all I saw around
our goal post was sun and a big yam.
The next thing that happened was that the ball sailed into our net
for the second goal. Thereafter, my legs started wobbling. I fell
down and I became unconscious. My players on the field came to my aid
and I was lifted up and taken to the hospital.
I was the only one that saw these objects. The whole thing was like a
spiritual attack directed at only me.
I didn't suspect that anybody was behind this attack. I have always
been a Christian. I don't have to suspect anybody. And I strongly
believe that my God will deliver me.
At the beginning of the problem, I was taken to Kent Hospital in
Keffi, Lagos, where I was on admission for four days. I was later
discharged from that hospital. When my condition did not improve,
Union Bank took me to another hospital - Obalen Aje Hospital,
Obanikoro. I was finally discharged on 19th March, 2002. While I was
in that hospital, my wife signed three different bills given to me by
the hospital management before they were later sent to my employers.
The first bill was for N57,000 and the second bill was N58,000 while
the third bill was for N38,400. At the first hospital we attended in
Keffi, my wife was not given any bill to sign, it was only my former
assistant coach, Banwo (now the substantive coach) that showed the
bill of N40,000 to my wife. Surprisingly, I got to know through one
of the staff that Union Bank had expended the sum of N1m on my
treatment.
Immediately I was discharged from the second hospital, Union Bank
withdrew completely from my treatment. It was through the former
coach that we got to know that my appointment was terminated and that
my salary had been stopped since December last year.
From the period I returned from the hospital, the Union Bank FC
management has deliberately kept me at an arm's length. My wife had
made frantic efforts to reach the management but the security men at
the gate would not allow her. I am sure these people are acting on
instructions.
It is painful to see myself in this condition, having spent some
months in the hospital. I had a relapse because I could not continue
with the treatment, since I don't have the money.
It's my Church, Shepherd Hill Baptist Church, Obanikoro, that has
taken over the responsibility for my medical treatment and even
feeding. They took me to Gbagada General Hospital for treatment. I
was only discharged on 17 June.
My problem with the management, according to the team manager, Mr.
Tunde Popoola, arose from the fact that I informed the Lagos State
Football Association and the Nigeria Football Association of my
predicament. But let me put the record straight. I did not report
Union Bank to any of these bodies. The Lagos State FA officials got
to know about this by chance. And it was through them that the NFA
got to know about it too.
I doubt if there is anything wrong about it if these people or bodies
get to know about my problems. I have served this nation in the past.
Unknown to many people, I captained the Nigerian Olympics football
team to Mexico 68. I was in the team that played a 3-3 draw with
Brazil that year. I was the captain of the national team that played
Ethiopia at the Liberty Stadium, Ibadan. The likes of the late
Oppone, Tony Igwe and Peter Aneike were my team mates. So, if these
people get to know about my predicament, what is the big deal about
that? Should anybody use that against me when I am already dying? I
think what is important now is my survival.
Honestly, I don't deserve what I am getting at the moment, having
served this nation and Union Bank FC faithfully. Look, I brought
Union Bank from the amateur division to the professional cadre. I won
the Lagos State Challenge Cup for them in 2000. I won the Central
Bank Cup for them in 1999.
When I was with the Stationery Stores FC of Lagos, I won the national
Challenge Cup for them in 1950. Ike Shorunmu was my goalkeeper. We
lost the Mandela Cup at the semi-final to Zamalek of Egypt in Lagos.
I have paid my dues as far as football is concerned. But I don't pray
I end my life this way.
I have only two children. The two of them are boys. They are both
graduates but are currently jobless. The small salary their mother is
getting from her place of work is what we're depending on. And this
meagre income cannot sustain all of us.
In fact, I am using this medium to appeal to the Union Bank to look
away from whatever "sin" they claimed I have committed and come to my
aid. I also appeal to well-meaning Nigerians to come to my
assistance. They should not allow me to waste away like this."

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Must Revamp Its Real Sector to Survive - Jamodu
Headline: Nigeria Must Revamp Its Real Sector to Survive - Jamodu
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Minister of Industry, Chief Jamodu has said that for Nigeria to survive economically it imperative that she must revamp her industrial sector which is presently in shatters.
Jamodu raised this point at the National Symposium on Revamping Industrialisation of the Nigerian Economy, organised by Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON) in consonance with the 2002 Nigeria Quality Week and World Standards Day Celebration.
Speaking at the occasion held at the National Art Theatre over the weekend, the minister who was represented by Mr. Damian Agbanelo, Head, Quality Control, Ministry of Industry, maintained that by resurrecting its industrial power Nigeria can absolve the army of the unemployed in her streets.
In his paper entitled: "Power Supply and Industrialisation in Nigeria, Which Way Forward," Engr. J. Mokoju, Managing Director of NEPA, posited that power supply is critical to industrial development and that his organisation is primed to rise to the challenge of improving the power situation to enhance industrial growth.
However, he stressed that the power sector is very capital intensive, arguing that this mandates the urgent need to increase private participation in the sector to ensure success.
According to him, "this will serve two important purposes. First, the government will be able to free up funds for investments in sectors such as health, education, security.
Director-General of Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Alhaji Abubakar Abdullahi in his paper, "The Strategic Race of Raw Materials Development in Industrial Growth, The Nigerian Experience," pointed out that Nigeria is adequately blessed to attain industrial mastery and economic buoyancy but that poor management has been the bane of the country.
For instance, he said despite being the world's largest producer of yam and cassava the nation has never thought to enhance its capacity in these products in order to generate export market and its attendant foreign revenue.
Abdullahi called for government support of processing of local food condiments and fruits such as tomato, mango, orange, saying that this would save the nation million of dollars in foods/fruit that go waste as a result of lack of processing equipment.
He praised efforts by government to save local industries through import restrictions, saying this has helped many industries, although some loopholes such as the porous borders and unwholesome practices need to be properly addressed.

Headline: Nigeria: Jos Market Traders Face Hardship
Body_Text:
Jos — Traders at the Jos ultra modern market gutted by fire about eight months ago have continued to suffer untold hardship.
An investigation carried out by Daily Trust revealed that most traders who usually display their wares in the middle of the road are either stampeded by the heavy traffic flow or have lost most of the their customers who no longer know their location.
They also complained that heavy downpour in the metropolis has constituted another great source of concern to them. This is because they no longer have a place to keep their goods, which are mostly perishable.
The development has also affected the prices of goods in the market as the prices of goods have continued to soar high. A survey revealed that prices of commodities like gari, local rice, potatoes, coco yam etc have sky-rocketed.

Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Thousands Flee Bouake
Body_Text:
Tiebessou — The number of people fleeing the town of Bouake in central Cote d'Ivoire rose from 100 a day on Wednesday to 2,500 on Friday and 5,000 per day on Saturday and Sunday, according to French army officers in Tiebissou, about 100 km south of Bouake. There were
also reports that other people had fled to the nearby town of Didievi.
However, the total number of people displaced in central Cote d'Ivoire since September, when insurgents occupied Bouake and towns farther north, was still unclear. A United Nations inter-agency mission which travelled on Thursday from Abidjan to the capital, Yamoussoukro, and then proceeded to Bouake saw
hundreds of people walking or boarding the few vehicles available in small towns along the way.
Most of the displaced were Ivorians although some 156 people from Burkina Faso sought shelter at the Catholic mission in Tiebissou. They said they had been displaced from their homes in villages around the town.
Sources in towns located between Yamoussoukro and Bouake provided figures on displaced persons they had managed to register, noting that most of the displaced were in transit. "The majority of them stop by for up to three
days and then move on," one such source told IRIN.
In Brobo, about 35 km from Bouake, the French military said some 10,000 people had arrived in the town on Friday. Up to 10 October, the Catholic church in Brobo was assisting 2,000 displaced persons, the parish priest said. Thousands of others sat by the roadside and any available open space as they waited to move on. The number of people passing through Brobo each day between 26 September and 10 October averaged about 4,000 to 5,000, sources said.
M'bahiakro, about 90 km from Bouake, received between 2,000 and 2,500 people on 11 October. Officials at the Catholic mission in the town told IRIN they had assisted some 4,739 people between 28 September and 10 October.
In all the transit points the most pressing needs were food, health care, protection, water and sanitation.
Armed men - who, according to government sources included mutinous soldiers and mercenaries - occupied Bouake after an aborted coup attempt in Abidjan on 19 September. All businesses and offices in Bouake have been closed since then. The market was virtually empty with only a few people selling yams and onions.
"We cannot buy anything because we have no money," a resident told IRIN."Most of us are just thinking of how to leave this place because life is becoming impossible."
The central hospital was operating on skeleton staff and there was no food for the patients. "We were forced to start cooking only for the staff of 80 but we will close the kitchen this evening [Saturday] because we have run out of stock," said a staff member.
Humanitarian agencies were responding by providing medicines and food. The World Food Programme (WFP) said on Sunday it had airlifted a tonne of high-protein biscuits to Yamoussoukro in a bid to bring urgently needed food rations to the displaced. The supplies, WFP said, came from humanitarian stocks in Guinea. It said the biscuits were essential during the first days of displacement to avoid malnutrition among women and children in
particular.
The agency planned to establish a logistics base in Yamoussoukro so as to better respond to the "unfolding crisis in the northern areas of the country".
WFP also said more than 2,000 displaced persons in Yamoussoukro and areas around Bouake had received "urgently needed" food rations and that the International Committee of the Red Cross had begun distributing WFP food aid to vulnerable groups in Bouake itself on Sunday.

Headline: Nigeria: Dialogue On $1.5 Billion Loot
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Did you hear the amount of dollars government is expecting to recover from the Abacha family in the money- for-freedom settlement?" "Men! $1.5 billion cool! I tried inputting that figure into my calculator and the poor machine almost crashed".
"That means your calculator is using acid or kerosene instead of battery?" "Why blame my calculator? Can't you see that the money is huge? Have you attempted converting the funds into Naira? I bet, your computer will surely hang up if you attempt it."
"It looks to me like a fairy tale. How did Abacha steal so much money? This is in spite of the money already recovered by General Abubakar's regime, and the ones yet unknown." "I hear the total amount stolen from the nations coffers is about $4 billion. The $1.5 billion is from the funds traced to bank accounts in Europe minus $100 million settlement for the Abacha family."
"So, that man who was arresting and locking up 419 people and bank directors for several years was himself helping himself with the nation's treasury? What an irony of fate!" "$ 1.5 billion! The Naira equivalent is 187.5 billion at the rate of N125 to a dollar! " " Impossible, N187.5 billion! What was he going to do with all that money?" "If you distribute that money to the 88 million Nigerians, each person will be smiling to the bank with about N2,130."
"Do you know the level of economic activity that money would have generated in Nigeria if it was made available to the common folks? Each person receiving N2,130 will turn our economy into another Japan. That is the fastest way to eradicate poverty in Nigeria forever!" "And also eradicate armed robbery and all the other vices plaguing our country today. In fact, the man's Family Economic Advancement Programme, FEAP, would have been most effective if he had supported the families by sharing part of his loot with them."
"At a minimum annual interest rate of 10%, that N187.5 billion will be generating about N19 billion every year for the family. So even if the Abachas spend N1 million daily for their needs, they will only spend N365 million in a year, which is not even up to half a billion. That means it will take about 51 years to finish the first year's interest earned on the loot. In the meantime, interest is accumulating on the principal for the 51 years at the rate of N19 billion per annum, i.e. N969 billion. "
"But the interest will also earn interest in the usual banking fashion?"
"Yes. It means that the whole generation of Abachas, to the one millionth generation, will not be able to finish the interest earned on their forebear's loot in their life time." So when will they start dealing with the principal amount itself?"
"My brother, only God knows. Can't you see they will never touch the principal amount forever? If the money does not earn any interest at all, which is most unlikely, it will take 514 years of spending N1 million daily for the Abacha generations to finish their great grandfather's N187.5 billion loot."
"So what did he really need that kind of money for?" "It is sheer greed, wickedness and gluttony, nothing else."
"That is what you call it. But I call it Life Family Support Programme (LFSP). He has secured the life of all the unborn Abachas." "And government wants to leave $100 million to the family? I think that is being over magnanimous."
"Ah! You have not even gotten the $1.5 billion and you are talking of being over magnanimous. If $100m will help release $1.5 billion don't you think it is an award - winning negotiated deal?"
"What of the interest the money has earned all these years since 1998 when Abacha senior died? At a conservative rate of 4% per annum interest on $1.5b comes to $60 million. That means for four years the accumulated interest amounts to about $240 million alone, not to talk of the interest on the interest!"
"You are just wasting your time. Stay there and be hallucinating over figures. The man's son said he is not aware of any deal with the Federal Government. He is not even ready to give out part of the life family support his father took five years to plan and execute. And here you are arguing whether he deserves the $ 100 million offer from the government."
"Call it what you like, I still maintain the family is not entitled to it. Let them go and enjoy the interest that accumulated on their loot while Mohammed was in jail. They cannot even finish it in their lifetime and in the life of their unborn generations."
"I hope you are not getting me wrong. I am simply saying that it is a display of good negotiation skill if all it takes for you to obtain a release of $1.5 billion is only $100 million. Look at it from that angle. Negotiation is all about Win: Win. You win and I win, no loser! After all, you should commend the skill of the man who was able to 'obtain' that amount of money from the public coffers in the glare of all regulatory and supervising bodies in the country. You also forget that the Abachas hold the yam and the knife - both the $1.5b and $100 million are still in their clutches."
" I see the point you are making, but I think it is only fair for you to appreciate my own point of view. This money we are talking about was not stolen yesterday. It has been there since 1998 and even beyond. Government only recently managed to identify the overseas accounts where they were stashed away. We have not even discovered all of the loot. I am saying we should collect everything from them and let them live on the over $240 million interest the loot would have yielded over time, and on the other money not yet identified."
"My worry really is the tribal colourations the issue of Mohammed's arrest, release, rearrest and re- release has taken. All of a sudden, the northern politicians see the issue of recovering our money from the Abachas as victimisation. The gang up to ensure his release worries me sick."
"When General Abubakar recovered over $600 million from the Abachas it wasn't the victimisation of a northern son."
"It is terrible!"
"Oh! Now you see my point. The whole thing is assuming a completely different posture. Now they see it as victimising their own son, and not recovering stolen money from a treasury looter. Look at the calibre of personalities that visited Mr. President to demand Mohammed's release. They must have intimidated the President into submission."
"My brother, any other person would have been intimidated. At a point I really wondered whether it was the same issue we were talking about, or may be there were other matters unknown to lesser mortals like us." "I hear Mr. President's travails at the National Assembly and his impeachment saga are not unconnected with the continued detention of Mohammed Abacha. If there is any element of truth in that rumour, then I am afraid there is no hope for Nigeria."
"It's all politics, or rather, politricks. They say in politics there is no permanent enemy but only permanent interests. The permanent interest of the North is control of political power. Every other thing is a means to achieving that end."
"But Mohammed has twice denied striking any deal with the Federal Government to repay $1.5 billion. In fact, after his release he still maintained that his release was unconditional and has nothing to do with the much touted money-for freedom deal." "Is he saying our President is Iying then?" "I don't know who is Iying! Na you talkam o! I don't know what to believe anymore." "If there was an agreement to which all those big men from Kano State signed as witnesses, then let the President publish it in the papers, or let him re-arrest Mohammed. That will give credibility to the government's side of the story. Right now people are beginning to doubt the whole gist."
"I think I agree with you on that point. If nothing is done now, sooner or later the whole thing pales into insignificance and Nigerians will forget about it. And life goes on as usual." "I can bet the photocopy of my degree certificate, if Obasanjo does not recover that money, forget it. It is gone." "Why are you not betting the original copy?"
"Ah! My brother, this is Nigeria. Everything is possible. If I bet the original I will be on my own o!"
"I won't even be surprised if Mohammed Abacha declares to contest the 2003 presidential election. He will rather use the $1.5 billion to buy votes than release it for President Obasanjo's 2002 Budget." "I think you are trivializing this matter. What do you mean?"
"So you didn't hear that ANPP visited the Abacha family recently to woo him into their party? What do you think will be the outcome of those manoeuvers?"
"God forbid bad thing! If Mohammed becomes the President of this country, he will steal all of us alive in broad daylight, not just our money." "Well, you had better prepare yourself for that possibility."

Headline: Nigeria: Brutal Militia Terrorises Nigerians
Body_Text:
Lagos — Onitsha, Nigeria: Even by the standards of this most violent of cities, the killings at a busy junction one recent evening got people shouting.
Armed men waiting at the junction cornered Barnabas and Amaka Blessing Igwe, a couple who were prominent lawyers in this part of Nigeria. They pulled them out of their Mercedes, witnessess said. One of the attackers cut off the wife's left leg with a machete. He hacked at her back so that her body folded. She breathed her last right there.
The men fired into the air to frighten away passers-by, though the witnesses, people living in nearby building, were able to tell the police later what they had seen. The killers pumped bullets into the husband's body. They got into the Mercedes and ran over him. But he was still alive and was taken to a nearby hospital. His brother, Vincent Igwe, arrived in time to see him die.
"He recognised me," Igwe, 45, recalled. "He was in great pain. He said, 'What killed me was government.' I shouted, 'Why is the government killing people?'
"He asked me to pray for him," Igwe continued. "I asked God to save him, but then he gave up."
As chairman of the Nigerian Bar Association for the state of Anambra, Barnabas Igwe, 43, had been one of the fiercest critics of the state government and the governor, Chinwoke Mbadinuju. The lawyers in Onitsha accused the Anambra government of being behind the couple's killing. Mbadinuju denied any inovlvement.
In the year leading up to the attack on the Igwes, Anambra had become the most gravely mismanaged of Nigeria's 36 states. Unpaid teachers were on strike for the full school year, few have gone back to work. All state civil servants and court workers have been on strike for many months.
The state's bar association accused state leaders of pocketing the money meant to pay the striking workers and, just before Barnabas Igwe was killed, demanded that the governor resign within seven days. With elections coming up next year, political killings have been on the rise across this West African country, whose military rulers ceded power to civilians only three years ago. In several states, politicians have supported ethnic militias that have been used to intimidate rivals and, according to rights organisations, have sometimes killed them.
"Our great fear is that politicians who must win at all costs will force their way through by using ethnic militias," Abel Guobadia, chairman of the Indepen-dent National Electoral Commission, said in an interview in the capital, Abuja.
Here in the state of Anambra, one of the country's most notorious militias, known as the Bakassi Boys, was legalised by the state government in August 2000, and the salaries of its members have been paid from public money.
