The Bakassi Boys, whose official name is the Anambra State Vigilante Services and whose members belong to the main ethnic group of this region, the Ibos, have an office in the governor's headquarters in the state capital, Awka.
Onitsha's main market is one of Africa's largest, selling everything from scrap metal and used clothes to bathroom scales and yams. Because of the strike by state workers, the city is littered with mountains of uncollected garbage, some of them sporadically on fire.
Trucks and cars, spewing thick exhaust fumes that sometimes make it impossible to see up a road, try to skirt potholes up to two-thirds of a meter, or two feet, deep. With the gridlock, armies of men make a living by pushing wheelbarrows around congested streets.
The chaos of this huge market has contributed to Onitsha's reputation as one of Nigeria's most violent cities, where criminals and traders fight each other for supremacy on the streets. In the main market on a recent afternoon, a car carrying Bakassi Boys bulldozed its way through the crowds, its siren screaming. Three of the men leaned out the windows, holding shotguns. The militia has been credited with reducing crime in killing criminals and leaving their headless bodies on the streets as warnings. Since they were formed three years ago, they have summarily executed more than 1,000 people in Anambra, according to Amnesty International.
But according to Nigerian and international rights groups, including Human Rights Watch, criminals are not the militia's only targets. The rights group have documented its killing of critics or opponents of the state government.
"I can't say it was the Bakassi Boys who killed the Igwes," said Pete Obiorah, secretary of the state bar association. "But the manner of the killings, which was by matchete cuts, is the same style used by the Bakassi Boys in their own executions." Obiorah also noted that the Igwes had not been robbed in the attack.
The state government denied that the Bakassi Boys had been involved.
"It couldn't have been them," said Victor Ositadinma Ezenwa, secretary to the government and one of the governor's top aides. "The head was not chopped off. Instead, after he was shot, they ran over his body with a car."

Headline: Ghana: Mystery Death
Body_Text:
The mystery surrounding the death of four-year old John Adiko, a pupil, remains unravelled almost a week after his body was found about a mile from Rainbow Motors, near the Spintex road in Accra
. Family sources said Ms. Sekyibea, a cousin of Adiko's father, took the little boy to his school, Learning Tree Academy, on the Spintex road on Wednesday, October 3, this year, and left him to enter when they reached the gates. That was the last time the family saw him alive.
Enquiries revealed that a woman selling yams took the boy away not quite long after Sekyibea had left him in the school.
A former teacher of the boy had seen the yam seller holding the hands of the little boy who was crying and the teacher supposed he was missing home and that the yam seller was giving him assistance.
In fact, on inquiring the teacher was reportedly told by the woman that she wanted to send her children to the same school Adiko was attending and would be glad to be assisted by the teacher.
The teacher said he would make a call first before joining the yam seller and the boy to the Learning Tree. But before she returned from the communication center the woman had bolted with the boy.
Mr. Robert Adiko, John's father, said on the same day around 2:00 p.m. he heard that the boy was lying dead about a mile away from Rainbow Motors.
When Mrs. Adiko, who is six months pregnant, heard the news she fainted out of shock and could not be revived until several minutes later in their Okponglo home, near the Spintex road.
Police Inspector Wilson of the Kpeshie Divisional headquarters, handling the case, said a caller had told them on phone that the body of the boy was floating in a pond and when they rushed there he was actually lying beside the pond.
An autopsy was being carried out on the body, while investigations into the act continued, he assured.

Headline: Nigeria: Strangulation Taken Too Far
Body_Text:
Lagos — Surviving the traumatic 30-month civil war was a terrible ordeal. But living with a deliberate state policy of curtailment is a nightmare. Although the tendency has been to shy away from the fact, the truth remains that Ndigbo are the worse victims of the perpetually marginalizing schemes of the nation's power blocs.
To benefit from federal projects, Ndigbo have had to double their persuasive skills, far and above those of similarly large ethnic groups in the country. Until and unless they take their case to the public domain, their case is as good as lost.
Yet, the impression is given of the Ndigbo as full and equal partakers in the nation's socio-political process. Quite obviously, successive governments have successfully implemented such schemes that ensured that almost 32 years after the civil war, Ndigbo are very much on the fringes of things. Merely canvassing the idea of a President of Nigeria of Igbo extraction is regarded as an affront. Mentioning it practically draws the ire of the typical Igbo haters; especially those who still believe the Ndigbo are yet to serve out the full punishment for their "rebellious act." To this moment, it is evident that all manners of designs are continually plotted to achieve the neutralization of the Igbo political interest. Twice under the present dispensation, unwarranted changes were effected to the Senate leadership. Unfortunately, the wrong reasons were bandied to rationalise the action.
Funnily, there were those who argued that the changes, unjust as they were, were nonetheless facilitated by Ndigbo's love for money. But they simply missed the point. The same people who sold the nonsense and those who imbibed it belong to that specie who always credit Ndigbo with less political astuteness than they clearly deserve.
The truth of the matter is that Ndigbo is constantly on guard and ever so conscious of the designs to relegate them to the nation's political backwaters. So, each time they had consented to finding a replacement for the position of the Senate President, they had done so conscious of the necessity to resist the temptation to fall prey to the grand design of those who are constantly on the look out for the slighted excuse to deny the Southeast that position. It is not a hidden fact that even the present federal government or its agents are sparing no effort to stagnate Igbo areas, socio-economically. This point is beyond debate. Why? Because three and half years into the life of the administration, Ndigbo are still crying cap in hand demanding their fair share of federal projects for the Southeast.
Does anyone require a special probe's findings to believe that a state policy of non-investment in Igboland exists and is being brazenly implemented? Can anyone in government, including the Ojo Maduekwes and Kema Chikwes of this world, who sadly have become government's megaphones on Igbo affairs on account of a small pot of yam portage, proudly beat his or her chest and point to a single federal government project executed since the present Federal Government assumed office.
Renowned novelist, Prof. Chinua Achebe, highlighted this obnoxious policy many years back when he stated, inter alia: "Many have tried but nobody has succeeded in explaining away the citing of five steel mills ...only in the north and west of the country." Taking cue from Prof. Achebe, it is pertinent to also ask why no one has successfully explained the absence of an international airport in the Southeast. Nobody has equally explained the criminal neglect of major federal highways in the southeast.
Ironically, it took the visit of Vice President Abubakar Atiku recently to the Southeast for a belated realisation of the extent of the deplorable state of roads in the zone. Are we then to believe that the Minister of Works and Housing's inspection tour of federal highways on assumption of office was simply a merry-go-round trip? Or doesn't the ministry have field officers who report to Abuja the state of roads around the country?
Only last week, the Imo State Governor, Chief Achike Udenwa, drew attention to awful state of roads in his domain, pointing out that unless urgent actions are taken the state will soon be physically cut off from neighbouring states. It is shocking to contemplate this situation when some states in the country have virtually all their inter-state roads fully rehabilitated, with construction commencing on new ones.
So bad are the roads in the Southeast that any one embarking on a trip to the zone and some parts of the South-south is simply on a suicide mission. It is bad enough that it now takes more than six hours to get to Benin, three hour in excess of the normal travel time, then making a trip to Igbo heartland and states beyond it is almost a journey to eternity. Yet, in a government that prides itself with financial prudence, some one considered it expedient to expend 12billion naira to construct a by-pass that leads to nowhere in particular.
Now, some people are at it again. With barely six months to the general election officials of government are bandying figures on the proposed rehabilitation of roads in the zone. Why now? Why make a political capital of an issue that other zones have massively benefited from, without even a whisper? Is this not another electoral bait? Must Ndigbo be put through this nasty prank again? What happened to the promises made in 1998 and 1999?
Landlocked and highly dependent on road transportation for its inherently commerce-centred economy, the southeast is definitely one zone in the country where the absence of motorable roads is tantamount to strangulation. Yet, for obvious motives, it is the only part of the country saddled with the worst network of roads. Such is the grave deprivation and callous act, which has remained the lot of the Ndigbo.
Consistently advanced as reason for this unbridled deprivation against Ndigbo is what other groups have termed the "Igbo Problem." Proponents of this problem define it as the inability of Ndigbo to speak politically with one voice. But must the Ndigbo speak with one voice to deserve what is duly theirs? Did the Southwest speak with one voice before roads in the zone were massively rehabilitated by the present administration? Or, did the Northeast act in similar fashion before the network of roads in the zone received extensive facelift? Beyond the Shagari era, roads in the Southeast have existed at their own peril.
As is quite obvious, the so-called Igbo problem is clearly, a case of misperception. Short on substance as the claim is, Ndigbo cannot make any apology for their republican values.
As Arthur Nwankwo pointed out "the Igbo culture, being (highly) receptive to change, individualistic and highly competitive," gives "the Igbo man an unquestioned advantage" as well as credentials to pursue both individual or communal advancement over other groups hampered by traditional and feudal hierarchies.
For those to whom Ndigbo have remained a permanent fixation, harping on the perceived Igbo problem is simply a disingenuous way of finding excuses for perpetuating such unthinkable schemes, which we have witnessed over time. Whatever is the problem of the Ndigbo exists within the Nigerian political context. Because as Prof. Achebe espoused on this issue: "Every ethnic group is, of course, something of a problem for Nigeria's easy achievement of cohesive nationhood."
So within this holistic context, Ndigbo are not different. Indeed, more than many other ethnic groups, Ndigbo have worked and pursued with greater vigour the crusade for the unity of the country, relative to the politico-economic gains that have accrued to them there-from. This is pay back time. The dividend of democracy or whatever tag it goes by must not be denied Ndigbo, neither should such goodies be the exclusive preserve of any zone on account of who the President is or where the vice President comes from. Or who the minister of works is.
Ndigbo must put up a bold fight against the recurrent policy of official strangulation against them. It is obnoxious, inhuman and so totally unacceptable.
Iwob writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Pandemonium As Randy Security Personnel Rape Housewife
Headline: Nigeria: Pandemonium As Randy Security Personnel Rape Housewife
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fear gripped residence of Ajegunle, Apapa, Lagos, as the Hausa community in the area took to the street early Friday following sexual assault on two of their daughters and a housewife by three randy soldiers.
The soldiers, who were identified as Corporal Sunday Simeon, Lance Corporal Amos Tafawa Balewa both attached to the Ajegunle Police Station on a joint security patrol and Lance Corporal Christopher Uche, aka Ecomog, 79 NA 14231, who is supposed to be serving at the 72 Air Borne Paramilitary Battalion, Benue State, but was aaleged to be always visible at the station.
They were accused of forcefully having carnal knowledge of a 16 years old virgin, living with her aunt in Ajegunle, and a 26 year old lady who was said to be a yam seller at the Alayabiagba market.
The Hausas, whose first pot of call was the Ajegunle Police Station, also accused the security operatives of extorting N15,000 from one of them, whose name was not given .
Spokesman of the Hausa community, simply known as Hassan, said trouble started when a trailer load of yam arrived at the popular yam Market, on Thursday night, and as usual, "intended buyers rushed to the place to get good yams" .
He said the girls and some other Hausa men were in the place when some soldiers suddenly stopped their vehicles and ordered their arrest.
"We (the men) were later allowed to go, after we had parted with N15,000. It was later in the night that we had that the two girls were driven to a bush, where they were raped by the three soldiers. The most painful aspect of it all is that an innocent girl of sixteen years of age was rudely disvirgined in a horrendous manner", exclaimed the aggrieved Hassan.
But for the quick intervention of the Ajegunle Divisional Officer (DPO), Mr. Felix Vwamhi, (Chief Superintendent of Police), the aggrieved Hausa men would have taken law into their hands, as they vowed to kill any man seen in a military uniform.
The DPO, expressed disappointment over the incident, and promised to get to the root of the case and insisted that the culprits would not go unpunished.
"Just yesterday (Thursday), I called Ecomog and warned him that I don't want to see him again in this station because I have been hearing series of negative report on his activities. Afterall he is not supposed to be here."
Vwamhi stated further that the molested virgin would be taken care of by his office, as deliberate step to pacify the Hausas and also the other lady would be given maximum security physically and psychologically.
Speaking during interrogation, one of accuse, Uche, who also identified by one of the victims, denied being part of the action, as he claimed to be relaxing after intoxication from several shots of locally made gin otherwise known as Ogogoro.
Although, he confessed that he was in the Nigerian Port Authority patrol vehicle when the people were arrested but declined being part of the exercise.
He said he did not know when the girls were raped and got to know of it when he was arrested by the police. He also said he did not know the names of his friends, who brought the vehicle but confessed that they both worked with NPA as a driver and clerk.

Original_Search_Title: Police Uncovers Plan to Disrupt Voters' Registration Exercise
Headline: Nigeria: Police Uncovers Plan to Disrupt Voters' Registration Exercise
Body_Text:
Owerri — IMO State Police Command says it has received intelligence report that some people who are opposed to the on-going review of voters' register, have perfected plans to disrupt the exercise in some parts of the state.
"To actualise this nefarious enterprise, the group has chosen this period of the voters' registration exercise to celebrate new yam festivals, wrestling competitions and compulsory community meetings," Mr. Oko stated.
He described the act as "a deliberate attempt to scuttle a worthy Federal Government programme," as well as to disenfranchise millions of citizens of the state.
The command warned that it would not sit back and watch what it called 'few elements in the state, derail the nation's nascent democracy."
Continuing, the police warned that in view of the foregoing, "it would not tolerate any act capable of causing breach of the peace in the state or such acts that will portray the state as a lawless.
The Police command also promised to "apply minimum force where necessary to disperse any group or group of persons found to be disrupting in any manner, the voters' registration exercise."
The PPRO appealed to all law abiding citizens to go to the registration centres nearest to them, to get registered, and assured that adequate security arrangement have been put in place to ensure a hitch free registration exercise.
"Parents and guardians are hereby warned to advise their children and wards appropriately, to avoid unpleasant consequences, pleas," Mr. Oko stated.
Meanwhile, a gubernatorial aspirant on the platform of All Nigeria People Party (ANPP), Imo State, Chief Mike Ahamba (SAN), has appealed to the citizenry to 'go out and register."
Chief Ahamba made the appeal while addressing members of Saint Mulumba's Catholic Parish, Owerri, after participating in a pontifical song mass on Sunday.
"To register as a voter is a civic right. The peoples' vote is their power. You cannot vote in any person of your choice or vote out those who have failed to deliver to our expectations if we do not register now," Ahamba said.
Speaking also on the voters' registration exercise, during an exclusive interview with Vanguard in Owerri, the state chairman of National Democratic Party (NDP), Mr. Bom Ohiri, appealed to officials of Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to be fair to all the parties.
"If INEC is fair to all the political parties, there won't be any problem but if they decide to be funny, we will resist it," Engr Ohiri said.
He disclosed that his party is interested in this voters' registration exercise, adding that 'anybody who fails to work towards the success of this programme is an enemy of Nigeria and Nigerians."
Although Ohiri did not want to be drawn into giving his views on the content of the police bulletin, he however said that 'they may have their facts which I do I may not comment on it."

Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Self-Inflicted Injury
Body_Text:
Lagos — A BBC correspondent was in 1978 taken aback when a former Nigerian Head of State told him that he would like Aminu Kano to win the 1979 presidential election because of his honesty. The correspondent asked: "Is honesty the sole qualification for leadership in your country?"
Even if a presidential candidate was unintelligent or naive but honest, the former military ruler would have still regarded him as a fit and proper person for the leadership of Africa's largest black nation in a very complex world which was getting more and more sophisticated.
I have in the last few weeks been reflecting on what our elite regard to be the functions of leadership in every society and the appropriate qualifications prescribed for those who want to assume leadership. It is dispiriting to hear otherwise knowledgeable people voice their disappointment with President Olusegun Obasanjo's uninspiring score-card when they expected so much from him given his prison experience and his credentials as a former military ruler, and as a born-again Christian. No-one ever speaks as a requirement for good leadership the critical element which Obafemi Awolowo called mental magnitude, that is, an intelligence far above average, defined by wide reading, good exposure, imaginative thought and a measure of philosophical speculation. Yet, it is a trite fact to acknowledge that it is not possible for any society to make appreciable progress if its leadership is not possessed of mental rigour. Think of John Kennedy, Bill Clinton, Winston Churchill, Lee Kuan Yew, Yoweri Museveni, Mahathir Mohammed, etc, and the monumental changes they unleashed on their societies at critical moments in history.
In his book, This House Has Fallen: Midnight In Nigeria, Karl Mier, the perceptive American journalist, quotes Bamanga Tukur as comparing Olusegan Obasanjo to a truck driver and Alex Ekwueme to a Rolls Royce driver. Still, Tukur spent considerable resources in 1998 and 1999 on Obasanjo's emergence as the presidential candidate of the Peoples Democratic Party over and above Ekwueme. Tukur's reason for giving solid support to Obasanjo, against Ekwueme, according to this book, derived from Tukur's reasoning that Nigeria was in need of a truck driver, rather than his Rolls Royce counterpart as its president. Bamanga Tukur was not alone.
And this is the tragedy of the Nigerian elite. What manner of elite would proudly tell foreign journalists that the leader of his country in the 21st century should be someone comparable to a truck driver, even when a Rolls Royce driver is available and willing to do the job? What do people like Tukur think of leadership and the qualities of someone who should lead Nigeria out of its present development morass?
The late Ken Saro-Wiwa used to be appalled at the tendency of Africans and blacks to abandon critical thinking in favour of cheap, illogical and sometimes superstitious ways out of every problem. In his novel-in-progress, Prisoners of Jebs, Ken argued vigorously that the failure to think creatively and deeply had made blacks be at the bottom of the global heap. Ken was right.
Obasanjo was not mentally prepared for the office of the President when elements like Ibrahim Babangida, acting in concert with top officials of the departing Abdulsalami Abubakar government, resolved to foist him on the Nigerian people. He made no promises, and never offered any insights into his understanding of the Nigerian condition. I was one of the organisers of a scheduled live television debate between him and Olu Falae, joint presidential candidate of the All People's Party and the Alliance for Democracy. Up to less than an hour to the event, we were assured that Obasanjo would participate in the debate at NICON Hilton Hotel in Abuja where he was also lodging. But Obasanjo didn't show up, after all. Falae gave a good account of himself on the live programme compered by James Audu and John Momoh.
After his election as President, Obasanjo was asked the same questions on an NTA live programme as Falae was asked, word for word. His performance was sheer embarrassment. For instance, when asked about what he would do to ensure that Nigeria joined the Information Technology revolution, he went into an anecdotal joke about pounded yam, egusi soup, bush meat, a piece of rock, warts and all.
The tragedy of Obasanjo's ill-preparedness has since been exacerbated by his choice of ministers and the portfolios assigned to them. How can one be enthused at the choice of Sule Lamido as Foreign Minister, Adamu Ciroma as Finance Minister, Sunday Afolabi as Internal Affairs Minister, Pauline Tallen as Minister of State for Science and Technology and Tony Anenih as Minister of Works in today's very complex world?
Obasanjo's preference to work with Evan Enwerem and Anyim Pius Anyim as Senate President was not borne out of a genuine concern for the nation but by a consuming desire to run an imperial presidency , unfettered by a system of checks and balances which a vibrant legislature would cause to be put in place. Though the President does not have a wonderful opinion about the abilities of Anyim and Enwerem, he insisted fiercely on their becoming Senate President because, in his language "there cannot be two captains in one ship". That's why he spared no resources, including the use of about 100 riot policemen at an odd hour, to intimidate and unseat Chuba Okadigbo as Senate President.
Obasanjo's hijack of the national leadership of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party and turning the party lights of the Peoples Democratic Movement (PDM) faction of the PDP was to cause severe problems for the nation. The PDM is a perfect example of a group which knows how to grab power but without purpose. The likes of Works Minister Anenih are not people imbued with any development vision or any kind of redemptive consciousness. All they know is, to borrow the words of Umaru Shinkafi, to put the nation on a perpetual tempo of politicking and electioneering campaign. Hence, the obsession with "re- election", "2003" and "no vacancy" right from the first year of this administration.
Obasanjo last April 26 officially launched his campaign re-election in general polls scheduled to take place in March, 2003, that is, a whole eleven months away. Let us compare this development with what happens elsewhere. President Jacques Chirac of France announced only last February his interest in recontesting in an election coming up two months later, but stated that he would not begin to campaign until April, the very month the first round of election was taking place. Chancellor Schroeder of Germany announced only last June his interest in the election taking place three months later but began campaign on August 6 would take him to 40 cities. In other words, he's campaigning for one month. In the Philippines, candidates campaign for only six weeks.
Taking a cue from the Obasanjo re-election hysteria generated by the PDM, state governors have adopted all manner of tactics to return to power by all means possible, including doing such grotesque and bizarre this, as their adoption in the second year of their four-year-tenure by their parties as gubernational candidates. Yet, there are clear party guidelines about how such candidates can emerge during special conventions.
No-one should be surprised at the deplorable state of federal roads or the parlous condition of our economy. After all, Anenih and Ciroma, arrow heads of the Obasanjo campaign organisation, are now more or less part-time Works and Finance Ministers respectively. Does Obasanjo need be told that when President George Bush, Sr., chose Secretary of State Jim Baker to co-ordinate his re-election campaign, Baker quit the government?
It is curious that despite PDP's overwhelming majority in the National Assembly, Obasanjo has been in a gridlock with the law-makers. This is because of lack of party cohesion, party discipline and party solidarity, a situation Obasanjo created more than any person. Didn't he very early in the life of the administration get APP and AD senators as well as renegade PDP senators to ensure the triumph of Enwerem, over Okadigbo, the official candidate of his own party, a party he himself leads by virtue of his position? And to think that Enwerem was an APP member a few months earlier, but also one of the so-called two million-man marchers for Sani Abacha's perpetual presidency!
As though to add insult to injury, Obasanjo, Abubakar, Anyim and Anenih decided that Okwesilieze Nwodo should make way for Vincent Ogbulafor as the PDP National Secretary. None of the stakeholders in the South-east zone of the PDP was consulted, as Ogbulafor, an APP member until two weeks earlier, was imposed on the party. By his new position, Ogbulafor became the custodian of whatever values the PDP has, a position Pope John Paul 11 spinted determination of the faith". No step was considered indecent in the to stop Nwodo and others from contesting at the November 2001 convention.
Obasanjo and his henchmen did succeed in having mostly pipsqueaks in the PDP leadership. But most of them are not popular with the party membership, nor are they competent. As the PDP leadership tries to rein in its National Assembly members who have engaged the president in a fight to a finish Audu Ogbeh of all the members of the national working committee has been inspiring a measure of confidence. But a tree cannot make a forest.
Obasanjo has paralysed the PDP. The same way he has paralysed governance. His is a self-inflicted tragedy. Too bad.
Adinaba is head of the West African Office Discovery International Communicators.

Headline: Kenya: Options for the Rainbow Alliance
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With Musalia Mudavadi having decamped from the Rainbow Alliance, one thing is certain even in the uncertain and constantly shifting waters of Moi Succession politics: the other key members of the alliance will have to re-evaluate their political positions and options.
Most political analysts expect Tourism and Information Minister Stephen Kalonzo Musyoka to follow Mudavadi's example and ditch the alliance any time soon.
In fact, conventional wisdom had it that Musyoka would be first of the Rainbow quartet to buckle when push came to shove. But as it happened, Musyoka has surprisingly so far managed to hold his own to the extent that he even parried off direct pressure from President Moi to ditch the alliance about a fortnight ago.
But just how long Musyoka will last now that Mudavadi has caved in to pressure remains the big question. Nevertheless, to many the question of Musyoka's going back to the main fold is not one of "if" but rather "when."
Whereas for Mudavadi, the Transport and Communications Minister, it may not have been very difficult eating the humble pie and going back to Mama na baba singing mea culpa, mea culpa, for other key, nay, principle players in the Rainbow Alliance-former Vice-President George Saitoti and Kanu Secretary General Raila Odinga- things may not be that simple.
As things stand, for Saitoti and Raila, chances are that they have gone beyond the point of no return. And as such, for them backtracking now from the anti-Project Uhuru path that they have chartered in the last couple of weeks may not be a very viable political option.
In fact, talking to sources close to the two politicians it emerges quite clearly that, at least for now, the two have adopted as their political maxim "forward ever, backwards never."
But on the other hand, the chief proponent of Project Uhuru and the man holding the yam and the knife and is still wielding the big stick, President Moi, has taken as his political mantra the words "its all or nothing" as far as Project Uhuru goes.
As such, with both Saitoti and Raila being experienced and streetwise politicians, it is obviously not lost to them that continuing to pursue their anti-Uhuru political agenda from within the precincts of Kanu will be the political equivalent of hitting their heads against a brick wall and hoping to make a pathway. Remember Mathew Adams Karauri's words?
But for some reason, it seems like although the only viable option open to both Raila and Saitoti is to cut loose and ditch Kanu, the two appear determined to tough it out until Kasarani II.
According to sources close to the Rainbow Alliance duo, the former V-P and the Kanu S-G are at the moment "not even considering ditching Kanu as a political option."
Their argument for sticking with the ruling party until Kasarani II is born out of the perception that by doing so they may be able to instil "an element of fair play" in the presidential nominations.
But this is an argument that is not necessarily based on sound political judgement. Given the amount of political carnage that the Kanu chairman has brought to bear on anyone who stands on the way of Project Uhuru, it would be politically naive for any politician to go to Kasarani II with the expectation of pushing an act outside the pre-determined script.
Political prudence dictates that any serious politician in Kanu who does not subscribe to Project Uhuru should either re-think his position and come to the realisation that "national unity is paramount to individual interests" as many are wont to claim when push comes to shove, or simply take a walk and fight from outside.
Mudavadi has taken the former route and Musyoka may soon follow suit. As for Saitoti and Raila, it may as well be the time to call it a day in Kanu.
From the firing of his vice-president last Friday, to the deals that got Njenga Karume ditching Mwai Kibaki last Sunday and onto the behind the scenes manoeuvres that persuaded Mudavadi to decamp from the Rainbow Alliance yesterday, it is becoming increasingly clear that to President Moi, no price is too high, no road is too long and no political manoeuvres will be too complicated when it comes to getting Uhuru in the State House.

Headline: Nigeria: Wem 2002: Lest We Forget
Body_Text:
Lagos — The World Education Market (WEM) 2002, which took place in Lisbon, Portugal, has come and gone. But with the recent announcement by the organisers, Reed Midem Organisation, Paris, that companies are already booking their stands for next year's edition and knowing that the event will still hold in Portugal, Bukola Olatunji, writes that the country should be prepared to do better than it did this year.
Twice in one day, Director of the World Education Market (WEM), Ms. Elaine Legault told "a moving story of a Nigerian journalist, Rotimi (Oyekanmi) who is not here."
First at the opening press conference of WEM 2002 held at the Lisboa (Portuguese word for Lisbon) Conference Centre, Lisbon, Portugal on Tuesday, May 22 and in her remark at the 'Spotlight on Nigeria' later that day.
Mr. Oyekanmi, who had gone to cover WEM 2001held in Canada, had walked up to her and observed that only three people represented Nigeria. These were Registrar of the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board JAMB), Prof. Bello Salim, Deputy Director, Research and Planning at the National Broadcasting Commission, Lagos, Dr. Ronke Ogunmakin, as well as former Minister of Education and Deputy Vice Chancellor, Academics of the University of Benin (UNIBEN), Prof. Abraham Imogie.
The fourth Nigerian was Prof. Felicia Oyekanmi (no relation of the journalist), formerly of the University of Lagos (UNILAG), now Senior Programme Officer, Council for the Development of Social Science Research in Africa (CODESRIA), Senegal.
But she was there to discuss how advancing the policy and practice of capacity building in international co-operation has enhanced the achievement of educational goals in emerging economies (a panel session on capacity building), with Senegal as a case study.
This attendance was however, an improvement on the maiden edition (WEM 2000) also held at the Vancouver Convention and Exhibition Centre, British Columbia, Canada. Only Dr. Ogunmakin, who is now a member of the WEM advisory board, was there.
In an interview with THISDAY, Dr. Ogunmakin, an instructional technologist, said she was in California in 1998, doing some networking for her sister who was seeking political office in the United States, when WEM was being conceived. She heard about it through some investors, had discussions with them, and later got invited to the maiden edition.
Upon her return, she organised a workshop in June 2000, for stakeholders within the university. Minister of Information, Prof. Jerry Gana and Prof. Imogie attended the workshop held in Lagos.
Participants were able to look at distance learning, using the mass media to propagate it in Nigerian universities. One of resolutions made was that there should be a network station, which the minister announced, and the Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) was chosen to carry educational materials.
So Oyekanmi told Ms. Legault last year how impressed he was by the quality of the market and promised to go back home and do all he could to see that Nigeria had a bigger representation at this year's edition.
The journalist kept his word. Legault met with the Minister of Education, Prof. Babalola Borishade, along with Oyekanmi and this writer, during last year's 31st General Session of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO) in Paris.
There the foundation for Nigeria's participation at this year's WEM was laid. The rest, as they say, is history. The Federal Ministry of Education approached the Education Tax Fund and the latter's huge financial support ensured that Nigeria had a good showing.
But when WEM 2002 opened its doors on May 21, Rotimi Oyekanmi, and many others who had worked hard to be there, were conspicuously missing. Many people, including Oyekanmi were denied visa by the Portuguese government. Only the Embassy can tell the number. They cut across different parastatals in the Federal and State Ministries of Education.
Executive Secretary of the National Universities Commission (NUC), Prof. Peter Okebukola, who represented the head of the Nigerian delegation and Minister of State for Education, Alhaji Bello Usman, said in an interview that the Abubakar Tafawa Balewa University (ATBU), Bauchi was supposed to be at the show.
According to him, "the institution is developing some very interesting solar-power-driven devices including solar powered computer technology equipment. We wanted to show them (the West) where we are so that we can seek partners from more developed countries to help improve on this prototype." But that did not happen.
To get to that point, the ministry had set up a technical committee chaired by Okebukola, to organise what he called "a mini WEM" at the NUC premises in Abuja, weeks before the event. There every potential participant displayed and demonstrated what they planned to take to Lisbon. For five hours, the Minister of Education, Prof. Babalola Borishade went round the stands. Some were approved, some asked to improve on what they had, and some others knocked off. Those asked to improve on what they had also had to show what improvement they had made.
After all these, some were still denied entry visa. Visa denial is not a big deal and it happens all the time. But there has to be a reason for it. In this case, none was given at all. Many of the passports, like Oyekanmi's were not virgins. They had several other visas in them.
After delegates began to arrive and the organisers got wind of what was going on, WEM officials entered into constant dialogue with the Mayor of Lisbon, the Nigerian Embassy in Portugal and and the Portuguese Embassy in Nigeria. All explanations and pleas fell on deaf ears. One parastatal, Agency for Mass Education had no representative at all.
In the first place, it was as if the Portuguese embassy in Nigeria here did not know about WEM or if it did, never knew how much interest Nigeria had in it. It was completely overwhelmed by the number of applicants. About 80 participants, including officials of State Ministries of Education, and the private sector (schools and computer companies) were able to attend.
But how much better did some of those who got the (15 -day) visa fare? Perhaps one should quickly mention the case of the proverbial outsiders that wept louder than the bereaved - immigration officials at the Schipol Airport, Amsterdam. The only reason we (some officials of the Pulse Marketing Communications or Nigerian Education Exchange, official organisers of the event; Mr. Laolu Harolds and this writer) found ourselves at that airport was because we travelled via KLM (Royal Dutch Airlines). All we wanted was to board the next available plane to Lisbon, which was due in about four hours. But the immigration officials would not let us go, because we did not have proof of our hotel bookings (whereas 120 rooms had been booked and paid for at the prestigious Hotel Estoril Sol) for all participants. Our explanation that we were part of that group fell on deaf ears for a while. Meanwhile, THISDAY and The Guardian had a second booking at Hotel Real Parque, courtesy of WEM.
Eventually this explanation was taken; we then needed to prove that we could feed ourselves for one week! Would they not leave the immigration officials in Lisbon to worry about this? "No way, all Shengen states have agreed to do this" we were told. If ours was like the Paris - bound flight that took off less than an hour after our arrival, and a family friend who was on the same flight with us from Nigeria took it, we would have missed it.
It took one reasonable immigration official to remind his colleagues that quite a number of people from all over the world were heading in the same direction, so they should allow us to go.
Even the former goalkeeper of Super Eagles, Peter Rufai, who had a foreign passport and was on the same flight with us, was not spared. He was surveyed all over by the official and then his passport very slowly scrutinised with a hand lens or magnifying glass. Thank goodness, we eventually got to Lisbon.
In his opening remarks, Chief Executive of Reed Midem Organisation, Mr. Xavier Roy, said that WEM remains faithful to its primary objective: To provide an unparalleled opportunity for business, for networking, learning and building partnerships across sectors and on a global stage.
WEM - where content, technology and expertise converge to shape the future of learning.
Ms. Legault also said in an interview with THISDAY that WEM came about out of the need that was expressed to Reed Midem "for an international platform that would bring the best solutions, practices and resources from around the world to one location so that those who are looking for these resources and solutions could be adapted to their own circumstances".
According to her, "this happens because we are facing a tremendous rise in demand. We have identified with two key associations and organisations like UNESCO, the need to mobilise our forces to accomplish the goals of education for all and WEM is one tool that allows us to get closer to bridging the gap in terms of access to learning opportunities." The host country has to understand that. Nigeria, like other participants, wants to get the full benefit of its investment.
The first and second WEM held in Canada, Legault said, because her organisation wanted the event to be very international from the beginning "and Canada has many links around the world to the commonwealth and to the Francophony. It is one of the countries that have a great deal of experience in education whether through broadcasting or distance learning. It is the heart of the North American continent, which is the most advanced in terms of their educational systems. It has proximity to Asia, and Vancouver is an attractive city that has the kind of infrastructure that we needed for this kind of event so we succeeded in that goal in that we had over 60 countries present both in 2000 and 2001. So when WEM became international, it became easier to move it around and that is what brought us to Lisbon."
It is also interesting to note that WEM had hosting requests from Australia, Asia, Africa and all over Europe. WEM went from 62 countries in Vancouver last year to 71 this year. Legault said, "obviously, our being in Portugal would enable many countries that were not able to attend, to come." And that explains why the market is going there again next year.
In choosing a location, Reed Midem, she said, considers "what our participants want, where they think is convenient and works for them. When I say participants, I mean those who have come as exhibitors and those who have only come to walk in and see. Then we look at very concrete things-the conference facilities, technology infrastructure, international travel access, conviviality of the location, hotels, it is a total experience." The host country has to think very deeply about this.
A journey that should have been real fun left a sour taste in the mouth. That one is reporting this instead of a travelogue is a clear indication of what it was. Although there was hardly any time to explore one of the oldest, yet beautiful cities in Europe. There was not even enough time to cover all the conference programmes. One had to make the sometimes-painful choice of where to be per time.
The opening cocktail that held at the ancient St. George V's Castle for example gave a breathtaking view of the city. Nice people (I believe), beautiful weather, very much like Nigeria's (and both countries are on the same time zone), a long stretch of beach, relatively cheap things to buy, but with little time.
One should not forget to mention that being one of the poorest countries in Europe has not stopped Portugal from maintaining clean streets and an efficient metro system, though wages are largely poor.
How can one forget the only Nigerian eatery in Lisbon, and perhaps, Portugal - Wazobia Kitchen (Restaurant and cafe bar) on Avenida Almirante Reis? The owner, an Igbo man simply called Jones, once played football in Nigeria. He and his wife (and their cute six - month old baby boy, Junior) had lots of pounded-yam, ogbono or egusi soup, as well as goat meat pepper soup, among others to serve several members of the Nigerian delegation that flooded the restaurant every day of the conference. The couple also ran a store where one could get smoked fish, stockfish, cray fish, raw egusi, ogbono. etc. With only about 200 legally resident Nigerians in the entire Portugal, it is easy to understand why Jones' Wazobia is one of a kind. Not cashing in on the monopoly, Jones tried his best to make his customers from home feel at home.
Of course there was the bad tempered driver of one of the two buses hired to ferry delegates through out the fair. The old man could not understand why delegates could not keep to time scheduled for boarding or getting off the bus.
Needless to say, the non-issuance of entry visa to some well deserving people generated a lot of bad blood.
But was the market worth all the trouble? Okebukola said Nigeria was "able to show to the world that development in education in our country is still impressive despite the problems and obstacles along the path to development. We are happy that the world has been able to appreciate what is going on in Nigeria"
The country was also able to forge partnerships with other countries, notably Canada, France, and Germany for developing the educational sector, especially in the area of e learning.
E-learning, he said, is at the heart of educational development in the world. "It is a relatively cheap way of delivering education primary, secondary, and higher."
But how far can e learning go in Nigeria with erratic power supply, inadequate telephone service and so on? The professor of science education said "obstacles to e learning in Nigeria are plenty, but I think we should not look at the obstacles as a way of not making us to begin. I think we should step gallantly on the path of e learning and ensure that within the next five years, we have structures on ground that can enable us beat our chest in another World Education Market about the progress that we have made as a result of our participation in the 2002 edition." That indeed is the crux of the matter.
According to him, "there are always ways round problems. Talk about NEPA, there are solar-powered computers and computer technology material that we can use to deliver e learning. So I would not see the problems of electricity to be one that we cannot surmount. My suggestion here is that we should look for solutions, and these are available by several providers, that can allow us to use solar powered panels to drive the computer technology for delivering e learning."
Okebukola added that "the idea again, is not for you to go national for a start, but to run a pilot project and build up. We have been able to see from so many countries that are represented at WEM that they started with just a handful of schools at the primary levels, secondary and higher. The message for me is that " do not wait until everything is in place" -that NEPA is running 100 per cent, connectivity all over the country, that computers are there before you begin to move."
The Executive Secretary of National Universities Commission (NUC) said the commission is going to organise a national seminar on e learning in higher education. "We want to brainstorm and see. We must begin somewhere and begin quick because every moment we are marking time, the world is moving several paces forward. So no matter how small the level of our movement, we want to move. We want to see how at the Colleges of Education, Polytech-nics and Universities levels, we can introduce some element of e learning albeit on a pilot scale, maybe in the large cities where electricity is not a problem. The University of Lagos is very hot in championing that, so may be we when we meet nationally, we will be to see how we can proceed."
For the next WEM, he said, Nigeria is going to improve on "where we are in terms of technology in delivery. Only the universities were able to put up some bit of technology having to do with our digital library project." Rating Nigeria's overall participation however, Okebukola said "the minister of Education of Zimbabwe and the representative of Cote d' Ivoire said Nigeria has done Africa proud. That in fact, the Nigerian stand is all Africa."

Original_Search_Title: Learning From Sydney, Korea And Manchester
Headline: Nigeria: Learning From Sydney, Korea And Manchester
Body_Text:
Television has introduced new pressures on whole publics in countries
which witness the rituals and organisational techniques used by those who
are hosting international sports fiestas. Each spectacle becomes a challenge
or rebuke to others.
The Australians used the Sydney 2000 Olympic Games to tell the visual
history of their ethnological growth and highest achievement to date, as
well as their unfinished search for racial integration. The Koreans used the
construction of stadiums for the 2002 World Cup to integrate their
ecological sensibilities into their architectural designs; with the swans'
wings in flight being one of the designs much emphasised.
Their most visual message to television viewers was their communal
spirit which was spectacularly expressed in whole peoples in stadia wearing
the same red dresses. Their unspoken national income was in their team doing
better than Japan; a much resented, if not hated, former colonial power. Out
of Manchester (or "Madchester") 2002 Commonwealth Games came England's
ingenuity in exploring the frontiers of light as a structural element in
architecture; a celebration of their achievements in the racial
incorporation of her colonial grandchildren; and a rare explosive
appreciation of talent and excellence by athletes other than their own
except when beaten by the St. Kitts and Nevice in 100 metres.
They could not be blamed for a Queen who made them momentarily miss that
human electricity in Princess Diana, particularly at that moment in the
beginning of the games when the little girl with severe heart problem
brought the Jubilee baton containing the Beckam provided sensitive
support. The Queen might have done a lot for the spirit of her people and
the world if she had touched Kristy Howard, that brave little light of
England. That royal chill was a lesson of sorts.
The central message that runs through the three arenas of collective
sporting is the integration of a people's productive genius and spirit, as
well as what economists call "the multiplier effect", into the event. In
Australia you can see that every weaver of every type of ancient costumes
and tools had earned money from the thousands of units needed; painters,
musicians, choreographers, architects, engineers, industries had made money
from meeting the demands of the opening ceremony along.
In Korea and Manchester the organisers had made orders for shirts,
flags, balloons. Manchester added even brooms and paint and a canvas in
their closing ceremony. Each event pushed fireworks by computer designing to
new mountaintops.
Nigeria will be hosting the 2003 All Africa Games. The government has
probably made one false start already by building an "enclave" and not a
national stadium in Abuja.
The spirit of the nation's ancestors and heroes past might have been
better pleased with a stadium built by the brawn of Nigerians and not German
and Chinese technology power. The military corps of engineers working with
hundreds of thousands of unemployed youth and workers draw from a "federal
character" pool might have taken longer, but as the Egyptians say: go slowly
to get there faster. Instead, Nigeria will "showcase" a Julius Berger-China
made enclave stadium, and pass it off as a national achievement.
This is a far cry from the achievements of Australia, Korea and
Manchester. Apart from the malnutrition which the people's history and
spirit are being subjected to, the billions of naira (now speculated to rise
beyond to 90 billion) will not touch Nigeria's food producers; textile
weavers (if the workers were required to wear work clothes woven in their
home areas); fabricators of wheelbarrows and buckets for carrying soil and
concrete, brewers of burukutu and palm wine, pepper-soup, amala, pounded yam
and miyan kuka, kilishi and suya sellers etc., etc.
The 'multiplier effect" factor will not propel the local construction
engineering and manufacturing sector. The Australians told a narrative which
avoided this economic cardinal sin.
The enclave structures fall apart from neglect and pilfering after their
construction. Blame is put on "lack of maintenance culture". It is like
asking a stepmother to care for an orphan the same way a natural mother
would. The economic umbilical chord must enter Nigeria's sports
planning.

Headline: Nigeria: Maiduguri Faces One-Week Blackout
Body_Text:
Maiduguri — Government, business and domestic activities in Maiduguri are gradually grinding to a halt as electricity supply will be cut off for not less than one week: Already the city is in its 3rd day, blackout.
According to Maiduguri, NEPA district manager, Mr. Johnson Bola Adeyemi one of the three high tension policies used to divert power from the three pulled-down towers got broken and caught fire over the weekend.
Maiduguri metropolis and environs were earlier subjected to a four days blackout last month. Adeyemi explained that the power outage which began in July 2 was caused by a powerful windstorm which pulled down the high tension towers on the Maiduguri-Gombe 132KV line along Maiduguri-Damboa road, about 70km from the city.
He further explained that the Authority's engineers subsequently erected three temporary wooden poles to divert electricity from the broken steel towers. The option was taken as a permanent solution would have cut off the city and three towns from power supply for upwards of four weeks. It is one of the three women poles which fell apart and caught fire.
Adeyemi blamed windstorms for the incident. "The wind-storm has caused a lot of damages to our system by pulling down three-high tension towers and burnt one wooden pole which was used to supply electricity to the metropolis and its environs," he said.
He also said that contract for its re-construction has been awarded on the FG. According to him, best krypton, the contractors handling the project has mobilised material and men to site and assured that electricity supply will be restored immediately the pole is replaced a process that will take not less than five days.
One of the effects of the power outage is hiking of the price of water by its vendors, popularly called "Yam Moya" in the local parlance. Already a 25-litres jerry can of water has jumped from the old price of N5 - N10 to N25.
Also affected is the world bank assisted water scheme (WBAS) located near the University of Maiduguri which has ceased to function as there are no funds to buy diesel to power the standby generators.
Small businesses like hair-dressing saloons, business centres and barber-shops would also witness a down-turn in business activities owing to the power outage and high cost of buying fuel for their generators.
"However, our belief is that before this week runs out, the government, whatever the case may be, will release all our salary arrear."
THISDAY investigation however revealed that from all indication, the judicial crisis in the state is far from been resolved as the workers expect more than was paid.
Consequently, cases awaiting hearings in courts and the acquisition of legal documents through the courts for various reasons might still remain unrealistic dreams for the people of the state, should the workers decide to resume their strike action again for nonpayment of their salary arrears.

Headline: Nigeria: Adieu, Jolly Good Fellow
Body_Text:
Lagos — There was no foreboding of danger on the fateful day. Three friends, united by the journalism profession over the years, took time off their busy schedule to unwind. The triumviate had been reporting crime for their respective newspapers for a number of years. In a profession that is no respecter of weekends or public holidays, they decided to make the most of the little time on their hands, after a hectic Saturday work. En-route the fun cum-professional ride, from the blues, danger knocked. When the dusts raised by the unfortunate fatal auto crash cleared, one layed dead and the two others hospitalised following injuries received in the accident.
Succinctly put, this is the story of how Sisaa Agbo of The Post Express and Emma Nnadozie of Vanguard, and the late Ben Akparanta of The Guardian met their fate penultimate Saturday on the Third Mainland Bridge in Lagos.
Speaking to THISDAY shortly after his discharge from the hospital, Agbo, still wearing stitches on his head, said the trio had no premonition that danger laid ahead on the day in question. He said Ben and Emma had called him from a location in Ikeja and invited him over for lunch. Due to certain pressing matters in his own newsroom however, he told them on phone to go ahead with the lunch, but they agreed that they should meet him at The Post Express in Apapa for a certain assignment on the Lagos Island.
The duo came to his office as promised after a trip to Bariga and waited on him to tidy up before they moved over to Lagos in Ben's car. With their Lagos assignment over, Agbo led his two other friends to a joint at Bonny Camp where they sell his Tiv delicacy of pounded yam.
He recounted what happened shortly before they embarked on the ill-fated trip: "After my meal, Ben's brother, Richy, a staff of Hallmark Bank, called him to say he was waiting for Ben at Ojota at a place called Ngozi Hot Spot. Ben told him he was with Emma and me and the guy asked him to come with us because we all know one another. He said since we haven't seen for a long time, we should come for us to chat over a few bottles of beer. One of my naval officer friends wanted us to stay back and have drinks with him, but I said no, this man who called us now is our friend whom we had not seen for a long time. We then left Bonny Camp at about 8.00pm".
He said that when they hit the road, he sat on the passenger seat in front while Emma, who sat at the back tried to caution Ben to take it easy since they were no longer under deadline to keep the appointment. Agbo said all of them laughed and Ben who used to drive fast, slowed down considerably to the extent that they even poked jokes at him again why he was so slow. When they had advanced considerably on the bridge between 80 and 90 kph, he saidthey noticed a luxury bus in front of them, which Ben tried to overtake. With the poor visibility, he suddenly noticed there was a broken truck ahead of him and because the bus was by the side, he tried to apply the brakes and the next moment, the car somersaulted.
After it came to a halt, Agbo said Ben was the first to come out followed by Emma while he was trapped inside the car until eyewitnesses came to his rescue by pulling him out. "Ben and Emma were shouting, 'we are journalists, please help' and passers-by stopped to render help. People were now making calls to the police and the ambulance services notifying them of the accident", he said.
He stated that with blood streaming down their bodies, a good Nigerian among the people offered to take them to the Gbagada General Hospital, but the road network was so bad they could not make it there. They were taken to Folabi Medical Centre at Oworonsoki, where he said the doctor was very co-operative. After a check of their heartbeats, he concluded that Ben was the most critical and should be taken to the Lagos University Teaching Hospital (LUTH), while the two of them remained there. He said the same good Nigerian took Ben to LUTH.
At that point he said Richy called to find out why they hadn't arrived. When he broke the news of the accident to him, he said Richy told him he would go over to LUTH to see Ben immediately. But a distraught Richy called back to say he had combed everywhere in LUTH without the trace of Ben. The brother headed to the Ikeja General Hospital once more without luck.
By 11.30pm, the man who took Ben to LUTH came back to say he was eventually taken to the Gbagada General Hospital and probably because of their state, he did not want to reveal to them that Ben had passed on. He said the man could not go home that night because he was heard saying they had locked all the gates leading out and that he was going to sleep at the reception. "I asked him to leave his address for us but he never did when I asked the next day. So I don't even know the name of this good Nigerian", he said of the man who rescued them from the claws of death.
At six the next morning, he said Richy called to say he could not locate Ben whereupon he told him to go to Gbagada where he was eventually taken. Thereafter, Agbo made another frantic call to Ben's wife correcting the earlier information that her husband was in LUTH. Moments later, Richy called and in a very sober voice, said contrary to the information earlier that Ben was responding to treatment, that he was brought in there dead. Both of them were obviously shattered by the news on their hospital beds. They quickly made contact with Ben's wife again and as a decoy, told her to stay back and prepare something so that Richy and herself could take to Gbagada as a decoy.
A complete gentleman and a detribalised Nigerian in the true sense of the word, the late Ben, THISDAY learnt had always been a very careful driver who never allowed any fault to manifest in his car. He said Ben, who hailed from River State was married to a lady from Cross River State. When his wife had their first child about five months ago, he christened him Gbolahan. To him, he married while in Lagos and his fortunes changed dramatically. Therefore, Gbolahan, which means wealth in Yoruba, brought him wealth. A simple man by all standards, Agbo described the departed journalist as one who could hardly hurt a fly. "If you had any disagreement with Ben and you try to behave funny the next time you meet him, he would smile and walk up to you to shake your hands. Thereafter, he would plead with you to forget whatever transpired the previous day", Agbo added.
He described the parlous state of affairs in the country, which have brought this sorrow of losing Ben. "If the street lights were working, Ben wouldn't have been dead today. The road was so dark, you could not see far enough", he said. He also cited the attitude of the LUTH medical staff, whose non-challant attitude necessitated going back to Gbagada that night. On the night of the accident, there was no ambulance on sight on the Third Mainland Bridge to take them to the hospital despite of the claims of government officials of a 24-hour service on the long stretch of the bridge.
Also speaking with THISDAY from his hospital bed, head crime beat of Vanguard, Nnadozie, the third occupant of the car said: "We were to meet our colleague, Sisaa Agbo of Post Express. We all had an appointment, but before we headed for the Apapa office of The Post Express, I left with Ben to see somebody on Toyin Street, we used his red starlet tudox. By the time we left Toyin Street, we remembered we had an interview appointment with the Oba-elect of Bariga, Oba Timson, we raced to Bariga through Ogudu.
"On the way Nnadozie saw an obituary poster of a police officer and called the name out: 'Adegbite'. Akparanta applied his brakes, rushed down from the car and went to see the poster to confirm the name, then he rushed to the house which was close to the place and banged at the door to condole with the family. He enquired about the burial, he was told Adegbite had been buried about three days earlier and he said: 'Oh God, what is all this".
When they came out and saw the poster again, the late journalist was said to have lamented the death of the police officer, whom he called his friend. He did that for a long time. Nnadozie explained that it took them about an hour to get to the Oba's house due to a traffic jam, but unfortunately they did not meet the king at home.
Asked whether they used seat belts while they drove around town that day, Nnadozie replied: "Nobody thought of that. Before we entered the Third Mainland bridge, I remember I told my friends, gentlemen, let's take things easy . There is always this rush on the part of motorists, especially at that hour because of the fear of bandits. The place was not lit at all as it was in total darkness. We had the option of plunging into the river, into the lorry or allowing the car to be carried by the double decker bus beside us. I just heard the crash and that was all".
Nnadozie said he regained consciousness immediately: "I thought I was using my hand to break the glass, I did not know I used my arm. The car had turned sideways, I brought out my head, sympathizers came in and got me out of the vehicle. Then, I can't say what actually happened, it was as if Ben was crawling out of the vehicle, but Sisaa was trapped.
"I don't know how I managed to come out with my handset. While we were still looking for someone to convey us to the hospital my phone rang, it was my editor, Gbenga. I said Oga, I'm dying, he said 'Emma where are you, what happened?' I said I don't know where we were, somebody out of the crowd was explaining where we were". The sympathiser who took them to the hospital simply said he hails from Enugu State without mentioning his name.
Nnadozie, who had a broken hand and collar, is responding to treatment. He said a thorough x-ray had been carried out on him and no internal injury was found.
"We were all bleeding as soon as we got to the hospital. Ben was shouting, 'I'm bleeding inside'. I was wondering whether somebody can know if he was bleeding inside. I checked my own body to know whether I was bleeding inside".
Nnadozie stated that the Lagos State Commissioner of Police was alerted by their colleagues who got the information about what happened and he sent his men to go and check the vehicle. "Could you believe that before they got to the spot where the accident occurred the lorry driver had sneaked in to tow the lorry out of the place as if he deliberately packed the tipper there to cause the carnage, kill people and then realise it was time to take it after a life has been lost". Nnadozie queried bitterly.
Even as friends and relations continue to mourn Ben, Nnadozie asked: "The biggest question is how many people will they kill? What is the essence of having a bridge like Third Mainland Bridge without street lights? Why is the bridge not policed? Where were the Lagos State Traffic Management Agency(LASTMA) officials, who used to tow people's vehicles indiscriminately? Where were the Lagos State Emergency buses? He expressed disappointment at the way the dead are treated at the Gbagada General Hospital. He alleged the they dumped Ben's body amidst decomposing unidentified bodies in the hospital. "Before 12.00 noon on Sunday, Ben's body had swollen. It was difficult to identify him. It took the timely intervention of the Commissioner of Police on the orders of the Inspector-General , the Oba-elect and The Guardian management for them to retrieve Ben's body, confirm the autopsy and do the embalming".
Looking back at the accident, Nnadozie felt that the late Akparanta instinctively wanted to save the two others: "If he had allowed the vehicle to move the way we saw the lorry, we might have plunged into the lagoon. He tried. Everything happened so suddenly". He advised Nigerians to learn how to stop at accident scenes, because the victims might be somebody dear to them.

Original_Search_Title: Stories About the Rand Are World's Apart
Headline: South Africa: Stories About the Rand Are World's Apart
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — As expected, Christine Qunta's minority report on the rand's 37%
depreciation last year did not provide any fireworks. It merely called for
more investigation, a call that government is unlikely to heed.
Qunta failed to come up with new evidence or starkly different
conclusions to those reached by the other commissioners, advocate John
Myburgh and Development Bank of Southern Africa CE Mandla Gantsho.
Although most of the differences related to the use of language, or what
Justice Minister Penuell Maduna refers to as nuance, the reports did give
important insight into broader debates about how SA should handle its
growing integration into the global economy.
It can be assumed that the authors of the majority report are more
comfortable with SA's integration into the global economy, despite the risks
it poses for the country's small and relatively open economy. This is in
line with the thinking in the presidency and the national treasury, which
sees globalisation and the fuller integration in the global economy as not
just inevitable, but desirable.
On the basis of her report, Qunta might plausibly be classified as an
economic nationalist, who views big corporations with suspicion and probably
sees greater regulation and not less as the solution to the country's
vulnerability.
The report almost takes one back to Trevor Manuel's early days as
finance minister, when he used to rail against the disproportionate
influence of "amorphous markets" on people's lives.
This is most evident when Qunta touches on the role of speculators
citing the example of the east Asian crisis of 1997 and her defence of the
Reserve Bank's administration of exchange controls.
In her report, Qunta draws widely on the experiences of the socalled
Asian tigers, and largely accepts the view that currency crises tend to have
more to do with outsiders' perceptions and actions than domestic
policy.
At face value, the Malaysian case would seem to support the
argument. Its currency depreciated 40% in six months during 1997 despite
posting economic growth of 8,7%, having low inflation of 3,8%, a 2,5%
unemployment rate and an external debt equivalent to 42% of gross domestic
product (GDP).
Qunta also quoted Joseph Yam, CE of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority,
who in 1999, blamed the Asian financial crisis on unmanaged and unregulated
capital outflows.
The trigger was the collapse of the Thai baht after investors, spooked
by a sharp drop in the country's exports, sold assets in anticipation of a
devaluation of a currency they felt was over-valued, but ironically given a
superficial boost by capital inflows.
It quickly spread through the whole region, affecting seemingly healthy
economies from Malaysia to Hong Kong.
The Malaysians took it particularly hard, with President Mahathir
Mohamad changing overnight from one of the darlings of the Washington
consensus to one of its loudest critics. Statements that crony capitalism
was largely to blame were particularly hurtful because of the suggestion of
racism in the analysis.
But what was clear was that the punishment meted out to east Asia was
disproportionate to the crime.
Mahathir blamed the crisis on greedy speculators and reacted by
reintroducing selected exchange controls, including putting a stop to the
trade in the currency and fixing its rate to the US dollar.
Those days seem a distant memory now as the economy recovers from the
bruising it took.
Those who said Mahathir's rejection of orthodoxy would lead the economy
into a path of destruction have been forced to eat their words. For their
part, the Malaysians seem oblivious to the fact that their neighbours, who
did not reintroduce exchange controls, have also since recovered.
Qunta, though, is likely to have taken expert testimony by
representatives from Malaysia's central bank to heart, and probably saw it
as confirmation of the inherent dangers of opening up one's economy to the
vultures sitting in financial institutions' trading rooms.
The theme is also evident in her defence of the Bank's actions and her
findings regarding Deutsche Bank's activities.
She does not make conclusive findings about the legality of Deutsche's
asset swap deals, except to call for an investigation by the office of the
director of prosecutions the main difference between her and the other
commissioners.
Another source of difference is that she absolves the Bank's October 14
statement, which told banks to observe existing exchange control
regulations, from blame.
She stops short of accusing private banks' CEs of lying or misleading
the public, but dismisses their claims that the October statement had been
misinterpreted, noting they had been fully briefed before it was made
public.
Her distrust of private sector players and preference for more rigorous
regulation also becomes obvious here.
She notes that for exchange control regulation to be effective, there
has to be an element of trust between the regulators and the regulated. She
notes that most big corporations view exchange control in a negative light,
the implication being that they cannot be trusted to honour their
responsibilities.
Qunta is also critical of the fact that the commission never had the
scope to investigate the activities of authorised dealers, including their
loss or profit positions during the period under review.
With their inconclusive findings, the most that can be said of the
reports is that they probably shed more light on the debate about the
economic direction SA should take.
There are two streams of thought. On one side there are those willing to
play the global game within the existing framework. Qunta represents those
in favour of a more forceful approach, which is not shy of increased
regulation.

Original_Search_Title: Proposed New Kaduna 'Ll Generate N20b Annually, Says Committee
Headline: Nigeria: Proposed New Kaduna 'Ll Generate N20b Annually, Says Committee
Body_Text:
THE proposed New Kaduna State if created, would generate about N20 billion annually from local endowments, the chairman of the committee for the creation of the state, Col. Elias Nyan has said.
He noted at the inauguration of the 15-member state creation committee in Kaduna that the area with a population of about 2.5 million, was blessed, in commercial quantities, with ginger, cassava, yam, soya beans, solid minerals, fisheries and livestock.
According to him, "we have the best all year round grazing reserves that can be tsetsefly-free with sufficient water for livestock development for export.
"We already know that we are endowed with sufficient skill to develop and train manpower for export to other states and neghbouring countries.
"All we need is freedom for self determination which the creation of a new state will offer," he said.Col. Nyan justified the yearning for the creation of New Kaduna State from the existing one, saying that the agitation was brought about by the search for justice."We want our state because we want justice. It is the lack of justice that forces our people in a crisis situation to put themselves on the line of fire to be shot and killed in the protection of their dignity."It is lack of justice that makes it possible that our own clap and jump for joy at the demise of ours.
"It is that lack of justice, met lack of peace, that ----- our people to be level that they think it is normal to deny their own and adopt an adversary in a contest that is otherwise within their own reach to win.
"Nobody can teach us anything about peace and nobody can deny us justice forever.
"Intellectual as the battle for the creation would be, we shall demise all democratic means to ensure our total self-determination," the chairman stated.He urged the people of the South part of Kaduna which makes up the proposed state to have faith in themselves and the ability of the committee to press for the relisation of their dream for an own state.Col. Nyan therefore called for their cooperation and support for the onerous task ahead.

Headline: Nigeria: JAMB As the Model
Body_Text:
Is the Joint Admission and Matriculations Board just astoundingly abreast of our hi-tech world, or did the sanitization process which had begun under the Abacha administration actually borne fruit? Perhaps a bit of both, but the fact is, JAMB has proved itself to be the comeback kid. An organization that has been so fraught with fraudulent activity, a sad state of internal administration and the inability to quite gain or earn the confidence of the people (including those who depended on it as the only route to university education, and later to other tertiary institutions of learning in the country) is suddenly an organization to watch.
To suggest that all the corrupt people have been flushed out of JAMB is to underestimate the power and the proliferation of corruption in this country. Corrupt people remain in their multitude, sadly, particularly in our civil service. It would not require any stretch of the imagination to figure out this includes JAMB.
Yet, JAMB has somehow made itself a model for all parastatals by employing the only means by which an organization can function properly. Structuring. Jamb has managed to put in place structures which will live after the people who created them, which will prevent massive corruption of the scale to which we are used, which will continue to ensure administrative efficiency long after its creator is gone unless there is some kind of sabotage. JAMB has demonstrated beyond reasonable doubt its belief in the theory that no one person's interest can supersede the national interest, and that national interest can be crowned by structures.
First, by its new 'computerized' system, JAMB deletes automatically a multitude of ills. The most obvious of these is the problem of crowds which have traditionally assailed the organization's offices from time immemorial. The number of students who have to see a JAMB official for their own peculiar problem will have to personally and physically see them, but a student who just wants to get the same information which thousands of others want to get will simply visit a website. Of course, this would come with its own problems, but progress always does.
Naturally, the less the access is to the officials, the less the access will be to the corrupt official, but the most important aspect of JAMB' hi-tech move is that it proves that accountability and openness makes a huge difference not only to the presence of the organization but to its very future.Our democracy has a lot to learn from this.
Yet JAMB has not only raised to international standards the level of the expertise of its individual employees, it has created huge revenue and a constant source of that to the private computer practitioners. It has raised the infotech awareness of the over a million students who took the examination this year, and there is no way this will not have its effect on the standards of graduates we turn out from Nigerian universities from now on.
Sycophancy and the abuse of democracy
The last time Mr. Annan visited Nigeria, he left us to face our national bereavement alone at the death (by tea partying, if it must be put in lay terms) of Nigeria's president-elect who was never to be, the late Bashorun M.K.O.
