Abiola. Abiola was hell bent on eradicating poverty, and most believed he would have. His death therefore had been like putting a steaming bowl of gourmet prepared pounded yam and edikaikong in front of a famished man for long enough to have him inhale the maddening aroma, and then taking it away from in front of him just as he is about to get at it.
It has taken Mr. Annan a little while to pay us a condolence visit, but he could not have made his short visit any more memorable, especially judging by the words by which he addressed us. Without as much as accusing us or pointing fingers, he had spoken about the abuse of democracy, a major disease and scourge ravaging our nation today.
If the fear of God is the beginning of wisdom, the fear of any lesser man or entity than God must, by the same reasoning, be the beginning of folly. The total disregard for truth, justice, equity and fair play which has become so pervasive in our society is by no means exclusive to the political scene. Unfortunately, with a new and delicate democracy to nurture, the environment in the nation right now makes it appear more prevalent in this department.
Political prostitution is not a new phenomenon in Nigeria, and we saw enough during the tenures of the last two dictators. There were enough people who literally put their mouths where their pockets were, so much so that we even had a million man march to prove popular support for the election of the late General Abacha as a civilian President. Against great hope and the goodwill of loyal Nigerians, things have not changed. If anything, the trend has become even more dangerous. It has become infinitely easier to exploit and hide behind the freedoms and liberties available with civilian governance with the often realized aim of propagating deceit for material gain.
If this is so because of the extreme poverty in the land, it is no wonder and certainly no coincidence that the same leaders who have understandable need for political prostitutes are the same ones who tend to keep their led in perpetual poverty, often while wasting money on exorbitant PR measures to somehow convince them that though their standards of living are dropping, they are, by some magic, somehow better off. It is no wonder either that Abiola did not have enough sycophants to ensure he got Nigeria's ultimate power.
The devil, the military political class and the deep blue sea
Right now, one thing is clear within our highly charged and still charging political atmosphere, the result of preparations for 2002 (August) and 2003. We are in dire want of credible presidential candidates. Most states have the same problem on the gubernatorial level, but Nigeria is so structured as to place far more importance on the presidency as the actually unfortunate man on whose shoulder the huge load of power at the center rests. We are in dire shortage of credible candidates, and the fear that 2003 might end up as business as usual is becoming more real and more justifiable by the second. Of course this could have been helped and can still be, if we wake up from this national slumber and realize that we can do something about the continued exclusivity and the unlevel playing field created by INEC of the Nigerian political scene specifically for 2003. There has been talk of massive boycott of registration, but that, judging by the general attitude, seems hardly likely.
The question of who a credible candidate is however getting more difficult to define. Is he the one who has an impeccable manifesto? Events in the more recent past have flawed that as a means by which a 'credible candidate' might be identified. There is now emerging a new political class in Nigeria_ the one of the politicians who have had enough exposure to be able to figure out the problems of Nigeria, and have enough education and intelligence to know exactly what to do to fix it, but alas, they do not have the integrity, and that is hardly their aim for seeking power. This leaves us with the present leadership, the determination of the Igbos to get the presidency this time around and those who have served under military administrations as the groups which we have to chose from.
Many fear for an igbo presidency, which might be unrealizable within the context of the money politics which we have accepted, as the Igbos are not very famous for diverting funds to Switzerland.The fear in ethnic quarter's that if the clamour for an Igbo presidency arises purely from a perception by that group that it has been marginalized, it is likely that the other ethnic groups might suffer the same fate as they had. This might not be a clever way of thinking, but politics is not about brains, it is about public perceptions.
But it is those who have served under the military who will have the hardest time convincing their followers, and this is in spite of the fact that notable Nigerians of integrity have served Nigeria under the worst military dictatorships (these include Tai Solarin, Wole Soyinka, Lateef Jakande, and others). Putting in mind however that it is between the devil and the deep blue sea, how can we run away from those who have served under dictatorships and embrace those who had been the dictatorships themselves. The whole thing makes little sense.

Headline: Nigeria: Battle for Abiola's Inheritance Continues
Body_Text:
The drama is still playing itself out in London .  But what have the
Emir of Zaria , Shehu Idris and business whiz-kid - Onwuka Kalu got to do
with the tussle?  A lot
This is not pretty.  It seems the family of the late business mogul,
M.K.O. Abiola, whose political triumph was cut short by the Babangida
administration in 1993, is still at odds on how to distribute his wealth
among his survivors.  Four years after his death in incarceration, the
acrimonious battle for the control of the purse strings inherited from the
super-rich man, reputed to be worth tens of billions of Naira continues and
it is drawing more and more people into the line of fire as they try to help
the family sort out the mess.
The main bone of contention is number of Wills that Abiola made before
his death.  How many are they?  The answer though complicated may help break
the logjam the family has found itself.  The truth, however, is, no one
really knows how many there are, although there is little doubt that there
may be at least two signed Wills - the 1989 and 1994 Wills.  Last year at a
trial contesting the 1989 Will in London , it was admitted in court that
there may be an unsigned 1993 Will.  Zeinab Duke, one of Abiola's wives had
sought to declare the 1989 Will a forgery when the revelation was made.  The
1989 Will gives substantial advantage to Kola, Samsudeen, Olalekan and
Monsuru, all Abiola's sons from his first wife, Simbiat, as they were
exempted from the blood test all other Abiola's sons and daughters were to
take to prove their paternity.  The four were also the proposed
administrators of the Will.
It was Duke-Abiola's contention in her court papers that the Will had no
original copy and that the bank - NatWest Bank, Pentonville Road , London ,
which was said to be holding the Will in its vault had disclaimed any
knowledge of it.  As she told Newswatch then, "they formulated the gossips
and produced it in paper form and called it a Will.  It has now backfired
because they were supposed to carry it out in England ."  Using her own
writing experts to declare the Will a forgery, she asked the court to throw
it out as a fake.  She lost the case early last year as the court held the
Will authentic.  But it did prescribe that all Abiola's children, without
exemption, take a paternity test.  Thereafter, on August 1, 2001 , a
district judge in the Family Division of the High Court of Justice, in
London , granted an order appointing Adam Broke, Timothy Daniel and Kolawole
Olatokunbo Abiola as "joint administrators with the Will dated 25th October
1989 ."
Just when you thought that the family would finally start putting the
Will palaver to a decent rest, a new revelation threw a spanner in the works
once again.  Apparently, Abiola had indeed put together a "last Will" on
June 7, and 9, 1994, shortly before his arrest and incarceration by Nigeria
's last unrelenting dictator, the late General Sani Abacha.  The new Will
revokes all "testamentary dispositions made by me with the exception of the
Wills I have for any realty or immovable assets I may have in England ,
Wales and Nigeria ."  But it covers all Abiola's liquid and other assets as
well as property in the form of shares, stocks, bonds, bank deposits, safe
deposit boxes, share dividends or in any other form over which I may have
general power of appointment which may at the time of my death be situated
worldwide including Holland , Switzerland , USA , Egypt , Togo , Spain ,
Senegal , South Africa , Guernsey , Jersey - Channel Islands , Canada ,
France , Uganda , Germany and Australia. "
According to papers in Newswatch possession, the joint administrators of
the 1994 Will are His Royal Highness, Shehu Idris, the Emir of Zaria , a
well-known friend of Abiola, and Onwuka Kalu, once regarded as the whiz-kid
of business and owner of the Onwuka Hitek Industries.  In a letter on June
9, 1994 , Abiola told Kalu: "As we have agreed, it is important that I tidy
up my affairs and prepare for the worst especially in view of the continuous
treachery of some of our friends.  Accordingly, I have drawn a Will for some
of my assets in which I have appointed His Royal Highness, the Emir of Zaria
- Alhaji (Dr) Shehu Idris and you as executors.
But the emir told Newswatch in Zaria that he had no knowledge of
M.K.O. Abiola's Will.  He said at no time during and even after Abiola's
lifetime was the issue of the will discussed with him.
"I am surprised that people come to ask me if Abiola gave me the right
to administer his Will.  But each time they come I tell them that I don't
know anything about it.  I wonder where they are getting that information
from.  Abiola never discussed that with me during his lifetime, and the
issue of administering his Will has not come up at least with me even after
his death," Idris said.
He told Newswatch that it is not possible to link him with the issue of
administering Abiola's Will.  This he said is because the concept of a Will
is against the tenets of Islam.  "Even if Abiola had discussed the issue
with me during his life time, I would have declined to administer the Will
because it is against the principles of Islam.  And as a religious leader, I
couldn't have accepted to do that," he told Newswatch.
Idris, however, said that Chief M. K. O. Abiola was a very close friend
of his during the former's life time, and appealed to the feuding members of
the family to eschew bitterness and amicably resolve their differences.  He
said the only way this can be achieved in the family, is through roundtable
dialogue with all members of the family.
He said the earlier this was resolved, the better.  It is only when
there is peace and harmony in the family that the principles, which the late
Abiola stood and fought for, will be better appreciated by Nigerians.
However, Kalu confirmed his own appointment and told Newswatch that the
emir was indeed appointed by Abiola but that because of his tight schedule,
resigned his role and asked Onwuka to take charge of the Will.  Onwuka said
the emir is kept fully briefed about developments concerning the Will.
Nevertheless, Onwuka is finding out that as with the 1989 Will, this new
one too does not sit down well with all of Abiola's family.  Although he
obtained a grant of probate in Amsterdam , the Netherlands , based on the
Will, he has run into problems executing it.  Kola Abiola and the other
joint administrators of the 1989 Will are opposed to the new one, which
gives Kola a lesser role but makes Zeinab Duke, the wife who challenged the
authenticity of the 1989 Will in court, the residual beneficiary.
A letter written to Kalu' s lawyers, Manches Solicitors, on January 4,
this year, by D.J. Freeman Solicitors who represent the court appointed
administrators of the 1989 Will clearly shows what their clients, Kola,
Broke and Daniel, think about the new Will.  According to the letter, a copy
of which is in Newswatch possession, "there is some concern with the family
over the 1994 Will and its authenticity.  I appreciate that your client has
his own views on this.  However, as you will be aware, I represent the court
appointed administrators under an earlier Will and therefore my clients wish
to collect it and administer the assets and therefore have to consider the
position of the 1994 Will very seriously.  For a number of reasons, they are
not prepared to simply step into your client's shoes as administrators under
the 1994 Will unless and until there is evidence that the Will is authentic.
We believe that the matter of the Will's authenticity needs to be dealt with
urgently and the simplest way of doing this is for the two Wills to be
compared so that concerns as to the authenticity of the 1994 Will can be
satisfied."
To this end, D.J. Freeman requested that the 1994 papers be transferred
from the Oxford court where they are lodged to the Principal Registry in
London where the 1989 Will is lodged.  Kalu didn't oppose the move although
believes that rather than resolve issues trying to engage handwriting
experts to test the signatures on the two Wills would further drag the
family's reputation in the mud and that collectively they will continue to
lose a substantial part of their inheritance to claims from the lawyers,
who, as Kalu describes it to Newswatch, "have the yam and the knife to cut
it with."
As Zeinab Duke's challenge to the 1989 Will proved, the conclusion of
handwriting experts may not necessarily be accepted by the court.  Although
the experts she employed cast some doubts on the 1989 Will, the judge still
held it authentic.  As Kalu noted in his letter to his solicitors January
31, 2002 , " I do not really have faith in the handwriting expert they are
going to engage because his work or comparison of the handwriting is going
to be one-sided since the materials for such work is going to be provided
solely by the contestants."
In a letter written by Kalu's solicitors to the administrators on March
26, 2002 , they were asked to provide Kalu with "details of any report you
have received from the expert analysing the handwriting contained in the
relevant documents in this matter.  If the expert's report had been
completed to date, please confirm when I can expect to receive the report."
As at the time of writing this report, Kalu had not received any words back
from the lawyers.
What he would really want is for the family to make some compromises and
resolve the issue to the satisfaction of all members of the Abiola family.
"Compromises must be made for the peace of the family," he told Newswatch.
"They must worry about the cost to lawyers who charge based on hours and
minutes.  The quickest way is for everyone to be ready to negotiate," he
added.
His worries may be justified.  Documents in Newswatch possession show
that on February 25, 2002 , the lawyers sent their bill number 23896 for
Â£29,439.30 to the administrators of Abiola estate for payment.  Added to
this was also bill 25329 for Â£22,590.60 for a grant total of Â£59,029.96.
Newswatch was unable to confirm whether or not the bill was paid, but
suffice it to say that if it had not been paid within 28 days of receipt, it
would have been attracting an "interest rate of two percent per annum over
the lending base rate of the Royale Bank of Scotland ."
William Brouthton, a representative of D.J. Freeman who spoke to
Newswatch on the controversial 1994 Will was unwilling to comment about
charges made by the law firm. "I can't comment on the billing," he
said.
However, Kalu is unlikely to get his wishes that the matter be resolved
by compromise, at least not immediately. Broughthton told Newswatch that the
administrators of the 1989 Will are challenging the authenticity of the new
Will in court.  "We are aware there is another Will," he said, adding "we
are currently dealing with issues regarding it.  In fact, we are challenging
it in court.
It is a scenario that Kalu thinks can drag on for many years if care is
not taken.  He admits that it may be unrealistic to expect those who have
much to gain by the 1989 Will to just give in and accept the new one, "it is
futile to go on like this.  Anyone can find reasons to challenge the Will.
And if you go on like this, the matter may drag on for God forbid, another
10 years.  Everyone must look at the experience of the Okoti-Eboh family and
how long it took to resolve problems associated with the man's estate.  What
is happening is not good for the memory of a great man like Abiola and the
longer the fight goes on, the more the assets are depleted."
What is clear is that the controversy and the court challenges to the
1989 Will have engendered bad blood among key members of the family to the
point where each side may probably not be ready for any compromise at the
moment.  It is hard to see how Kola will accept that Zeinab plays a key role
in the execution of his father's Will.  In fact, during the hearing of the
challenge to the older Will at a London court last year, Gloria, another of
Abiola's many wives was keen to prove to the world that Zeinab was not
married to Abiola, no matter the evidence suggesting to the contrary.  It
was Gloria's impecuniousness, according to a Newswatch source, that led to
the appointment of administrators for the 1989 Will by the court.  The
administrators can disburse money from the estate to aid family members who
are in financial difficulty, although an agreement was reached with Kalu
ensuring that he is kept abreast of such development.
It is also important to note that a wary eye is also cast on Kalu's
role, especially since a member of the family told the man himself that
Abiola is a tribalist who would not make an Igbo man an executor of his Will
or even leave substantial inheritance to Zeinab by making her a residual
beneficiary.  Kalu said he has nothing to gain but has been trying to keep a
promise he made "to a friend."
Nevertheless, the fear here is that no one really knows the value of
Abiola's wealth or what the residual inheritance would amount to for Zeinab.
Even Kalu has been pleading with all and sundry to forward to him a list of
Abiola's holdings they may be aware of with lawyers to Kola and the other
administrators of the 1989 Will to "let me have: (a) full details of all
assets of Chief Abiola which you have located up to date, (b) full details
of those assets which you have distributed, and (c) full details of to whom
those assets have been distributed."
If the 1994 Will survives all challenges, especially the court
challenge, not a few people outside the Abiola family will thump their chest
that it reflects the generosity for which the man was so famously known.
Apart from the generous sums of money bequeathed his children, wives and
extended family, he left substantial money for many causes, charities and
organisations that he supported in his lifetime.
Among the African and other foreign organisations who benefit from this
1994 Will are: the International Red Cross, International Peace Academy,
Martin Luther King Centre in Atlanta, Georgia, USA, Amnesty International,
United Nation's Children's Fund, Greenpeace International, Black
Parliamentary (sic) Caucus, Jimmy Carter Foundation, the Abiola Foundation
for Reparations, and the African National Congress (ANC).  The Imperial
Cancer Research, London and even the Confederation of African Football are
also beneficiaries.
Locally, the Gani Fawehinmi Scholarship Fund benefits from the Will.  So
do Nigeria Labour Congress, Nigerian Union of Journalists, National Union of
Petroleum Engineer (sic), NUPENG, National Association of Nigerian Students
(NANS), Campaign for Democracy, Civil Liberties Organisation, National Union
of Road Transport Workers, Petroleum and Gas Senior Staff Employees of
Nigeria (sic) PENGASSEN (sic), Nadeco, Baptist Boys High School, Abeokuta,
Nigerian Football Association, Zulikha Abiola Memorial Qur'anic Centre,
Nigerian Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs, Christian Association of
Nigeria and the Nigerian Union of Teachers.  Ajoke Murtala Mohammed, wife of
the assassinated Nigerian leader, was left $50,000 in the 1994 Will.
For these so-called beneficiaries, this is a pie in the sky for them as
the authenticity of the Will remains a major headache and it may take
sometime down the road for the controversy to be resolved.  This is why Kalu
told Newswatch that he is trying to encourage the emir of Zaria to call a
meeting of all the stakeholders in this battle for Abiola's wealth to
resolve the issue.  He said he was disappointed that Yoruba elders have not
thought it fit to call members of the family to order to protect Abiola's
good name.  He is equally unhappy that President Olusegun Obasanjo, a man he
claims benefited from Abiola's misfortunes, has not intervened to help the
family sort out its problems.  There had been rumour that the president had
tried to help the family but Newswatch was unable to confirm this.
The safe prediction is that unless there is some "divine" intervention,
the battle over the Will of the late business mogul may rumble on for
sometime to come.  It is indeed a sad prospect.

Original_Search_Title: I 'Listen' to My Body a Lot - Charles Edosonmwan, Lawyer
Headline: Nigeria: I 'Listen' to My Body a Lot - Charles Edosonmwan, Lawyer
Body_Text:
AS a young Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), Charles Umwese Edosonmwan has a crowded schedule that takes him around Nigeria and beyond almost on permanent basis.
Charles, a martial arts enthusiast, believes his life lies in the hand of God, however, this does not in anyway suggest that he takes the state of his health lightly.
In this brief chat, he speaks on his work and general attitude to his own well-being. Excerpts:
"Everyday, I wake up around 5.00 am after which I say my prayers. This is usually the first thing I do as a Christian. I normally pray in the company of my wife as my children are still quite small, so they cannot participate actively in the prayer sessions.
After prayers, I may remain in bed for another 10 minutes or so just to relax. I do not do anything at this time apart from lying very still. At times, when I feel like it, I go out for a walk. A few people who are quite close to me know I am into the martial arts, so I'm fond of doing my Katas which is actually some kind of body enhancing exercises. After all these, I return to have a quick bath at about 6.00am before dressing up. I take my time doing this. It is also around this time that the kids wake up and I'd love to spend some time with them since the job does not allow one to see much of them during the day.
I leave home early without taking breakfast. My routine does not really allow time for that. However on weekends, when there is time, we take breakfast as a family though it may come as late as 10.30 or 11.00.
On Sundays when we go to church, we often do without it. But I do not joke with lunch and dinner no matter the circumstances. These are my main meals of the day, and when I eat, it is always in the African way. Pounded yam is a favorite especially for dinner if I have to take something solid.
Most days, I head straight to the court from home or drop in for a few minutes at the office. I stay on the mainland and I have to leave home early enough to beat the usual early morning traffic. Court activities come early so all relevant materials have to be prepared at least a day before in order to move to the court on time.
I go back to the office right after court sessions and if there are meetings and law reports to be read, files to be seen or clients to be met, I do that before leaving. The office closes by 6.00 pm although I could remain until I'm through with any outstanding work. There are times I take my work home. To be frank, no serious barrister would say he does not take work home. There are these law reports that are better read and digested at home. Clients who keep coming to see you or telephoning you do not allow enough time and concentration in the office.
Usually, I retire to bed anytime between midnight and 1.00 a.m. If I'm home early enough, I catch up with latest happenings on the news broadcast on TV. You would also find me glued to the set if a football match is being televised. In fact, I bought a new TV set just for the ongoing World Cup. I love the game and I have not missed a single match. I was a keen footballer when I was much younger, and although I'm too old to kick and jump around now, I still do some training moves related to the game.
It's not that I'm getting very old, but the fact is that I cannot do certain things as well as I used to when I was younger. For instance, I know I cannot kick or punch the way I used to but I believe I'm still quite a healthy person. I know my life is in God's hand. Let us not underestimate His mercies in this regard. It is a good thing to remain active and to work out as much as possible.
There are a few people who cannot really differentiate between working out and building muscles. Anyone who needs to work out should see a doctor first. You need to have your physical fitness assessed to estimate just how much rigors your heart can take. You know how common it is for people to collapse and die while jogging on the road. It's a lot more risky than could be imagined.
My involvement in the martial arts has made me more conscious of my health. Now I listen to my body a lot."

Original_Search_Title: Show Them Pepper, Obasanjo Tells Eagles On Phone
Headline: Nigeria: Show Them Pepper, Obasanjo Tells Eagles On Phone
Body_Text:
AS Nigeria's Super Eagles confront Sweden today in Kobe, Japan, in their second match in the on-going World Cup competition, President Olusegun Obasanjo spoke with the players and their officials on phone yesterday, asking them to face the match squarely.
The Eagles need an outright win today to remain in contention.
President Obasanjo told them to put behind them Sunday's defeat by Argentina.
He spoke to, one after the other, Sports Minister Steve Akiga, Coach Adegboye Onigbinde, NFA Chairman, Dominic Oneya and six players.
President Obasanjo told Coach Onigbinde in pidgin English: "The soup wey go sweet go need plenty fire," (meaning no success without sweat). Coach Onigbinde assured the President that everybody was in high spirits while team captain, Okocha said, "the boys are fully ready."
The President told Ifeanyi Udeze that the team should show their opponents "pepper."
"If possible, score from your left full back position," the President added, with Udeze assuring him that "African food," such as "pounded yam" was available to them in Japan.
"Pastor" Taribo West said they were also going to pray to which the President replied: "God will answer your prayer." The pastor told the President that the team had prepared "wonderfully well."
Upon being told that the Super Eagles Supporters' Club were already in Kobe, the President said: "Tell the Supporters' Club that they should cheer the boys to victory."
To Goalkeeper Ike Shorunmu, the President said: "Be cool, calm and calculated. Nothing should pass you tomorrow."
When Garba Lawal told the President that they were all ready, the President asked him whether he could tell Nigerians at home that "victory is assured"? Lawal said the President's earlier letter had also been read to them.
"Fight like wounded lions. Don't eat too much. You are Super Eagles. Everything would be super. You dey kampe!" he told the boys.

Original_Search_Title: PDP Women Team Off To India-On Tech Transfer Mission
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Women Team Off To India-On Tech Transfer Mission
Body_Text:
Abuja — AS a 37-member Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) women delegation left for India Saturday, on a technological transfer mission, the party's national secretary, Prince Vincent Ogbulafor, said the visit was not a jamboree.The PDP's chief scribe made this clarification in Abuja at a press conference where he formally announced the party's version of poverty alleviation progamme tagged "PDP's income generating programme".
Prince Ogbulafor said the delegation, which is expected to spend a week in India at the total cost of N12 million, is being headed by the PDP's national women leader, Chief Mrs Josephine Anenih. Other members of the delegation, according to him, are women professionals carefully nominated and selected from each State of the federation and Abuja.
He said the women were going to India to see things for themselves especially small scale industries and to come home with something that would be practiced and passed on to others, adding they are being briefed on tips that they would look for to avoid coming home empty-handed.
India, he said, was chosen because it has an established framework for the training of small scale and micro enterprises as well as other income generating measures.
"The country produces over 2,500 different items from its small scale and micro enterprises. These enterprises account for 40 per cent of value added in manufacturing 35 per cent of national export, seven per cent of the gross domestic product and a sizeable proportion of employment. Small scale enterprises have the greatest potential traditionally for employment generation," he said.
He added that the above constitute the attractions for choosing India for the visit by the women who will play a central role in the execution of the part income generation programme. While PDP State chairman nominated one delegate each, he said, for States with PDP factions or where PDP is not in control, the party ministers were asked to make the nomination.
According to him, the party's income generating programme (IGP) is aimed at complementing government's efforts in poverty eradication through measures at empowering the poor and vulnerable groups, stressing that it will also provide a source of income to the target beneficiaries by equipping them with the necessary means of sustaining themselves.
"We are adopting the doctrine of providing a hoe instead of yam, fishing net as opposed to the provision of fish. By empowering the poor, we are helping to reduce poverty," he said.
While the party has since received about six rice milling machines for onward distribution to the six geo-political zones, he said upon return, the delegation will be assisted by the party to procure small loans from the bank. He further said that the target group of beneficiaries of the IGP are the women and other vulnerable groups, especially the disabled.

Original_Search_Title: Peoples Democratic Party women delegation leaves for India
Headline: Nigeria: Peoples Democratic Party women delegation leaves for India
Body_Text:
THE Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), will today despatch a thirty seven member strong women delegation to India to understudy the Asian country's small and medium scale industry with a view to transferring the skill to the country.
The visit which is the direct result of the recent visit by the party's Income Generating Programme focused on women delegation to India recently, according to the national secretary of the party, Chief Vincent Ogbulafor, is to put women in a position to help themselves and their society.
He said the Income Generating Programme of the party is aimed at complementing government's effort at poverty eradication through measures to empower the poor and vulnerable group. It aims at providing a source of income to the target beneficiaries by equipping them with the necessary means of sustaining themselves.
"We are adopting the doctrine of providing a hoe instead of yam; fishing net as opposed to the provision of fish. By empowering the poor we are helping to reduce poverty," he said.
The PDP secretary said that women are being focused on because of their central role they play in the family and the society. Women bear the bulk of the hardship in the family and any adversity on them affects the family most. Above all they are more vulnerable because of cultural and social limitations on them', he observed.
He explained that India was chosen because of its well established framework for the thriving of small scale industry and micro-enterprises as well as other income generating measures.

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Lagos — She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary recently : "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advice of the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Ghana: AMA Gives Squatters 2 Weeks
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) has given squatters living in the Korle Lagoon Project area two weeks to vacate the place, particularly Sodom and Gomorrah, Konkomba Yam Market, Onion Market and Galloway because the Government is losing $74,000 dailyfor not making the area free for the project to continue.
Mr. Solomon Darko, the AMA Chief Executive gave this ultimatum in Accra yesterday. He said the second phase of the project should have continued just after the completion of the first phase but the squatters failure to leave the place has impeded the project and the government is losing huge sums of money due to the contract agreement it has with the donor partner.
Mr. Darko said the project area has become a haven for criminals, unacceptable environmental sanitation practices and the squatters have been removing the stones and gravel from the dykes and drains constructed under the first phase of the project.
He said the lagoon has been subjected to environmental abuse by industries and human activities. "In order to find a solution to the problem, the AMA therefore seeks the co-operation of all squatters in ensuring that the areas mentioned are made free from any human habitation and any other activities likely to obstruct the early completion of all the projects," he said.
Mr. Darko said if the squatters fail to comply with the directive, a special task will come in to demolish the buildings on the project site.
He said an AMA committee negotiated with the squatters who are engaged in petty economic activities to be relocated to another area and those who may want to go back to their various homes would be provided buses for their journey.

Headline: Ghana: 'Show Love to HIV/Aids Victims'
Body_Text:
FAMILIES OF people living with HIV/AIDS have been urged to show love and care to the patients as this would improve their quality of life.
The appeal was made by the chairperson of the Koforidua Association for Women Empowerment (KAWE) when they made a presentation to an HIV/AIDS patient they adopted at Asamang Tanfoe, last week, in the Atiwa constituency.
Items presented include sugar, rice, cooking oil, tubers of yam, soap, vitamin-enriched drugs and Â¢200,000 cash.
Presenting the items to a close family member of the patient, the chairperson of KAWE, Mrs. Joana Asante Ansong, advised the public to join the campaign against HIV/AIDS and help reduce the stigma attached to the disease in the Eastern Region and the country as a whole.
She appealed to churches, traditional authorities and resource persons to assist the association to train more peer educators to improve the lives of HIV/AIDS patients and their immediate dependants.
The family member who received the items on behalf of the patient (name withheld for obvious reasons), thanked the association for the kind gesture and called on other groups to emulate KAWE's example.
The family member who was touched by KAWE's presentation assured all that she would take good care of the patient and asked for God's guidance to do so.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Second Term, an Injustice - Okonkwo
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Second Term, an Injustice - Okonkwo
Body_Text:
Chief Solomon Okonkwo, the national chairman of Igbo Forum is not known for frivolities. He is also not a man of many words. But when an issue revolves around the Igbo race, Chief Okonkwo doesn't betray his age-long patriotism and commitment as an Igbo leader. This he did as he bares his mind on the thorny issue of Igbo presidency next year. He spoke to EJIKEME OMENAZU. Excerpts:
SIR, can you tell us your political antecedent?
Since after the civil war, while trying to survive, I did choose to devote my time to joint Igbo survival especially in Lagos and the whole nation. After the war, I was the first Igbo man appointed to represent the Igbo in the Landlord Residents Association being held at Baale House in Agege Dopemu. During my tenure, I made it possible that no policeman from Elere police station could arrest an Igbo without referring to me.
Then I became the Igbo leader for Agege-Dopemu. Being an Igbo, I knew we cannot do without God. I founded the only Igbo Anglican Church in the area known then as St. Barnabas Anglican Church, now St. Michael's Anglican Church in Dopemu.
Right from that time, I devoted all my time to the church and Igbo course. After that, I got involved in politics through a friend of mine, the late Alhaji Abdullahi Mai Kano due to my growing popularity. Through Mai Kano, I got in contact with the late General Shehu Yar'Adua. I was among the first set of people who founded the defunct Patriotic Front of Nigeria (PFN) in Yar'Adua's house in Ikoyi, and later Kaduna. I was then appointed the co-ordinator for the Eastern zone. I took Yar'Adua round during his first tour of the East then under PFN which later became PDM now being led by the Vice President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar.
Among other founding members of PFN at the first meeting were one Alhaji Buhari, my friend Mai Kano, Prof. Ango Abdullahi, Alhaji Kaita, Alhaji Maina Ma'aji Lawan, now Deputy Senate Leader and Prince Yomi Edu. Among the PFN founding fathers then was Vice President Atiku Abubakar. Then from the PFN when there was no more politics, I was elected the President-General of Igbo speaking community in Lagos, a position I held for two years before we formed the Ohanaeze Ndigbo, Lagos chapter. Then I was elected the chairman and I relinquished the position of President-General Igbo speaking community and Deputy-President of Igbo Council of Chiefs which was Eze Igbo Ojukwu's cabinet.
I was the chairman of Ohanaeze Ndigbo in Lagos until Ojukwu, Ralph Uwazuruike and myself set up the position of Eze Igbo in Lagos which brought a lot of crises. Because of this crisis Igbo in Lagos abandoned other units in Lagos and outside Lagos and found the Igbo Forum and I became the national chairman. Igbo Forum since its formation is the umbrella of all Igbo unions especially in Lagos.
You are the chairman of Igbo Forum, what informed the formation of the body?
Due to this crisis, and the need for peace, and to unite all Igbo groups, we decided to revive the defunct Igbo State Union. Since the Igbo State Union was proscribed by the military, we decided to call the new body Igbo Forum. We operate from all the states of the federation.
What are the main objectives of the body?
The main objective of the Forum is for Igbo to have one identity, one voice and one action. By the grace of God, we are moving on the right path.
What is the strength of your body in terms of national spread?
In Lagos alone we have a registered membership of 1.5 million people. In the East we operate on two levels, the Igbo Elders Forum and the Eastern Pioneers which are mainly the youth political pressure group. In all other areas we are known as Igbo Forum but we also have other associate organisations. We have affiliate relationship with town unions and market associations across the nation.
Would you state the major achievements of the Forum?
Our achievements are many. The Igbo are now discovering that they can work with each other, which was not so before. The youth are now discovering new avenues of using their strength and initiative without being purely militant. Besides, the Igbo Forum succeeded in bringing peace between Igbo and Yoruba especially in Lagos. When trouble developed in Alaba area, leaders of Igbo Forum held several meetings with OPC that led to the peace at Alaba and other areas. Since that time, there has been total peace between the Igbo, Yoruba and OPC. Yes, there is now total peace among the people.
Now, we are working on the need to see our people take full active part in the politics of Lagos. I am using this opportunity to call on Igbo to go for registration in any party of their choice. Those who want to contest election in any part of Lagos can contact us to assist or direct them. Igbo should be prepared to vote and be voted for in any election. They should be bold enough to come out.
I want to stress also that Igbo Forum pioneered and joined other groups to host one of our great sons, Senate President, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim. In fact, we wrote the project proposal which the Senate President approved which led to the hosting of Anyim on December 2, 2001; and we took active part in the planning and implementation of the civil reception.
Since then, we have been shopping for the true Igbo persons from which Igbo can pick the next Nigerian president and we are surprised that there are so many people of Igbo extraction who can be president of this nation. What is the relationship of the Forum with Ohanaeze Ndigbo which has also been at the forefront of Igbo cause?
As I said earlier, I was the founding chairman of Ohanaeze Ndigbo in Lagos State. I held that post for four years. Ojukwu and other Igbo leaders installed me here in Lagos. Then we were holding Igbo Day and Iri Ji (New Yam Festival) every October in Lagos which was the greatest attraction of Igbo in Lagos then. Before the advent of Igbo Forum, I was one of the Igbo leaders in Ohanaeze but when the Igbo middle class and masses felt that Ohanaeze then was too meek we felt that there was need for other people that would be more fearless in fighting for the integration of Ndigbo into the mainstream Igbo politics without apologies or fear.
With my position of chairman of Igbo Forum, I am now in full contact with the reformed Ohanaeze especially the political committee which is where we are interested in now. The political right of every Igbo man is our interest now with other groups that are also fighting for Igbo rights and dignity. 

In one of your recent public statements you lamented that the Igbo do not speak with one voice. Why is this so?
It is so because before now, since the end of the civil war, every Igbo man was going about with the defeatist and selfish aura that as a defeated person. He had to find a platform to actualise himself.. While working for himself, he pretended that he was fighting for others. But that is coming to an end as Igbo are learning to overcome fear and come out for the general good of the Igbo. We are now learning how to co-operate with others. With God being on our side, those with defeatist tendency will see that they are in the minority, while those who want to seek the full right of Ndigbo will be in the majority. And that will be Ebe Ano.
Those who still think that they want to be servants should because of their sons and daughters change their mind and guard their utterances and their old ideas making the Igbo followers and not leaders. The tiger has been unchained. I am calling on all Igbo wherever they may be to reject fear, servitude, threats and intimidation of the past and come out now to tell the world that Igbo are part and parcel and full citizens of Nigeria and should have the rights of full citizenship. There are no two ways about that.
Your Forum has also been advocating for Igbo presidency like the other Igbo-based bodies. Why do you think Ndigbo should have a shot at the presidency?
Thank you very much for this question. It is not why do I think but it is as good as accomplished right because it is our right. It was agreed that the presidency should be rotated to the South and Igbo agreed that the Yoruba should go first. In APP, Ogbonnaya Onu left it for Olu Falae of AD. We wanted to show the Yoruba in AD that Igbo can work with them.
In PDP, Igbo worked for Obasanjo against Ekwueme their own son so that Yoruba will have the taste of presidency. The road was then cleared for the Yoruba when Falae contested against Obasanjo, both Yoruba and Obasanjo emerged president. For Obasanjo to want to go for second term will be the worst injustice to Ndigbo and the Igbo masses and youths will not like that.
Those who are telling Obasanjo go for second term are not telling him the whole truth because he will be disgraced. If any voice comes to him as the voice of God telling him to go for second term, he should check that voice because God has also spoken to us that we should not allow this chance to slip from us, because we shall do all within our power to see that Igbo man emerges as president in 2003.
The best thing that will happen to Obasanjo and the best history he can leave for Nigeria is that he should be the person to hand over to an Igbo president in 2003. He would have made the best history for himself and his grandchildren. He should also remember that his dream to go again means that he is indeed seeking his third term having ruled this nation during the military as head of state. This is a great injustice if it is allowed for one man to rule the nation three times in spite of gentleman's agreement to zone the post to the South with Yoruba taking the first shot and then Igbo. Now he wants to perpetuate himself to the detriment of Ndigbo. It is not acceptable to us.
Do you think Igbo presidency is realistic in 2003?
My answer is yes! yes!! yes!!! Therefore, Igbo should come out now and take their right.
You recently named eight Igbo sons who should be considered for presidency. How did you pick them? First, I want to say that these eight people are eminent sons of Igbo and they have performed creditably in private and public lives. From the PDP we shortlisted Chief (Dr.) Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim and Dr. Orji Uzor Kalu.
For APP we shortlisted Dr. Ogbonnaya Onu and Ikemba Ojukwu. Then for AD we shortlisted Rear Admiral Ndubuisi Kanu. Standing by as consensus candidates are Chief Emeka Anyaoku and Chief Sonny Odogwu. We want Ndigbo to seriously consider these names and may be, reach us with their comments and whatever they have to say about them. We need their support because we have started serious consultations among Ndigbo and other Nigerians. We are also in serious talks with other Eastern ethnic groups on this issue.
These eight eminent sons of Igbo to the best of our knowledge can give Nigeria the best president or leader that they will ever have, that will make everybody say "Aha"! We are calling every Nigerian especially Igbo to give us support. What is the Forum doing to convince Ndigbo that these people should be considered for the plum position?
We have had series of consultations, discussions and contacts. We have started to present these names for everybody to join us, shout them, pray for them, those who have money should bring their money, those who have strength should bring their strength, those who have knowledge and wisdom should bring them out. But those who do not have anything, especially knowledge and wisdom should keep quiet to avoid mistakes because Ndigbo will not forgive any person who will contribute to deny the Igbo their right this time.
Have you also been making contacts with the people you shortlisted to convince them to come out for the position in their various parties?
Yes. It is a continuing process. But some of them are afraid of betrayal of their fellow Igbo as happened in the past. That is why I told those who lacked wisdom to keep quiet because Igbo will not condone betrayal this time around.
What do you think Ndigbo should do to avoid their choice candidate being schemed out at the last minute as was the case with Chief Ekwueme in PDP and Dr. Iwuanyanwu in APP in 1999?
That is why we are saying that every Igbo should bring out what he knows best or what he has for the collective good of the rest and that is the collective will of the Igbo race. That is why we have just said that those that lack wisdom should keep quiet.
Those who think they have money to buy the Igbo rights should go and rest and enjoy their money because the Igbo youths will surely react to any unnecessary selfish sale of the Igbo collective right. This is the time for Nigerian president of Igbo origin. Every other thing to the Igbo conscience, Igbo person is immaterial.
What do you consider as the major advantages of Igbo presidency?
First, it is our right as Nigerians to have an Igbo person as president. Knowing what an Igbo can do, any of these eminent men listed or many others not listed yet, will transform this nation to one of the happiest nation in the world because Igbo men thrives on challenges. The Nigerian nation now is a challenge. Put any of these eminent Igbo sons there and you will see that he will turn the nation to a modern wonder of the world in the shortest possible time.
For example, see what the young man, Anyim Pius Anyim is doing in the Senate. He was unknown until he became the Senate President. He was even untested. But he has wonderfully stabilised the Senate and carried everyone along and all the rancour, all fighting and factions are gone and the Senate is now one body moving like one family. The same thing will happen to the Nigerian nation when an Igbo has become the president in 2003. All ethnic militia will go and Nigeria will move like one family and hidden potentials will sprout up and Nigeria will take her position among the comity of nations. Let the sleeping Igbo wake up now.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Traders React to Proposed Modernisation
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Traders React to Proposed Modernisation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Traders in Mile 12 Market have reacted to the proposed modernisation
of the market as hinted recently by the Lagos State Governor, Senator
Bola Ahmed Tinubu.
Malam Abu, one of the key dealers of fresh tomatoes and pepper in the
market explained that if the market is modernised the prices of goods
will automatically increase. The trader fear that the government is
bound to charge them exorbitantly for the new shops as well as the
space to park their vehicles.
Another trader who spoke to MetroBusiness (Mama Kayode), a yam dealer
in the market, noted that the prices of goods are now high but will
even be higher when the market is modernised.
She, however, noted that she would have loved it if the market is
modernised as the present structure that makes up the market is not
conducive for business.
Madam Oshinowo, another prominent fruit dealer pointed out that the
government is not fair to its citizens in terms of welfare. She cited
the poor Mile 12 structure as an example of government careless
attitude to the people. Oshinowo noted that it is good that the
market is modernised to change the face of the filthy market, but
expressed fears that the traders will be made to pay the cost of the
rehabilitation.
"We are not saying that the market should not be modernised at least
the look of the place will change for the better, but the problem is
that we will be made to bear the high cost of the rehabilitation,"
she said.
Another woman who sells tomatoes at Ola market, in Itire-Mushin
dismissed the pronouncement made by the governor as political, adding
that such pronouncement has been made by past governors with the goal
of reaping political gains.
"Don't mind them, they don't want to do any work. We have heard of
such tales before. Tinubu is talking like that because of election.
He wants the peoples votes," she pointed out.
MetroBusiness gathered that the relocation and modernisation of the
market will definitely have some adverse effect on the users as the
taxes/levies charged on the traders may be increased and this will
inadvertently affect price of goods and services. Some also fear that
they could lose their stalls after payment.
"The government is not sincere, how are we sure we will retain our
shops after payment. It has happened in so many markets where traders
paid, yet lost their stalls," one of the traders said.
It would be recalled that Governor Tinubu early this month while
touring Ikorodu LGA announced plans by his administration to commence
work on the relocation and modernisation of the Mile 12 Market.

Headline: Nigeria: The Queen of Robbers
Body_Text:
This is the story of how the love of money leads a woman into armed
robbery
She could pass as a lady any man would love to have as wife. Tall,
fair skinned and with a glowing set of eyes, Onyinye Abosi is any
man's delight. But, if only looks were all it takes! Beneath the
radiating skin is a rot that has eaten deep into the marrows. Barely
22 years old and at an age when any serious minded lady would be
ambitious about life, especially in the two key areas of career and
marriage, Onyinye had a different kind of passion, a very ravenous
one at that. She loved quick money and would get it at all costs.
This insatiable passion led her to abandon her education at JSS III
level (which her widowed mother paid for from the stipends she makes
from selling fried yams) for the hotels where she made quick money as
a mistress of the night. In no time, she was already in the grip of
sexual promiscuity. "I was making like N2500, N3000. But if I do all
night, I make like N5000 per day" she told this magazine with shaky
lips.
Soon, the proceeds from her 'profession' were no longer enough to
keep body and soul together. That ravenous passion for money once
again took the better part of her and it was later to destroy her
destiny in a subtle but debilitating way. She alone knows how it all
started for she was purposely silent about it. Ekene one of her
boyfriends won her heart with money and quality of gifts he lavished
on her. Even though she knew he was an armed robber, she dared to
stick by him. He kept his armory with her and would return to her
warm embrace after each operation. She also nursed any wounds he
sustained while "on duty." Ekene did not last long. He was set ablaze
by an irate mob who caught him in the act. She went to see his
charred body. Onyinye was devastated but life must go on.
Love, they say, is blind but Onyinye's was insane. Putting her
lover's inglorious demise behind her, she was back to the old rot.
This time around, it was with two Emekas, one is at large while the
second is indetention at the Lagos State special Anti Robbery Squad,
(SARS). Again, she was their armorer, their nurse and bed mate. She
was the life line of the gang who helped keep them together a case of
an extra notorious woman behind notorious robbers. Onyinye was
heavily paid for her services.
But police sources told this magazine that beyond all these, Onyinye
partook in some robbery escapades. They were so sure she was the lady
in the gang of robbers that on Wednesday, 20 February, robbed in
different parts of Lagos and killed eight policemen. Most of the
fallen policemen's service guns were later found in her apartment.
Onyiye's story is that of a prodigal daughter who never found a
reason for living until the bubble burst. Her cousin, Blessing,
attested to the fact that Onyiye was like the proverbial obstinate
fly that follows the corpse to the grave: "Everybody in our family
has quarrelled with her to stop the bad life but she did not listen.
We begged her to come home and we always warned her... But she did
not listen. In fact, when her first boyfriend died, we all called her
and advised her to go and learn a job and stop all these rubbish. She
got us into this mess now," a very bitter Blessing said out.
Even when she was forced to learn tailoring, Onyinye's concentration
was elsewhere. She therefore spent little or no time at her shop
while she camped in hotels with Emeka, her robber husband whom she
claimed charmed her so she would not marry anybody else.
When she learnt that the police were cracking down on robbers in
Lagos (in fact, a few of her friends were already in the police net,)
she ran for dear life to her village, Isiala Mbanu, in Imo State,
abandoning all her guns in her sister's house. Men of the Lagos State
Anti Robbery Squad (SARS), however traced her to that rustic village
and there, she was arrested and brought back to Lagos. Her
confessions helped the police in cracking down some members of the
gang but her boyfriend and leader of the gang, Emeka is still at
large with a broken thigh bone. Onyinye told this magazine that she
was nursing the tigh before her arrest.
As painful as her arrest is to her however, Onyiye's headache and
worry now is finding a man to marry her. "No man will marry me
because I have soiled my image".
In this interview with Lara Owoeye - Wise & Richard Elesho, Onyiye
tells the story of her life.
The Story Of My Life
- Onyinye
Q: Who are you?
A: My name is Onyinye Abosi. I am from Isiala Mbanu, Imo State.
I am twenty one years old. I came here because I was arrested about a
bag Emeka gave me to keep. I kept the bag and travelled.
Q: What was in the bag?
A: Guns, but I didn't know so I travelled. I was not at home when
they came. I did not check the bag. I just kept it, because Emeka
told me he wanted to travel. So I just collected it and kept it. Then
when I got to the village in Mbanu, Imo State the police came to
arrest me, My mother then decided to come down to Lagos.
Q: Why did Emeka give you the bag. Are you his girlfriend?
A: The Emeka police arrested is not my boyfriend. He had a shop
beside us, so we were just like brothers. I have known him for about
eight years now, but that was the first time I would keep gun for
him. Allah. Give me something to swear. I am telling you the truth.
Please help me.
Q: If you want us to help you, then tell us the whole truth about
your life and your contact with the robbers?
A: Okay, I want to tell you the truth now. One day as I was walking
on the street, Emeka came to meet me. He said he wanted me to do
something for him. I asked him what. He said I should agree I will do
it before he make the request. I refused. But he pressed on and
before God and man Emeka come give me N5,000. I told him I would not
want his girl friend to accuse me of man snatching, but I collected
the money. I was learning fashion design at Orile. So one day Emeka
brought one bag and asked me to keep it for him. When I came back
from work, I saw the bag but I did not open it. He kept the bag
straight with another one underneath. The bag was there before I
travelled but I did not know what was in it.
Q: What did he ask you to use the N5,000 for?
A: He did not tell me. He just gave me and I collected it. He used to
give me money before like N200 or N500. He was not my boy friend. I
don't know his work, but he used to stay in his shop. I have known
him for a long time. Even before I started living in the Hotel and
even when I was in the primary school at Orile. I also attended
Gaskiya High School at Ijora before I dropped out in JSS III, to live
in the hotel.
Q: What were you doing in the Hotel?
A: I dey hustle
Q: How much were you making?
A: I was making like N2,5000, N3000. But if I do all night, I make
like N5000 per day. I don't drink beer or take drugs. But I am very
stubborn and I drink malt. Allah. I don't tell lies. Please help me.
Q: But you are telling lies now. Tell us everything about the armed
robbers that you know, if you want help
A: I will tell you the truth now. There are two Emekas that I know.
Emeka the shop owner who is now with the police and Emeka chemist
that gave me bag, the other Emeka my boyfriend. I knew him through
the chemist. So one day I saw Emeka chemist, he was treating one boy
called Ichor, he said he had an accident and sustained wounds in the
hand. I did not believe him. Which kind accident way go make person
hand wan fall?
He said he was his friend. Later Emeka my boyfriend started coming to
our house and we started going to the hotel together. We dey do till
day break
Small time all of us started meeting in Mushin. Sometimes they will
carry me with Okada to the Hotel Remembrance or to Mushin.
One day, Ichor, that boy that had wound came to give me a gun to
hold. I come ask am say this thing wey you give me now wetin, I go
take am do? But he said him brother wey be policeman no give am
money, so e come seize him gun. So I told Emeka about it. He said I
should collect and keep it that his brother is truly a policeman.
The next day, Emeka came to collect key from my hand to collect the
gun. That time they have not brought the bag o. So he later has his
own key and he would come and collect the gun at anytime from my
house. I saw one v-boot car with them, but they said it is the boy
that own it. That night, they carried the car away.
When they came back they talked about Oyingbo operation. But for
about four days I did not see my boyfriend.
One day he sent one of them to come and call me that I should come to
Mushin. When I got there, they were sharing money inside the house. I
asked Emeka where they got the money. E say na money wey in boy sell
for market.
So Emeka chemist then put me on the machine, took me to the hotel and
lodged me. Later my own Emeka came to meet me in the hotel and we
played and slept. He gave me N3,000 that day before I went to where I
was learning fashion designing.
Q: Is Emeka the only armed robber - boy friend that you have?
A: No
Q: Tell me about the others
A: The name of my first boyfriend is Ekene. But he is dead now
Q: What killed him?
A: They said they killed and burnt him. They said he went to rob and
they killed him. I will tell you the truth. One day in Mile 2, I was
walking with Ekene when a boy harassed him and called him a pick-
pocket. They fought seriously and I ran away. Ekene came to me after
to say the boy was telling lies.
Q: How many times have you gone for robbery?
A: I never rob before
Q: When you follow armed robbers to Mushin what do you people talk
about?
A: They talk about many things. One day Emeka told me that they have
killed one policeman called Owolabi, but I later saw Owolabi on the
road, they did not kill him. Allah. But they always talk about
policemen. When they come back from operation. I usually don't stay
with them, but they always carry me for lodging
Q: Why did you travel?
A: There is one hotel in my area called Joy Centre. One day people
told me that the police had arrested Ngozi, that they went to rob and
the owner of the hotel gave them off, they told me she was looking
for us up and down. So I went to look for my friends Obi that she
should go since they had arrested Ngozi. I don't know the people that
robbed, but Ngozi is not Ichor's girl friend, She is the girl friend
of stubborn Naughty
Q: Who is he?
A: Na armed robber. It was Chikito that told me how they hold Ngozi,
so I come travel
Q: What of Emeka your boyfriend, what is his father's name and where
is he now?
A: I don't know his father's name and I don't know where he is now.
Before he sustained injury in his leg from accident, he wasn't going
out. There is x-ray. It is not gunshot. He told me it was accident
that broke the bones into two.
Q: Did you not know your boy friend was an armed robber?
A: I did not know
Q; And you were taking care of his leg in the hotel for two weeks?
A: No it was his house, that my self and his younger brother were
taking care of him. But his brother knows he was an armed robber.
Please help me. I want to say something.
One day, Emeka carried me to one Juju man to take oath. Even when one
man wanted to marry me, he did not allow me to leave. The juju man
put mark on my breasts e com say make me and Emeka fuck, and we fuck
finish, e come put hand for my private part comot something come say
if I leave Emeka go marry another man, I go die. The man then gave me
water to drink. That is why since then I no fit leave Emeka.
Q: How can you now convince us you have never robbed before, with all
these things?
A: I never rob, I just dey stubborn, my father is dead. My mother
roasts yam for sale, they asked me to go to school but I refused and
went to live in the hotels. I cannot explain why I behaved like that.
No be say Emeka dey fuck me well I no fit say na in prick make me
follow Emeka. I have lived im hotels before, so it is not because of
his penis at all. I don't know why I followed him. No be say na fine
person, e tall, e dey like man sha. I beg.
Q: Do you go to church?
A: Yes. I am an Anglican, I don do confirm self - my church is in
Amukoko. As it is now, no man will like to marry me and I want to
have a good husband. May God forgive me and give me a bright person.
The juju in my body has failed since I left him and travelled. Other
girls must learn from my case. They must ask their boyfriends the
source of their income, and background. I have made a mistake please
help me beg my sister and family. I want to go and learn my work
well, but I am afraid my past might not allow me have a good husband.
Friends brought me to this. Please help me beg my family. I wan marry
a good man.
She Lives A Bad Life
- Blessing Abosi
What is your name?
A: My name is Blessing
Q: Why are you here?
A; I am here because one early morning policemen came and asked of my
sister with one boy. That time my sister was not around and I told
them. Then they were searching my house. As they were searching, they
saw gun under the old big box in the house. They searched the shop
and found a gun too. So the police arrested me and my little sister.
I was beaten and tortured. They asked me how I got the gun and the
whereabouts of my sister. I told them I didn't know anything about
the gun.
As for my sister, I told them she had gone to the village.
They asked me to take them to the village. When we travelled to the
village, by the Grace of God, we saw my sister, they arrested her.
And on our way to Lagos, they asked her certain questions to which
she answered.
I did not know there was a gun in my room. She kept it there. I am a
market seller and every morning I go to the market to sell my wares
with that my small sister, and around ten we return home. I am not a
thief.
Q: How did you know Onyinye
A: She is my cousin
Q: What does she do?
A: She does anything
Q: Anything like what?
A: Yes, because she no dey do anything. All I know is that she had
lived in a hotel before, everybody in our family was quarrelling with
her to stop the bad life, but she did not listen. We begged her to
come home, but she refused.
We always warned her. Even my younger sister always quarrel with her
to stop, now she has brought us to this mess.
For two weeks before she travelled, she was not staying at home. I
went to the market and when I came back, they told me she had dropped
the key and travelled.
She knew Emeka was an armed robber. All of us knew he is an armed
robber. She is telling lies. Emeka is one boy that all of us have
being telling her to leave because of his rough life. I knew she was
following an armed robber. We have all talked to her but she did not
listen. Infact, when her first boy friend died, we all called her and
advised her to go and learn a job and stop all this rubbish she was
doing. Initially we thought it was because of adolescent problem, but
now we know it is not, because she is old enough to differentiate
good from bad.

Headline: Nigeria: The North As the Issue
Body_Text:
"The truth is that the north decides who becomes Nigeria leader whether military or civilian."
The political foundation of this country was laid on a tripod of the geographical expression grouped into north, west and east. Expectedly the political growth of the country also went along the geo-political equation. Although it must be noted that the growth along this line was facilitated by the existence of the three main ethnic groups in the country, the Hausa Fulani in the north, the Yoruba in the west and the Igbo in the East.
It is perhaps not by accident that the early political fathers of the nation emerged from within the three main ethnic groups. The late Owelle of Onitsha Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe, an Igbo who became the first president of Nigeria at the nation's independence in 1960, the Sardauna of Sokoto, Sir Ahmadu Bello, Premier of the defunct northern region whose nominee, Alhaji Abubakar Tafawa Belewa became the first Prime Minister of the country at independence and of course, the legend of Yoruba politics, Chief Obafemi Awolowo who was the Premier of the west.
Since Nigeria independence 42 years ago, the socio-political economic developments of the three geo-political areas have continued to be influenced by the ideals of these great leaders.
Even the delineation of the country into states which started in late 60s to douse the rising regional and ethnic sentiments, could not wipe out the influence of these men. Not even the prolonged military presence in our polity could ignore the demagoguery of these political personalities.
But of the three geo-political zones, the north overtime demonstrated and still demonstrates better knowledge of political environment called Nigeria. As a result, the struggle for power whether civil or military has always ended in their favour not necessarily because of their size and population which is reasonably larger than the other two but more on their ability to manage and co-ordinate themselves to survive in a plural environment.
Late premier of the northern region Sir Bello with his endowed vision successfully laid the foundation that saw the north emerge the way it is today. Over time the geo-political north continues to establish that they hold both the yam and the knife in our polity. When late Major General Aguiyi Ironsi as the military head of state in the 60s came up with his naive political idea, the geo-political north were angry moreso when it was largely believed in the north that the coup that brought him (Ironsi) was sponsored by the Igbo. It was easy for them to push him away after six months to install their own son Yakubu Gowon who reigned for nine good years.
The next time a non-northerner came close to political power was by accident just as was the case with General Ironsi. This time General Obasanjo as the second in command to late general Murtala Mohammed who died by an aborted February 13, 1976 coup was "allowed" to climb the ladder to be the Head of State, on the condition that he hand over power soonest. He survived by doing just that handing over to a northerner, Alhaji Shehu Shagari in 1979 amidst celebrated protest by his kinsmen.
Since then the north held on to power rotating it among themselves from Shagari to Buhari through Babangida to Abacha and Abubakar before returning it to the same Obasanjo. It was obvious that in 1999 Obasanjo came to power simply because the geo-political north wanted him. His geo-political area - the South-West did not want him and they stressed their dislike through their mass vote for their choice Chief Olu Falae even when they knew that Obasanjo was going to win.
It is an obvious thing that although the East voted massively for Obasanjo in 1999, he did not win because they wanted him. Even if his South-West people had wanted him, he would not have been president without the north. The truth is that the north decides who becomes Nigeria leader whether military or civilian. If the political climate in the country seems hot now, it is because Obasanjo and his friends are rightly or wrongly beginning to think that they can do with the north.
This might seem dangerous in reality because no serious effort was made by him to create a matching South, no effort was made to court the friendship of the East and the West, the other tripods that could have matched the north. If the north seems powerful overtime, blame it on the Southerners selfish attitude and northerners strong belief in the ideals of late Ahmadu Bello of building a geo-political area that believes in unity in diversity.

Original_Search_Title: Kwara Awards N237m Kaiama Road Contract
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Awards N237m Kaiama Road Contract
Body_Text:
Ilorin — The Kwara State government has approved the award of contract for the construction of a 124-kilomentre road to open up the Kaiama and Baruten Local Government areas and link them properly with other parts of the state. The approval, given by the State Executive Council last week, is in fulfillment of the promise of the State Executive Governor, Alhaji Mohammed Lawal to develop the area which has remained neglected by previous administrations in the state.
A statement signed by the Commissioner for Infor-mation Mr. Wale Oladepo said the road construction which will be in phases, was awarded to Habibu Engineering (Nigeria) Limited at a total sum of N237,090,200. To ensure speedy completion of the road, approval was also given for immediate release of 30 per cent of the contract sum.
The road construction is divided into two sections. The first section which covers 98.5 kilometres will take the road from Kaiama to Adogo while the second section will cover a 25.5 km stretch of difficult terrain from Adogo to Bode-Saadu in the Moro Local Government area of the state where the road will link up with the North-south Highway.
The Bode-Saadu-Adogo section involves the blasting of rocks for allignments and construction of several culverts on streams and rivers.
The scope of work in the first phase involves clearing of bush to allow a total average width of 15 metres and laterite filling as carriage way to sub-grade level.
Baruten Local Government shares Nigeria's border with Benin Republic. The two local government areas are a major food basket of the state with the production of yam, yam flour, gari and rice. Transportation of the huge stockof food stuff has always been through Kishi, a Northern town of Oyo State.
Speaking at the Executive Council meeting, Governor Lawal said it has been a burden in his heart to break the jinx of under-development of the area which all previous governors had shied away from. He said it will be a fulfillment of his electoral promise to the people of the areas and a furtherance of his desire to carry all parts of the state along in his government's programme.
