Governor Lawal said he is undaunted by the fact that revenue allocation to the state from the Federation Allocation has dwindled following the crash in oil price in the world market following the September 11 attack on the USA.
Expressing appreciation to the state government on behalf of the people, secretary to the State Government Alhaji Umar Abubakar, and the Commissioner for Women Affairs Mrs. Dorcas Gunu Afeniforo both from the area, thanked the state Governor for taking the laudable step which will further boost the popularity of the government among the Baruten and Kaiama people.

Original_Search_Title: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Headline: Nigeria: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Body_Text:
Ado Ekiti — A gubernatorial aspirant of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Ekiti State, Mr. Ayo Fayose, has called on the state government to waive stringent taxes that could discourage investors from the state.
Speaking at the commissioning of an ultra modern Eatery Centre called De-Prince & Princess in Ado-Ekiti over the weekend, Mr. Fayose expressed displeasure over the low level of commercial activities in the state.
He commended the initiator of the new business outfit, Chief Christopher Uzomah, who is a native of Enugu State, while urging the administration in Ekiti to consider appointing an Ibo man as commissioner for Commerce to boost commercial activities in the state.
In his welcome address at the occasion, the initiator of the outfit, Chief Uzomah, who is the chairman and chief executive of Uzotraco Nigeria Limited based in Akure, Ondo State appealed to the people of Ekiti to see the new eatery centre as part of the efforts to develop the state.
Also the Managing Director of the new outfit, Mr. John Orji described the eatery as an idea conceived out of the desire to bring such quality services to Ekiti State, as the outfit's products are prepared under the most hygienic conditions.
Speaking during an interview, a legal practitioner in Ado-Ekiti, Mr. Ray Anyanwun, described the establishment of the eatery as a welcome development, saying it was another opportunity to break away from the traditional and conservative culture of eating pounded yam every afternoon as launch by the people of Ekiti State.
The legal practitioner commended the People of Ekiti for allowing people of other ethnic groups in the country to contribute to the development of the young state without any form of discrimination.

Original_Search_Title: Hausa Community in Rivers Begs Odili for Mosque
Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Community in Rivers Begs Odili for Mosque
Body_Text:
Members of the Hausa community at Slaughter, Trans Amadi Road, Port Harcourt, have called on the Rivers State governor, Dr Peter Odili, to come to their rescue by ensuring that the authority of Obi/Akpon local government area allow them continue with the building of their mosque.
Their appeal was made over the weekend during an interview session with Weekly Trust.
The chairman of the Butchers' Union Association at the main Slaughter, Trans Amadi, Alhaji Ibrahim Maisundu stated that the mosque committee members decided last year to improve on the temporary structure of the mosque which has been on ground since 1979.
"They found the relevant papers and approached the Obi/Akpon local government authorities who gave them approval to embark on the project," he said.
The chairman lamented that after the approval, the building of the mosque project began on July 22, 2001 and his team was asked to stop work one month later by some local government officials.
He said a delegation from the Hausa community went to the local government to find out what prompted the order and they were referred to members of the Oginiba community who refused to grant them audience.
According to him, the delegation went back to the local government chairman to give him feedback and he informed them that the matter was above his power.
Alhaji Maisundu said the Hausa community tried again and this time discussed with some members of the Oginiba community who asked them to bring yams, drinks and a specified amount of money.
His words: "We promised giving them money and after delivering our promise, the community members refused to set a date for a meeting till now."
Maisundu appealed to every Muslim, especially the supreme leader of Muslims in Nigeria to come to their aid.
The patron of the Northern community in Rivers State and chairman of the central mosque committee, Alhaji Uba Sule said the same problem was also affecting the central mosque at another road in Port Harcourt.
He said since the land was acquired honestly, they should be allowed to go on with their building projects.
Alhaji Sule called on the governor of Rivers State, Dr Peter Odili to come to their rescue because since they have been living in the state for the past 30 years, they have been law-abiding and living in harmony with their hosts.

Original_Search_Title: House: Minister Quarters Contract Inflated By N1.4 Bn
Headline: Nigeria: House: Minister Quarters Contract Inflated By N1.4 Bn
Body_Text:
Abuja — Bars Obasanjo's officer from N'Assembly
To examine el-Rufai for contempt
Nigeria paid the sum of N1.4 billion in excess for the acquisition and furnishing of a total of 63 houses for members of the Federal Executive Council and Special Advisers to the President in 1999.
This was the submission of the Ad hoc committee raised by the House of Representatives to investigate the purchase of the 63 houses by the Federal Capital Development Authority (FCDA) yesterday.
This revelation appears to be the first fruit of the resolution of the House three weeks ago to conduct a wide-ranging probe of the income and expenditure of the executive arm of government.
The ad hoc panel submitted the report on the day the House formally declared the Presidential Liaison Officer (PLO), Dr. (Mrs.) Esther Oduehi, a persona non-grata at the National Assembly complex.
While submitting its report to the plenary session of the House, the committee noted that the N3 billion spent on the acquisition and furnishing of the 63 houses was withdrawn from the Federation Account without due approval by the lawmakers.
Said the committee chairman, Hon. Iheanacho Obioma: "From the evidence of the Minister of Finance and Minister of the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), the 63 houses were bought and furnished at a cost of N3,019,153,178.06 instead of N2.74 billion approved by the president on September 10, 1999."
Obioma said that based on the valuation report of two estate agents, the purchase prices and furnishing costs of the houses were grossly inflated by N878.3 million and N552.3 million respectively.
He said that the committee also established that there was no budgetary provisions for the purchase of the houses in the 1999 and 2000 Appropriation Acts as well as in the supplementary appropriation passed by the National Assembly.
Obioma said that request for the purchase and furnishing of the houses was made to Obasanjo on September 2, 1999 while the supplementary bill was passed on Sept. 8, 1999 and contained no provision for the houses.
"It was also established that the furnishing was done arbitrarily ranging from N3.2 million to N12.1 million per house without regard to the relevant public and civil service regulations," he said.
He said that there was gross violation of the 1999 constitution, the Independent Corrupt Practices and other related offences Act and the Finance (Control and Management) Act.
Obioma said that the committee recommended that the president be cautioned to desist from further violations of the constitution and to immediately submit a supplementary bill to cover the purchase of the houses.
He said that the House should urge Obasanjo to set up a judicial commission of enquiry to probe the purchase and furnishing of the houses with a view to recovering the inflated amount and bring the culprits to book.
The raging war of nerves between the House and the Presidency assumed another dimension yesterday as the House barred Dr. Uduehi from entering its chambers or coming near the precinct of the National Assembly, warning that she risks arrest by the Sergeant-at-Arms.
Also, the House directed the Committee on Ethics and Privileges to investigate alleged violation of legislative privileges of the House by the Director General of the Bureau for Public Enterprise (BPE), Malam Nasir el-Rufai.
The PLO on Monday had accused the lawmakers of "insulting" and "blackmailing" the president over his insistence on accountability and transparency in governance.
The members, while deliberating on what they considered scathing comments on the House by the PLO, called on the Sergeant-at-Arms to the House and other security agents attached to the House to arrest Dr. Uduehi if she ventures near the precinct of the National Assembly or enters the chambers of the House of Representatives.
Deputy chairman of the House Committee on Justice, Human Rights and Legal Matters, Celestine Ughanze, who briefed the press after the plenary session, said categorically that nobody is entitled "to come into the chambers of the two houses of the National Assembly unless granted the privilege to enter."
He quoted sections 14 and 18 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act CAP 208 of 1990 of the Laws of the Federation of Nigeria to support the ban on Uduehi. He explained that already the House had in a letter to President Obasanjo requested for the withdrawal of Uduehi as the PLO to the House.
Similarly, the House passed a motion directing its Committee on Ethics and Privileges to commence investigation on the alleged breach of legislative privileges by Malam el- Rufai.
According to the motion sponsored by John Agoda and 14 others, the House said the address by el-Rufai to the Ad-hoc Committee investigating the sale of NITEL was a "defamatory statement made and published by Mallam Nasir el-Rufai against the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Hon. Ghali Na'Abba, Hon. Nduka Irabor and the entire House of Representatives."
The motion read thus:
The House of Representatives set up an Ad-hoc Committee to investigate the privatization of NITEL;
The Bureau of Public Enterprises of which Mallam Nasir Ahmed El-Rufai is Director General, being a key player in the privatization of NITEL, was invited to answer questions;
The Director-General presented a paper dated March 5, 2002 (which became widely circulated) wherein he, instead of answering questions put to him, displayed utter disrespect, scorn and lack of confidence in the House of Representatives to inquire into the privatization process of NITEL (especially as regards the roles of the Bureau of Public Enterprises).
Agoda states amongst others thus:
The Punch Newspaper of Wednesday, March 6, 2002 on page 8.
"He discounted the opposition of the House of Representatives on the privatization programme, saying that the credibility of the legislators within the country and outside the shores of Nigeria is not the best and highest in the world."
On the paper he presented, he said amongst others:
"For all the reasons above, we want to make it abundantly clear that we do not have much confidence in the outcome of these proceedings. We however have respect for our democracy and its fledging institutions. We are therefore participating in this forum out of a constitutional responsibility, and respect for the House and the integrity of some of its members. We do not in all honesty expect a fair hearing."
"We understand the context in which this investigation is being conducted. We will not allow the privatization process to be held hostage to the personal interests of a few individuals. We realize that there is a lot at stake here, and there is a concerted attempt to stall the process." See pages 4 of the paper.
"Fundamentally, the Honourable Speaker's statement calls into question the whole basis of this investigation for if indeed the House has decided this to be the case, then it is quite clear that we are all wasting our time here as we would merely be props in the conclusion of a pre-determined outcome. It is difficult to conceive a situation where the Speaker having ruled on an issue, the Committee will come out with findings that are contrary to his views. In that case, we would merely be participating in a charade." See page 4 of paper.
"It is however worth mentioning that it is impossible to expect a fair outcome when the accuser is also the chairman of the committee that will establish the veracity or otherwise of the claims. Even in a village square setting, a man accused of stealing yams is not judged by his accusers but by a neutral party. No less a standard should be applicable to the House of Representatives of the Federal Republic, one cannot be a prosecutor, judge and jury".
The statements reproduced above are in their ordinary meaning contemptuous of the House of Representatives, and in violation of Section 24 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act, Cap. 208, Laws of the Federation of Nigeria, 1990.
WHEREAS this Honourable House resolve as follows:
That the House Committee on Ethics and Privileges by virtue of Order 16 of the House Rules be and is hereby directed to inquire into and report on the extent of Mallam Nasir Ahmed El-Rufai's violation, if any, of the legislative privileges of this Honourable House.
Recommend appropriate measures the Committee deem fit.
In his comment supporting the motion, sponsor of the motion, Agoda quoted section 24 of the legislative Houses (powers and Privileges) act CAP 208 1990 of the laws of the Federation of Nigeria which reads " any person who (a) publishes any statement, whether in writing or otherwise, which falsely or scandalously defames a legislative house or any committee there of ; or (b) publishes any writing reflecting on the character of the President or Speaker, as the case may be, of a legislative house, or chairman of a committee in the conduct of his duty as such President, Speaker or Chairman or (c)publishes any writing containing a gross, wilful or scandalous misrepresentation of the proceedings of a legislative house or of any member in the proceedings of a legislative house, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine of two hundred naira or to imprisonment for twelve months, or to both such fine and imprisonment."
Agoda, who described the utterances of el-Rufai as contemptuous, called on the members to guide the institution of the House jealously as such utterances from the Director General of the BPE was, he said, capable of bringing the integrity of the House to ridicule.
He was supported by the Chairman of the House Committee on Petroleum, West Idahosa, who said that el-Rufai went too far by describing the investigation that was authorised by a resolution of the House as a charade.
He said that it was not only an attempt to deride the House and its resolution, but also an attempt to incite the people against the members of the House.
Hon. Ibrahim Ganyama informed the House of how a Director of NITEL, Mr. Tunji Bello, allegedly insulted another member, Hon. Temi Harriman in the course of her duty.
Thus, the House directed its Committee on Ethics and Privileges to investigate the utterances of el-Rufai and the recalcitrant posture of Mr. Bello, and recommend appropriate actions and measures to be taken against the officers within two weeks.
Uduehi had on Monday at a press conference in Abuja attributed the decision of the House to investigate Obasanjo to selfish motives.
"The President is not being persecuted and insulted by the leadership and some members of the House for non-performance, but for his high level of accountability and transparency in government business which cannot be negotiated no matter the opposition. The genesis of the crisis can be traced to Mr. President's desire to know the wage bill of members of the House," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Blast Victims Turn Alausa to Relief Camp
Headline: Nigeria: Blast Victims Turn Alausa to Relief Camp
Body_Text:
Lagos — Following the distribution of relief materials by the Lagos State
Government to victims of the January 27 bomb blasts, more victims of
the disaster have continued to invade the Lagos State Secretariat
daily.
The victims who have almost turned the secretariat premises to
another relief camp daily converged at the secretariat seeking the
state government attention.
Some of the victims who spoke with P.M.News stated that they decided
to come to the secretariat to collect food items and other relief
materials since what they are getting from the relief camps are not
sufficient for them.
Some of them, mostly women, were seen by P.M.News been attended to by
officials of the ministry of special duties who distributed one bag
of rice, 1 bag of gari, two tubers of yam, two bags of semovita and a
loaf of bread to the victims.
Mrs. Josephine Osakwe of CBQ Flat 104 Ikeja Military Cantonment told
P.M.News that the assistance of the state government is still needed
to help the family get back to normal life.
While commending the Lagos State government for its efforts, Mrs.
Joke Fagbemi solicited the assistance of the state government in
re-building their damaged houses.
The victims, mostly from the Ikeja and Ipaja barracks were full of
praises for the state government for its concern and sensitivity to
their plights.

Original_Search_Title: Syrian Community Donates N10 Million To Bomb Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Syrian Community Donates N10 Million To Bomb Victims
Body_Text:
The Syrian Community in Nigeria has donated relief materials worth N10 million to the victims of the January 27 bomb explosions in Lagos.
The material comprised bags of rice, yams, matresses, bags of beans, sugar, flour, biscuits and milk.
The material were delivered to the Lagos State Government representatives at the Police college in Ikeja.
Leader of the delegation who is also the Honarary Consul of Syrian Arab Republic in Lagos, Mr. Ziad Saimua, said the community was ready to do more if the situation arises.
"The Syrian community has stayed over 100 years in Nigeria and we felt that anything that happens to Nigerians, happens to us. Nigeria is our second home," Saimua said.
He advises that the government should ensure that all the victims have a fair share of the relief materials being donated by every concerned individuals, institutions or groups.
"We hope that the relief materials would help alleviate the sufferings of the victims," he added.

Headline: Nigeria: The Prostitutes of Ijora Badia
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ijora Badia, Lagos' notorious slum, has taken a dangerous plunge. Sex
trade thrives there at an alarming rate, leading to fear of widespread of
AIDS. There is also fear of imminent outbreak of epidemic. ANTHONY OKORO who
visited the place, reports.
It has a semblance of the defunct Maroko, Lagos most notorious slum. It
also has a striking similarity with the notorious Kaduna 'Yam' market where
no single yam is sold, except a high level ignominious sex business. It is,
in fact the exact opposite of a modern day abode.
Living in Ijora Badia, a swampy area of Apapa, Lagos, is a nightmare.
But then, it is a haven for maximum enjoyment and relaxation. One peculiar
feature of Ijora Badia is the mountains of refuse that 'decorate' the entire
area, constantly sending out offensive odour.
There is hardly any street in the area without heaps of refuse, the most
notorious being Fadaini Street. Nearly half of the entire street is blocked
by mountains of refuse, thereby obstructing both human and vehicular
traffic. Metrolink gathered that the street was tarred over 30 years ago and
is now full of dangerous potholes.
Apart from this, the drainage network in the entire area has completely
broken down. The gutters are full of all sorts of dirty things and stinking
all the time.
Similarly, all the houses in the area constitute an eyesore.  Metrolink
findings revealed that about 85 per cent of the houses at Ijora Badia are
built with planks. No wonder wood business booms in the area.
In areas like Akorede, Amusu, Oniyide streets, all the living rooms and
business premises are built with planks.
The situation is even pathetic at Amuda Lane where all the Pako houses
are built on swampy soil. When Metrolink visited the area, hundreds of water
pipes were seen lying on the muddy ground. Some have broken many years
ago. In all the streets, people have to meander through the stinking refuse
and muddy ground before they eventually get to their destinations.
The state of the roads in Ijora Badia is very deplorable. It is only
Obale Road which links the slum with Orile that is a bit motorable.
For residents of Ijora Badia, it is a taboo to provide toilet facilities
in houses. People just defecate into nylon bags and throw them into the
swamp. Some even make holes in their huts and defecate directly into the
swamp. One worrisome issue is that the swamp is stagnant. So, apart from the
refuse, stench also emanates from the swamp. The fear among the people is
that one day, an epidemic of immense magnitude may soon occur there.
However, the people of the area survive by the grace of God. This
explains why churches abound there, some with names that are
jaw-breakers.
Despite this, life at Ijora Badia is at its peak. The Ajeromi railway
line is the hub of activities. It is often described as the life-wire of
Ijora Badia. Both day and night, the railway line is always a beehive of
activities. Different categories of people, rich and poor, old and small,
throng there daily to have a taste of life.
The Ajeromi railway line is notorious for illicit sex trade.  Metrolink
investigations revealed that the line alone has over 200 brothels. No other
street or area in the whole of Lagos state has such a large number of
prostitutes homes.
Of all the brothels, only two have signposts. They are Chico Star Light
Hotel and Titilayo Hotel, situated at 55 and 52 Ajeromi Railway Line
respectively.
Others simply have street numbers to identify them. Such brothels are
located at 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 30, 42,
45, 55, 80, 86, 87, 90, 92, 94, 98, 197 Ajeromi Railway Line. And the list
is endless. The way and manner the brothels are so clustered indicates the
degree of competitiveness in the sex business here.  Apart from three, all
the brothels are built with planks. In some brothels, there are over 200
prostitutes while the smallest of them have about 10 call girls. Built very
close to the brothels are cinema houses. Statistics showed that there are
over 50 of such cinema houses, all built with planks. Some popular ones are
Love Film Centre and Base One. Operators of the cinema houses don't show any
other film, except pornographic films. They operate both day and night with
impunity. Outside the cinema houses are pornographic materials which are
well displayed to attract customers. Metrolink observed that some youths
after watching the blue films, become sexually stimulated and storm the
brothels to satisfy themselves.
The sex workers openly solicit for men. The sexual escapades at Ijora
Badia has reached such a crescendo that an elderly man who was just passing
by the Ajeromi Railway line murmured to himself: "The population in hell
fire would be great because I know that none of these people can smell
heaven".
At Ijora Badia, the dreaded Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS)
is a non issue. The only language they understand is enjoyment. This
explains why they go about their businesses without using condom.
"Condom or no condom, person must die when it is time. Na money I dey
find", a sex worker told Metrolink.
As expected, crime rate is high at Ijora Badia. There are numerous smoke
shacks along the railway line and these joints are highly patronized by
miscreants.
The situation was worsened in that for long, the place had been without
a police station.
However, a new police station has just been built along Fadaini Street
and is now waiting for commissioning. But would the police be able to
contain the excesses of dwellers of Ijora Badia?

Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Leader Disappears With N150, 000
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu's efforts to alleviate the sufferings of
displaced victims of the 27 January bomb explosions at the Ikeja
Police College relief camp have been thwarted by the Seriki Hausawa
of Mushin, Babalosha, Alhaji Sani Lambo, who has reportedly
disappeared with N150,000 given the victims by the Lagos governor.
Governor Tinubu, who visited the camp last Friday shortly after the
Ju'ma'at service was said to have given the victims (about 20
families) N150,000 to share.
According to one of the displaced soldiers, Corporal Hassan, the
money was given to them so that they could travel to their states to
celebrate the Eid-El Kabir.
The money, P.M.News gathered, was given to Alhaji Lambo to share but
no sooner did he collect the money than he disappeared from the camp.
The displaced soldiers from the Ikeja Military Cantonment, who said
they have been looking for him since Friday vowed to do everything
possible to fish him out.
"You see, some of us have packed our loads, we were ready to travel
to our states to celebrate the Sallah, but there is no money. The
Seriki has disappeared with our money. This is wickedness. The
government should look for him and collect the money from him",
Hassan stated agitatedly.
P.M.News further gathered that Governor Tinubu specifically told the
victims to use the money to celebrate the Sallah holidays and that
those whose families are not in Lagos should use the money to travel
home to see their loved ones.
Apart from the N150,000 donated, the governor also gave each of the
families a bag of rice, semovita, carton of large size Bournvita,
tubers of yam, cartons of macaroni, groundnut oil, garri and other
foodstuffs.
Governor Tinubu told them that despite the fact that they are
soldiers and employed by the federal government, the fact that they
reside in Lagos which he governs makes it incumbent on him to take
care of their welfare. He urged them to be patient as the government
is making efforts to provide them temporary accommodation.
P.M.News observed that houses made of roofing sheets are being built
at the camp to accommodate the victims.

Original_Search_Title: Nasfat, Eib, Syrian Community Donate to Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Nasfat, Eib, Syrian Community Donate to Victims
Body_Text:
More donations are still coming in for the victims of the Ikeja military Cantonment Explosion of January 27 2002. Eko International Bank plc (EIB) donated the sum of N2 million naira to the Lagos state Disaster relief Fund to assist the state government minimise the hardship of the victims of the bomb explosion. presenting a cheque to the governor of Lagos state, Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu during a condolence visit at Alausa, the Chairman Dr. Akinola Ogunlewe, together with some board of Directors of the bank expressed deep concern for the plight of the victims. Ogulew also said the humanitarian gesture was informed by EIB's commitment to social responsibility.
He said, "EIB as socially responsible corporate citizen has come to demonstrate support for the laudable humanitarian gesture being accorded victims of this unmitigated disaster through a modest donation of N2 million." The chairman commended Governor Tinubu's exemplary leadership qualities which he said has ensured a steady flow of relief for the victims.
The governor also received one million naira cheque from members of the Nasrul-lahi-il-fathi society of Nigeria NAFSAT. The President of the association in his presentation stated that the amount is a token contribution of its members towards resettlement of the displaced persons as a result of the bomb explosion and the recent clashes at Idi-araba. The president also used the opportunity to pray for the repose of the soul of those who died in the two incidents.
Also the honorary consul of Syrian-Arab Republic in Lagos Mr. Zaid Saimua together with some of his consuls also made some donations at the Lagos State Government house. The presentations of relief materials which they said was valued at N10million naira, comprised of; rice, yams, beans, sugar flour, biscuits milk and mattress. Mr Saimua later advised that the government representative endeavour that all the victims have a fair share of the materials Saimua also stated that "the Syrian community has stayed over 100 years in Nigeria and we feel that anything that happens to Nigeria happens to us Nigeria is our second home, we hope that the relief materials will help alleviate the suffering of the victims."

Original_Search_Title: We'll Process Your Claims With FG Free of Charge - Tinubu Tells Victims
Headline: Nigeria: We'll Process Your Claims With FG Free of Charge - Tinubu Tells Victims
Body_Text:
Governor of Lagos State, Ashiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu has assured all the victims of the January 27 bomb explosions that his government would process all their claims for damages suffered with the Federal Government, "free of charge."
Governor Tinubu made this known while assessing the mode of distribution of relief materials at the premises of the Lagos State Television, Agidingbi, Ikeja.
According to him, "I want to assure you that your claims with the Federal Government will be processed free of charge. We will do our best to help you. I have seen your problems. I have moved round. I have seen it all. We'll attend to all your problems we will help you."
While explaining that they should be patient with his government over their quest for damages from Federal Government, Ashiwaju Tinubu stated: "we will process your claims with the Federal Government. You know it is difficult getting money from the Federal Government. It is like using a pair of pliers to remove an aching tooth."
Governor Tinubu nonetheless assured them that everything was being done to provide them with the necessary succour to enable the victims and their families outlive the trauma of the January 27 explosion.
Talking on how he has priotise the succour that would come their ways, Tinubu continued: "My priority for now has been how to give you food to quench your hunger. I have also made the prompt rehabilitation of several thousands of school children affected by the bomb blast back to school my priority, because we cannot afford to abandoned them. Then the priority is shifting to according the dead befitting burials tomorrow Saturday at Oke-Afa, after an Interdenominational service at the LTV 8 premises, Ikeja.
He added that the new priority Saturday's burial ceremony would be paying urgent attention to the settlement of their damage claims against the Federal Government.
Governor Tinubu also assured the victims that his administration would soon make some cash donations to those who had already registered but it will be such that would be aimed at assisting them pending the time their claims would be approved and released by the Federal Government.
He then disclosed that the various donations will go to the reconstruction of schools that were demolished while outstanding if any will be given to the victims.
Governor Tinubu also stated that the various state owned hospitals have been directed to treat any victims free of charge adding too that the state has ensuring that they get the best treatment free of charge.
At the venue a number of cases were also treated on which the Governor directed that prompt medical attention should be given to a boy who had a fractured skull on whom N30,000 had been spent and another with eyes affected by the explosion.
Governor also altered the original plan to limit relief being distributed to the 500 persons who had earlier registered, saying that relations, wives and children of military persons who claim they have not been covered by the exercise at the military cantonment will now be considered.
He directed that evidence of being relations should be brought to verify their claims.
The governor explained that though the state government had not, in it original plans, made a destruction between the military and civilian victims, the military and civilian victims, the action of the Ministry of Defence officials and the Federal Government have shown that their concern is with the military establishment, so the consideration for only the civilian victims.
He urged the victims to find solace with relations and made do with the little that they got by way of food stuff adding that he will only assist with repairs of houses and not major repairs.
Speaking earlier with media persons Information and Strategy Commissioner Mr. Dele Alake opined that though the state plans to have a proper funeral for the victims who lost their lives it will not be a mass of black water that is about 15kms. What can be done is to fence it to avoid people running into it.
On the issue of honouring the volunteer who brought out the bodies, Mr. Alake said while politicising such things in unfortunate that state government will still go ahead with it planned honouring of such people who had been given N10,000 daily while the operation lasted.
Also speaking the Chief Secretary of the Salvation Army, the government's partner in the distribution, Lt. Col. Rowland Sewell said there is a need for the Nigerian Government to put in place a disaster response agency which can act quickly and decisively in terms of emergency and prevent such fall out as this.
He said his organisation which preaches the gospel and undertake humanitarian services, is well placed to handle the distribution having done so in places like India Singapore, Ecuador and such other places.
In all about 500 persons were given such materials as rice, spaghetti, semovita, yam, bournvita and rubber shippers.

Original_Search_Title: Fleeing Idi-Araba Residents Yet to Return One Week After Mayhem
Headline: Nigeria: Fleeing Idi-Araba Residents Yet to Return One Week After Mayhem
Body_Text:
Lagos — ROWS and rows of burnt houses, charred walls, shattered and fallen
roof-tops, burnt cars and buses, premises strewn with fallen walls
and torn corrugated iron-sheets, the sad and bitter faces of the
dispossessed and the bereaved this is Idi-Araba.
But it has not always been so, for this Lagos suburb which
accommodates the famed Lagos University Teaching Hospital (LUTH) has
just unwittingly hosted the consequences of the infamous ethnic
hostilities in the country.
Now in Idi-Araba, a man can stand at a street corner and point at
where his house once stood.
On Saturday, February 9, 2002, in Idi-Araba at about 12 noon, trouble
started when an alleged Hausa man defecated somewhere on the roadside
and was asked by alleged Yoruba to "pack the *****."
Reports had it that the man resisted all efforts to make him "pack
the ****."
According to Mr. Chris Thomas, a resident of No. 8 Fagbenro Street,
one of the affected streets, "they then asked the Hausa man to pay
N5.00 but he refused."
The Hausa man, according to Mr. Thomas demanded: "What about other
people who have been defecating in the same place? What are you
people going to do to them? "They told him they had warned people not
to defecate there.
Subsequently, the Hausa man was beaten up and a report said, to a
state of unconsciousness. There was a reprisal attack from the Hausa
community in the area and a major conflict ensued, leaving death and
destruction in its wake.
When Vanguard visited Idi-Araba yesterday it was no longer the
bustling Lagos suburb it once was. It was deserted with skeletal
activities here and there.
At the Ishaga/Fagbenro streets junction near the Idi-Araba Motor
Park, a police jeep with registration number NPF 2776 B is packed by
the roadside.
A few policemen holding on to their guns are sitting on a bench in
front of a house at the junction. Two or three other policemen, guns
hanging from their hands, are on the road-side checking the light
traffic.
Not very far from his colleague, another policeman also with a gun is
in the process of buying fried yam by the road-side.
The police men are relaxed. The fighting has stopped. The dead have
been taken away. The streets are almost deserted. Most of the shops
are locked up.
Down Fagbenro Street, some of the shops are locked. In front of house
No.
14, Fagbenro Street, a heap of rubbish is in flames, sending smoke to
the skies. The house, a burnt storey building stands there minus its
roof. Some metres away on the roadside is a burnt 505 Peugeot car and
beyond that, is a burnt Volkswagen bus.
At house No. 8 lives Mr. Chris Thomas, an elderly former staff of the
New Nigerian Newspapers. He stands alone in front of the house.
Except for his presence, the entire two-storey building is deserted.
Recounting his experience, Mr. Thomas said, "almost N50,000.00 was
taken from my flat. All these locked stores you are looking at, the
owners are afraid to come back.
Some have said that their customers are no more around to patronise
them.
Some of the men who fled are, however, making plans to come back.
They looted some of my properties."
In front of house No. 6, is a burnt 305 Peugeot with an Edo number
plate and registration number AA362-EKP.
At the intersection where Fagbenro Street meets Ajegunle Street,
stands a school, named Unique Junior Academy a nursery and primary
school. Other houses around the building flaunt diverse levels of
destruction. The proprietress of the school, Mrs. Chinyere
Aiyemonisan had a sad tale to tell.
According to her, "my pupils have all left. They have refused to come
back because their parents are threatening to take them to other
schools. I have 300 kids in the school. I thank God that the crisis
erupted on Saturday and not a school day, you can imagine what would
have happened to my school kids. But I thank God I did not lose any
pupil.
I paid the rent for this place in December and it was to expire in
December this year and none of my pupils have paid their fees, I have
spent huge sums of money. I have not recouped and this crisis
occurred. Nobody knows when life is going to return to this place and
I am about to lose my pupils to other schools. I would like to get a
new place. My school business has been disrupted by this crisis and I
am asking government for compensation.
As Mrs. Aiyemonisan spoke, her teaching and non-teaching staff, about
16 men and women sat on chairs and benches watching.
From the look of things, it will take quite a while for Idi-Araba to
be reconstructed. The destruction was on a large scale affecting
Taiwo, Morgan, Paul Okuntola, Ishaga, Hassan, Ajegunle, Gbolahon,
Bakare, Fagbenro and Ojerinde streets

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Orders Withdrawal of Soldiers From Sankera
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Orders Withdrawal of Soldiers From Sankera
Body_Text:
Benue State Governor, Mr. George Akume, has disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo has approved the final withdrawal of the men of the Nigerian Army from Sankera area in favour of the police.
Mr. Akume, who disclosed this on Tuesday in Sankera during a civil reception organised in his honour by the Ukum Local Government Council as part of his official visit to the area, noted that the recent withdrawal of soldiers from the area was part of the overall process of total withdrawal.
Governor Akume further assured the Ukum people of the total implementation of the Kuru retreat recommendations to ensure that absolute peace returns to Tivland and Ukum area in particular, adding that the state government would continue to demand that justice be done.
The governor commended those who have stood in defence of the interest of victimised Tiv people, particularly late General John Mark Inienger, adding that those who refused to speak out when they had the opportunity have no moral right to now claim interest in the welfare of the people.
The governor further assured the Ukum people of the massive implementation of projects in the area this year with the commencement of the year 2002 budget, noting particularly the expected completion of the Zaki-Biam-Gbeji road and the proposed General Hospital, more boreholes and rehabilitation of more schools, including the realisation of the proposed Zaki-Biam Yam Flour Mills among others.
While calling on the youths to be more dedicated to their callings in life with due respect to the elders and deep interest in education, Governor Akume urged the elders to also advise the youths, assuring that their request for new local governments would be given due consideration.
Earlier, the council chairman, Mr. James Tyoakosu, in his welcome address commended the governor for siting several projects in the area as well as appointing several sons of the area into key positions in his cabinet and for his astitute and matured handling of the communal crisis affecting Tivland and her neighbours.
The chairman, who appealed to the state government for resettlement assistance to set up new infrastructure destroyed by the soldiers, called for the speedy resolution of the crisis and withdrawal of the military from the zone.
Mr. Tyoakosu on behalf of the council assured the governor of the continued support and loyalty of the people of the area particularly his re-election in 2003.

Headline: Nigeria: Man, 27, Nabbed for Impersonation
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Police in Makurdi have arrested a 27 year old impersonator army corporal, who has been harassing and extorting money from businessmen and traders in the Wadata area of Makurdi, the Benue State capital.
The man identified as Mohammed Usman was arrested in an army uniform. According to the Benue State Police Public Relation Officer (PPRO), ASP Emmanuel Adesina, the suspect "got another person's identity card, belonging to a military officer, removed the officer's ID card and inserted his own."
Armed with the ID card and the uniform, the suspect, it was learnt, went about extorting money on a daily basis from traders who had to part with N50 and more daily depending on the amount of wares.
But unfortunately for Mohammed, who claimed he was from 72 Battalion, North Bank, Makurdi, some "soldiers came from the 72 Battalion and demanded for his ID card," which they suspected immediately to be fake.
According to Adesina, the "soldiers came to the police station and complained" on the basis of which the police effected the arrest.
"In the course of our investigation," according to the PPRO, "we discovered that his real name is Mohammed Usman."
A trader, Madam Adamu, told THISDAY that she could not remember the total amount of money extorted from her by the corporal already accused of impersonation, but stated that the man collected N60 from her daily.
Similar complaints came from yam sellers as well as other traders who in grains, vegetables and other produce, all of who said the "fake" corporal has been extorting various sums of money from them.
THISDAY also learnt that the action of the suspect did not end with petty traders; sometimes representatives of some companies often fell victims,." Perhaps given the mentality of the Nigerian public concerning the military, some of them keep giving him money each time he visited them," said a police source.

Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers Sack Town, Raze 60 Houses
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — In what appears as a re-enactment of the raid on Odi town in November 1999, soldiers have sacked Liama town, also in Bayelsa State, leaving no fewer than 60 houses burnt down completely.
The attack on Liama in Brass Local Government Area, is the third in the state in recent times. The incident has left hundreds of the inhabitants homeless even as others have sent a passionate appeal to the Bayelsa State Government to come to their aid in form of relief materials.
Speaking in Yenagoa, a spokesman for Liama community, Chief Ikisa Dokubo Ikisa, said the soldiers came into the community with sophisticated weapons.
Ikisa also alleged that three youths of the community were shot dead by the soldiers. His account tallied with the figure of those earlier declared missing in the community by the state government.
The community spokesman who sobbed intermittently while narrating their ordeal, said that apart from houses destroyed, properties worth millions of naira were also vandalised.
Governor Diepreye Alamieyeseigha had already paid a visit to Liama to assess the level of damage in the community even as he called for the immediate withdrawal of troops from the area.
Alamieyeseigha also summoned the commandant of the military operations, the management of the contracting firm as well as Shell to a meeting in his office at Government House, Yenagoa.
The state Commissioner for Environment, Chief Lionel Jonathan-Omo in a chat with THISDAY confirmed that part of the community was actually razed down by military men attached to the Nigerian Agip Oil Company (NAOC) Limited's terminal at Brass following the seizure of a company boat by youths of Liama.
He blamed the incident on Shell for giving the contract to the oil service firm without any input from the state government.
The commissioner stated that the incident would have been avoided if the relevant government agencies were involved in the drafting of the memorandum of understanding (MOU) between Liama and the oil service firm.
He said most oil companies were operating in the state without the knowledge of the state government. He said that it was only when most of these companies ran into problems with their host communities that they run to the state government for help.
Efforts to speak with the authorities at the Nigeria Agip Oil Company (NAOC) Limited terminal at Brass proved abortive as at press time.
In 1999, Odi was also razed down by soldiers. Militant youths had killed some policemen, who were protesting perceived neglect.
Last year, soldiers sacked Tiv land kiling hundreds in retaliation of the kiling of 20 soldiers who were part of the peace-keeping mission in the area following the outbreak of violence between the Tiv and the Jukun.
Areas affected include Zaki Biam in Ukum Local Government which is widely acclaimed as the food basket of Benue State on account of having the largest yam market. Thousands of people had to take refuge in Daudu and Ukpam camps.
Buildings belonging to prominent Tiv citizens were razed including that of former Army Chief, Gen. Victor Malu by the vengeful soldiers. Three family members of Gen. Malu were also killed.
Last month, security agents also attacked Danja community in Katsina in protest of the killing of a policeman.

Headline: Nigeria: Bombs, Blasts And Smoking Corpses
Body_Text:
It was not exactly 11 September, 2001. But while it lasted, the
concurrent feeling of anxiety, confusion, panic and anguish must have
convinced Lagosians that last Sunday - 27 January - could not have
been more than a few streets away from the grisly episode in New
York.
And the day almost slipped into history as just another Sunday. Sadly
it did not. Lagosians knew where they were and what exactly they were
doing when the city started throbbing at about 5:15 pm on Sunday;
when explosions from the munition dump of the Ikeja Military
Cantonment started, killing hundreds and displacing a lot more.
David Eche, son of a Warrant Officer II (WO2) Michael Eche, was in
the cantonment's Catholic Church with other youths. David and his
peers were having a youth meeting when the bombs announced their
rage. Because the first explosions were milder, their minds never
went to the cantonment armoury, also known as Armunition Transit
Depot (ATD), as the source of the explosive sounds. "We initially
heard that the sounds were coming from expired bullets that were
being disposed," David told TEMPO.
Moments later, louder explosions followed. Before David and his
friends could work out what the matter was, a bomb seared the
church's roof, landing right in the midst of the congregating youths.
It exploded. And, the impact was wicked - a scary and fast-paced
confetti of asbestos shards from the collapsed ceiling. Shrapnel of
glass from the windows and fragment of concrete from the shuddering
walls mixed up with the smoke of bombs and fire. Hell, literally,
paid the church a visit. "There was no time to see if anyone was
injured. I just wanted to get out. Some people were injured but they
kept running with their injuries," David told TEMPO.
With the church in the process of incineration, the youths fled the
fire. But a hotter hell awaited them on the outside. By the time they
stepped out, the bombs had grown angrier, spewing, smoke, fires,
destruction and, naturally, death. The latter came in a huge dose.
Warrant Officer II (WO2) Peter Monday, returned from the Lagos State
Orientation Camp of the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC), where he
had been a platoon instructor for three weeks. Monday returned home
to a grotesque welcome of bomb blasts. His wife and eight children
fled when the bombs hit their home at Corporal and Bellow Quarters
(CBQ) 117, Flat 19 at the cantonment.
According to Monday, his family, like many others, fled the
cantonment when the bombs exploded hitting homes and killing
occupants as well as those fleeing. As at press time, Monday had
found his wife and two of the eight children. "I don't know where
they could have gone. I've served the army for 35 years and
everything I have is lost in the building," Monday moaned
regretfully. But he is not alone. An estimated one thousand people
are yet to return home.
They will return if they are alive and their homes are still standing
as many buildings, both within and outside the barracks crumbled like
cookiesfrom the serial explosions. Residents of buildings which
defied the blast were kept busy sweeping mounds of broken glasses
from their windows, ceilings and walls.
At the Senior Non-Commissioned Officer's (SNCO) quarters, Flats 6
-10, is a pasable imitation of the World Trade Centre scenario. The
roofs of the flats were licked by the hungry heads of the bombs,
flying off from the armoury.
The impact was the same as with everywhere at the cantonment. So
twisted was the ceiling fan in one room that it seemed it had been
made of Plasticine. The kitchen of the flat was strewn with various
food items which blended with pieces of the ceiling brought down when
the bomb exploded. Sitting, ominously, amidst the rubble, was an
unexploded bomb the size of an average yam tuber.
Thankfully, the occupants were not at home when the bombs visited.
The sister of the allottee who refused to give her name, told TEMPO
that she saw a boy pick a hand grenade that fell onto his path.
According to her, the boy attempted to throw off the grenade but he
wasn't lucky and the grenade exploded in his hands.
Scenes like that produced panic as homes and offices burnt without
hindrance.
Eyewitnesses said soldiers, with guns on their soldiers, fled,
scaling the high walls of the cantonment. In the melee, miscreants,
otherwise known as Area Boys cashed in on the situation, raping
fleeing soldiers wives and female children. Within the cantonment,
the explosions destroyed the armoury itself, three secondary schools
and numerous residential quarters among which were the 1,004 flats
occupied by middle-level military officers and the ICB, popularly
called Oshodi Barracks. Other buildings hit include CBQ55, 62, 64 and
68 and SSCO among others. On one road, a deep gash was burnt into the
tar by a bomb which exploded as it hit the road. Motorists abandoned
their vehicles as shops were looted and people robbed.
The bombs were all over. Residents fled in all directions. Parents
forgot their children and children, naturally, adopted a safety-first
approach abandoning parents too. But safety was in very short supply.
The fires grew with every explosion. Buildings quaked and fell within
the cantonment, fires flared so did panic, resulting in a massive
stampede through which hundreds met their deaths.
Sefiu Akojenu and Godwin Johnson, national and Lagos State Training
Officers of the Red Cross movement, told TEMPO that there was really
little to do by the way of rescue operations. The rescued persons
were those lucky to sustain only injuries in the stampede. "We took
victims to the hospital," they said. But the greatest worry is that
of displaced persons. As at press time, many parents were yet to
locate their children and other family members. To help, the Red
Cross, according to Akojenu and Johnson, has established a number of
centres in Lagos where displaced people are advised to go so that
their parents or guardians could trace them. Media houses are also
being used. The success rate however, has been low. Many of those who
fled, ran as far as Ikorodu, Epe and other towns.
Outside the cantonment, life was worse. At the neighbouring
Onigbongbo and other areas in nearby Maryland, panic walked tall. The
explosions rocked buildings, smashing windows, ceilings, burglary
proofs and in some cases bringing down buildings. At the Maryland end
of the Mobolaji Bank Anthony Street, the Maryland branch of the Wema
Bank had its sliding doors smashed. On Irawo, Oketa, Araromi,
Omolanke and other streets at Onigbongbo, residents fled in droves as
their homes heaved with ceaseless explosions, with a few returning on
Monday afternoon when the explosions had subsided considerably.
On the streets at Onigbongbo lie a large number of unexploded bombs.
However, residents are still not over the shock and the presence of
unexploded bombs makes returning unappealling. According to Jude
Ogbechie, a resident of Oketa Street, they have been warned not to
return to the neighbourhood until the unexploded bombs were removed.
Within the upscale Government Reservation Area (GRA) Ikeja, the
damage was equally massive. The chamber of the human rights activists
and lawyer, Femi Falana, was destroyed. Falana's office is on
Adekunle Fajuyi Street, beside the cantonment. When TEMPO visited the
office on Monday, it was deserted, with everything upturned. Numerous
spent shells were strewn all over the premises. On the same street,
the home of General Oladipo Diya, former Chief of General Staff, also
suffered extensive damage. The same fate befell the home of another
human rights lawyer, Chief Gani Fawehinmi, who had his ceilings blown
off.
Elsewhere in the city, the panic was huge. While those within the
vicinity of the cantonment knew the cause of the explosion, people in
other parts of the city had their heads whirling as homes in places
as far as Ikoyi, Idimu, Agege, Ogba and Ipaja throbbed relentlessly.
Motorists sped like demons as they tried to flee from what was
initially thought was a coup attempt. Buildings on Agege Motor Road
were not spared. The LSDPC estate was badly hit. Also hit were Wema
bank, Agege Motor Road branch, Ben Clinic and Maternity Centre, Omega
Community bank, Mandela Hospital and Christ Foundation Miracle
Centre.
In fact, the earliest explanation of the event suggested that a coup
was in progress. A television broadcast by the Lagos State governor,
Alhaji Bola Tinubu, however, dispelled this. Tinubu told Lagosians
that it was the armoury that had gone on fire. Tinubu appealed for
calm.
Calm, however, was uncommonly rare as the explosions continued to
rage late into Sunday night. At Oke Afa canal, Isolo, several
kilometres from the cantonment, the impact of the explosions drove an
estimated 200 persons - mostly children - to their death.
The victims are believed to have rushed into the canal because there
was no clear information on what was happening.
By Monday morning, hundreds of corpses were taken out of the swampy
canal where they mistakenly sought refuge but found death. Mr. J. A.
Moses, one of those who brought out the corpses told TEMPO that he
came to the canal to dig sand but found bodies floating. "So I
started removing them together with my son Gbenga. I have removed
about 150 corpses," Moses said. Monday Olaleye, a bystander at the
canal, told TEMPO that he saw about 2,000 corpses. "The victims were
coming from so many areas. They were trying to cross the canal but
they were deceived by the hyacinth in the water," Olaleye explained.
Among those who found their relatives in the canal was Mr. Jelili
Amuda. Amuda found the corpses of his elder brother and three of his
(the brother's children) floating. Also found, were people identified
as Babatunde Ogunsemowo, of 50, Olaiya Street, Mafoluku Oshodi.
Babatunde was said to have fled his home when the explosion began.
Mrs. Awawu Salami, 47, of Boladale Street, Oshodi, lost her son
Isiaka Salami to the canal. The list is endless.
On Monday, President Olusegun Obasanjo visited the cantonment. The
President, who arrived in the company of the Lagos State governor,
Alhaji Bola Tinubu, addressed a large crowd of displaced persons,
promising to relocate them. Obasanjo also promised relief materials
to the victims of the explosions. The President has also directed the
army authorities to commence inquiries into the tragedy.
According to TEMPO investigations, the tragedy could not have been of
lesser magnitude. TEMPO gathered that the affected armoury was built
by a Finnish Company, Panistama Nig. Limited (PIN) in 1974. Within
its now burnt bowels were an array of high calibre weapons. These
included thousands of 100mm bombs used by T55 armoured tanks, 75mm
artillery launchers and 95mm anti-aircraft bombs. Others were Olikum
bombs which are used for blasting bunkers and other concrete
structures. The Olikum bombs are believed to have been the largest
contributors to the explosion.
Military sources also told TEMPO that the armoury had numerous rocket
launchers, Brownie Magazine Guns (BMGS) made in Hungary and a slew of
British made hand grenades.
Sources told TEMPO that some of the ammunition were acquired during
the Civil War and moved to the cantonment when it was completed in
1974. This magazine learnt that the facility's cooling system,
numbering about 50 units, packed up a long time ago but the army
authorities refused to put them in order.
Successive commandants of the cantonment, we gathered, had complained
about faulty cooling systems, but such complaints did not stir the
desired actions from the appropriate authorities. A hint to this was
dropped by the commandant of the cantonment, Brig-Gen. Gabriel Emdin,
in a broadcast featuring Governor Tinubu, Emdin and Lagos State
Police Commissioner, Mr. Mike Okiro.
Meanwhile, the army authorities have cautioned that more explosions
could occur in the cantonment and nearby neigbourhood. These are
expected to come from yet unexploded bombs which may be set off by
excessive heat. There are many of these all around the cantonment. A
source also told TEMPO that another bunker behind the 1004 (magazine
depot) quarters, which contains low-calibre weapons, could go up in
flames because of lack of maintenance.
Though the bunker was built with the capacity to absorb shock and
pounding, it was razed because hand grenades and smaller bombs had
been exploding for hours before the extensive conflagration was set
off.
The cost of the explosions remains unknown. Military sources,
however, told TEMPO that the tragedy would affect the troops on
peace-keeping duties abroad. The collapsed bunker is said to be the
source of supplies for peace-keeping operations.

Original_Search_Title: The Flight From Bombs to Death
Headline: Nigeria: The Flight From Bombs to Death
Body_Text:
Godwin Ifijeh, who was part of the crowd, fleeing from the bomb explosions in Lagos recalls the journey that led to death for many
Mrs Tokunbo Adeniji, a resident of Ogundele, Mafoluku, Oshodi, a Lagos suburb, had just began preparing dinner that Sunday evening. Time was 5.55pm.
Now a routine, the family takes pounded yam for dinner every Sunday.
"My oga (husband) loves it. He eats it with vegetable soup every Sunday", Mrs. Adeniji, who had delivered a baby boy only a week earlier, began the story of her experience of the bomb explosions at the Ikeja Army Cantonment last Sunday, which not only destabilised the immediate neighbourhood completely but the entire Lagos.
Moment after she peeled the yam, put it on fire, the first bomb blast reverberated, shaking the entire community. Mrs. Adeniji did not take it seriously. She thought a Nepa transformer in the neighbourhood had exploded and went about what she was doing.Then, a second explosion of a proportional vibration came on,it then dawn on her that something was amiss.
Before she could come out to the balcony to take a look at the direction of the explosions, a third of a more severe dimension did not only explode, it came on, flamming all over the sky. It shook the house to its foundation, shattering slide doors and windows. More than ever before, she realises the seriousness of the situation. She rushed out of the house,abandoning her food on the cooker and her other children, playing next door ,jumped into her car to run for cover. The woman had gone over a kilometre before she recollected her self. She stopped,waited at the point speechless, watching the blazing explosions with fear. She eventually summoned courage,made a u-turn to race back home as the flames of the explosions burst with much more rapidity.
At home, the food was almost drying up on fire , she put off the cooker , raced out of the house to the direction the children were playing but like their mother, they had gone their own way to run from the explosions. Mrs Adeniji was aghast and confused but the explosions were no ones she could stand. Everywhere and every object around her was almost giving way from the quaking vibrations of the explosions.
"I stood there for over five minutes, contemplating what to do. I eventually raced all over the place in search of the children, and I eventually met them around the Airforce gate, running towards the airport. I shouted their names, picked them into the car and we sped away towards Ajao Estate. Vehiculer and human traffic was heavy.
"Resident of Oshodi, Mafoluku, Shogunle and to a large extent the exploding cantonment all headed in that direction, some holding out in the premises of the St. Jude Catholic Church, and Church of God Mission International both around the Murtala Muhammed Airport Road .
"We managed to find our way into Ajao Estate but before long we were trapped, human and vehiculer traffic were completely held down. We locked up our car, and abandoned it. The bombs were still flaming, flying all over as if the farther we go, the more they pursued us. There was stampede, people were climbing over themselves, scrambling to get past the area.
"With movement totally halted, motorists abandoned their vehicles and joined those walking to further compound the stampede," Mrs. Adeniji explained.
The story as told by Mrs. Adeniji adequately tells what the situation represented for residents of Oshodi/Mafoluku, Shogunle, Ladipo, Ilupeju, the Ikeja GRA, Maryland Onigbongbo, Anthony, Palmgrove, men, women, children and soldiers of the Ikeja Military Cantonment.
There was chaos all over. Women, children wailed, running as in war situations. Families mixed themselves with everybody including children for running in different directions as the earth and homes continued to quake from the explosions.
As residents of the immediate nieghbourhoods to the Army Cantonment moved away inward from the explosions, those of where they were fleeing were found to have abandoned their homes to move inward safety, equally shaken by the explosions. With vehicles completely out of the roads, people fled to as far as Badagry from Oshodi and suburbs, some others to Sango Ota, then of course, Ikotun to turn the Synagogue of All Nations to a refugee camp. It was in that attempt by some to cross from the inner most part of Ajao Estate into Ejigbo,the next community behind that ended in many dying in the stinking Oke-Afa Canal.
The Oke-Afa incident resulted from the fact that the more you moved away from the explosions, the more they appeared to be coming on. And with the explosions quaking the entire Lagos, no one in the suburbs was spared the flight bid into safety.
At Ajao Estate, while some of the panicking crowd gathered in groups to sing praises to God and pray for His intervention to save the situation, some others, children especially, chose to move deeply away across the swampy Oke-Afa canal if that would make them more prefected. But it turned into a flight from bombs to death.
The swampy grassy canal, an area, where a military jet, which claimed the lives of several officers some years back, crashed and sank, kept swallowing any one that stepped on it. And with the explosions continuing in the same proportions into the night, hundreds, thousands pushed towards the canal, attempting to cross the place in flight. But the hungry canal kept eating them up. No one stepped on it and crossed over.
While many slept in the open and in their cars that Sunday in Ikotun, Ejigbo and Ajao Estate till Monday morning, hundres had sunk to death in the Oke-Afa Canal

Headline: Nigeria: Ethnic Clash: 19 Killed, 19 Houses Burnt
Body_Text:
Tinubu orders security agents to deal with trouble makers
The ethnic clash between the Yoruba and the Hausa in Idi Araba, Mushin local council area of Lagos State, which erupted late Friday, continued yesterday with the death toll, rising to 19.
The clashes also left 19 houses completely razed.
Mike Okiro, Lagos State commissioner of police (CP), who told THISDAY on phone last night that the situation had normalised, confirmed that the clashes left 19 persons dead and 19 houses burnt.
"The situation is now under control. Casualties showed that 19 persons have been killed, and 19 houses burnt. "We have arrested 10 persons in the area with dangerous weapons, petrol and matches. All those injured have been taken to the General Hospital Ikeja, we cannot readily ascertain the figure of the injured right now," Okiro said.
The clashes, which began late Friday, had continued throughout Saturday, leaving many houses burnt and several persons killed. The two sides had used dangerous weapons, including guns, machetes and knives.
Residents of Okunola Street, the scene of the trouble in Idi Araba, Mushin local government area said the two sides engaged themselves in an all night shoot out through Saturday.
The clash, which initially involved Yoruba residents in the area, particularly Area Boys, and some Hausa, was taken over by members of the Oodua Peoples Congress (OPC), who reportedly carried guns and other dangerous weapons as against the Hausa, who were using machetes and knives.
The OPC were said to have come in after the Hausa youths attacked innocent Yoruba as against the Area Boys who were involved in the clash with them. The OPC were said to have been particularly touched by the killing of a three month old baby by the Hausa. The Hausa youths were said to have slashed off the head of the three-month old baby, a development that made residents to invite the OPC to come to their rescue.
Residents said the crisis began after a Hausa boy allegedly went to a public toilet in the area being manned by Area Boys to ease himself and thereafter refused to pay. The Area boys N5 from anyone who uses the toilet. In the process of forcing him to pay, the young man, who was being chased by the Area Boys, was said to have ran into an Hausa house.
The boys were said to have followed him to meet a strong resistance by the Hausa, who were there.
The Area Boys reportedly regrouped, mobilised and returned to the house to attack the Hausa, thereby triggering the crisis.
THISDAY however was privileged to meet the Hausa young man, Yam Idris from Kano, who reportedly used the public toilet and refused to pay in the area. He denied in smattering English that the toilet he used was a public one maintained by the Area Boys.
According to him, the toilet has been there for a long time and was always used by them, a reason he gave for refusing to pay as demanded by the Area Boys.
Meanwhile, speaking last night in a broadcast to the state, Governor Bola Tinubu ordered security forces to deal with all acts of public disorder.
"There is no where in the Holy Quran or the Holy Bible that says we must promote hatred and kill over minor disagreements. The various ethnic groups have lived together in peace in Lagos for several decades. Why are we now needlessly antagonising ourselves," the governor questioned.
Governor Tinubu therefore, expressed condolence to the families of the deceased saying: "I stand with you in this time of great sorrow."

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Questions why the police are holding on to the corpse six years after she was allegedly killed by a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi
She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary last weekend: "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advicof the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Strike: An Interpretation (2)
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are coming back to this topic today. The reason we find the
interface interesting is that we view it as a contest for relevance
before the citizenry.
Government believes that as an elected regime it has a right to make
whatever policies it deems necessary to enable it govern as
effectively as possible. It wants more cash in government coffers and
therefore decides to raise the prices of these products on which
every Nigerian depends one way or the other. To justify the move, it
adopts the term: liberalisation or deregulation; a system of making
fuel a commodity whose prices are determined by market forces and not
by fiat.
The NLC, led by Comrade Adams Oshiomhole, argues that there is no
problem about all this. During earlier negotiations with government,
the union of Nigerian workers and by extension, the ordinary people,
argued that government should increase the prices marginally at the
rate of 7.5 per cent of the existing market prices of N22 for petrol,
for instance. In so doing, an increase at the beginning of 2002 would
not exceed N1.50k. Labour posited further that with N23.50 for a
litre of petrol, those involved in the business of buying and selling
fuel from any part of the world will make modest profits and the
common man can absorb it with minimal pain.
With this argument, Labour s position looked more concerned with the
plight of the people than government s, which is supposed to be an
offshoot of the people s mandate. Adding four Naira to a litre of
petrol and other petroleum products seemed arbitrary and an overkill.
Setting up the Petroleum Products Prices Regulating Committee
(PPPRC), headed by amiable Dr. Rasheed Gbadamosi to effect the price
increases when the Bill establishing the body is said to still be in
the National Assembly, seemed an illegal and unconstitutional act of
the Obasanjo government. And so, on Wednesday, January 17, 2002,
Labour called a national strike.
Our first part of this write-up on Thursday last week put on the
table Labour s two major reasons for embarking on this strike. Number
one, according to the NLC, it was aimed to "force" a reversal of the
increases or at least a negotiation to reduce them. The second was to
let the PDP Federal Government led by General Olusegun Obasanjo to
know that this is a democracy. Governance must not only reflect the
popular wishes of the citizenry, it must also be sympathetic and
humane. It must contain its impulsive propensity to raid the pockets
of the already impoverished common man from time to time in search of
cheap raw cash to throw around without even bothering to account for
how much was gotten and spent. It must, for once, do a bit of
management, a bit of creative thinking, which was the way of past
genuine political leaders, such as Dr. Michael Okpara and Chief
Obafemi Awolowo. These people knew how to create wealth and deploy
it. They did it so effectively that other newly-independent Asian
countries such as India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Pakistan, which have
now turned into economic "tigers", came here to copy our economic
models in the early 1960 s. What we seem to have now is a bunch of
neo-military cake-sharers and cash maniacs who grab the common man by
the throat, tear his pockets and collect the little pennies he
manages to get, and then go sharing among federal, state and local
councils.
Today, we wish to do a little post-mortem of that two-day war of
nerves.
This has become important in view of some of the things that happened
during those 48 hours and what some people have been saying. I have
been listening to various classes of people. To my dismay, traders
are among those who are the least informed on the finer things of
politics and civics. My people are mostly traders. Many of them are
graduates. Those who are not have been travelling round the world and
transacting business with people from all climes, and so their level
of exposure and experience far outstrip those of university graduates
who sit in offices and do the same crummy routines year in, year out.
These traders are the ones who are forever complaining about one
thing or the other that governments are doing to frustrate their
vocation. Little do they know that the moment they are able to
acquire political education and consciousness, they will understand
the power they hold in the downstream sector of the economy. They
will understand that apart from petroleum, commerce is the only
reason that the Nigerian economy has not yet collapsed. Armed with
this knowledge and knowing how to close ranks to manipulate this
power, they can make the government to concede to them a better
operating environment for their businesses.
The working class, by embarking on such measures as strikes and
work-to-rule actions, are only capitalising on the power at their
disposal.
Surely, sometimes this power is ill applied or abused or
over-dramatised.
The fact remains that you should understand the power your
circumstances put in your hands and apply it in pursuit of your
legitimate interests and within the purview of the law.
Questions have been raised. Labour went on strike to force a reversal
of the price hikes. Instead, the labour leaders found themselves
being arrested and arraigned in court for engaging in an illegal
strike and activities liable to cause a breach of the public peace.
The prices that government fixed are being subscribed to by the
people and willingly too.
What has Labour achieved with the strike? Can t it be validly said
that the strike failed? In fact, Professor Jerry Gana, the
