Information and National Orientation Minister even said the strike
"collapsed". I think they all got it wrong.
The Minister s use of the word: collapse, is a misnomer and obviously
part of the sudden recourse to cheap propaganda by the Federal
Government. The strike did not collapse, just as it was not scored a
hundred per cent success. The fact on record is that the NLC called a
strike on Wednesday, 17th January 2002. The Federal Government,
through its Minister for Information, Professor Gana and his Labour
and Productivity counterpart, Alhaji Musa Gwadabe, told workers to
ignore the call. In fact, the Secretary to the Government of the
Federation, Chief Ufot Ekaette, threatened to sack those who failed
to report to their duty posts. What did happen? Whom did the people
listen to?
On Wednesday and Thursday last week, the country was at a standstill.
There was no work, no movement, and no trading activities. Labour
leaders were being arrested, re-arrested and put on trial in courts.
They were being abused over the FRCN and NTA, which suddenly resorted
to its old the-cap-fits-Abacha sycophancy journalism. But the moment
the labour leaders asked workers to return to work in compliance with
the ruling of an Abuja High Court, offices re-opened. Market
activities swung back to life.
The roads bubbled to life again. Life came back to normal. To me,
this does not suggest a "collapse". It suggests "success". It implies
"power", "effectiveness", and "popular support". During the strike,
Nigerians ignored the Federal Government. They listened to the NLC.
They took directives from the NLC. They believed the NLC officials
were suffering on their behalf. They knew that Adams Oshiomhole and
his colleagues were being subjected to police harassment, arrests,
re-arrests and legal terrorism for their sake. The independent media
reported it correctly. The world witnessed it. The only persons that
saw it and refused to admit it were those in the government media.
They reported only what they felt would please the government.
The only problem that the NLC had during the strike was its own lack
of readiness. They were caught flat-footed. Labour under-estimated
government s own preparedness and determination to fight back this
time. Labour was lulled into a slumber after it invaded Aso Rock and
the grounds of the National Assembly in the past two years and got
away without arrests. The carrots that the Federal Government was
believed to have dangled before some of its officials further
deceived them. These officials, including, as some speculated,
Comrade Oshiomhole, were lured into believing that they were now the
"friends" and confidants of Mr. President. All the while, government
officials, who have never forgiven Oshiomhole for his invasion of Aso
Rock, were perfecting strategies for striking back at him. They
dusted the state instruments of coercion the police and government
electronic media. They exploited the law, which the military made to
enhance government s capacity to burst strikes. They knew that the
National Assembly was on recess and in no position to mount their
usually feeble opposition.
To cap it all up, they noticed that Labour was in the lowest state of
alert since the restoration of civil rule. They had relaxed and taken
things for granted. They had gone palsy-walsying with government. It
is like the rat becoming friends with the cat, or the yam accepting
to be buddies with Mr.
goat. They only end up being eaten! They forgot that Labour s number
one job is "the struggle". The struggle is not meant to take over
governance but to make those in governance to do their jobs better.
It is not meant to derail democracy but to make democracy thrive. It
is not meant to sabotage the economy but to make the economy more
suitable for the citizens of the country rather than the foreign
masters of people in government. It is a struggle to bring governance
"down to earth", where the people reside.
One hopes that Labour has learnt its lessons.

Surely, sometimes this power is ill applied or abused or
over-dramatised.
The fact remains that you should understand the power your
circumstances put in your hands and apply it in pursuit of your
legitimate interests and within the purview of the law.
Questions have been raised. Labour went on strike to force a reversal
of the price hikes. Instead, the labour leaders found themselves
being arrested and arraigned in court for engaging in an illegal
strike and activities liable to cause a breach of the public peace.
The prices that government fixed are being subscribed to by the
people and willingly too.
What has Labour achieved with the strike? Can t it be validly said
that the strike failed? In fact, Professor Jerry Gana, the
Information and National Orientation Minister even said the strike
"collapsed". I think they all got it wrong.
The Minister s use of the word: collapse, is a misnomer and obviously
part of the sudden recourse to cheap propaganda by the Federal
Government. The strike did not collapse, just as it was not score a
hundred per cent success. The fact on record is that the NLC called a
strike on Wednesday, 17th January 2002. The Federal Government,
through its Minister for Information, Professor Gana and his Labour
and Productivity counterpart, Alhaji Musa Gwadabe, told workers to
ignore the call. In fact, the Secretary to the Government of the
Federation, Chief Ufot Ekaette, threatened to sack those who failed
to report to their duty posts. What did happen? Whom did the people
listen to?
On Wednesday and Thursday last week, the country was at a standstill.
There was no work, no movement, and no trading activities. Labour
leaders were being arrested, re-arrested and put on trial in courts.
They were being abused over the FRCN and NTA, which suddenly resorted
to its old the-cap-fits-Abacha sycophancy journalism. But the moment
the labour leaders asked workers to return to work in compliance with
the ruling of an Abuja High Court, offices re-opened. Market
activities swung back to life.
The roads bubbled to life again. Life came back to normal. To me,
this does not suggest a "collapse". It suggests "success". It implies
"power", "effectiveness", and "popular support". During the strike,
Nigerians ignored the Federal Government. They listened to the NLC.
They took directives from the NLC. They believed the NLC officials
were suffering on their behalf. They knew that Adams Oshiomhole and
his colleagues were being subjected to police harassment, arrests,
re-arrests and legal terrorism for their sake. The independent media
reported it correctly. The world witnessed it. The only persons that
saw it and refused to admit it were those in the government media.
They reported only what they felt would please the government.
The only problem that the NLC had during the strike was its own lack
of readiness. They were caught flat-footed. Labour under-estimated
government s own preparedness and determination to fight back this
time. Labour was lulled into a slumber after it invaded Aso Rock and
the grounds of the National Assembly in the past two years and got
away without arrests. The carrots that the Federal Government was
believed to have dangled before some of its officials further
deceived them. These officials, including, as some speculated,
Comrade Oshiomhole, were lured into believing that they were now the
"friends" and confidants of Mr. President. All the while, government
officials, who have never forgiven Oshiomhole for his invasion of Aso
Rock, were perfecting strategies for striking back at him. They
dusted the state instruments of coercion the police and government
electronic media. They exploited the law, which the military made to
enhance government s capacity to burst strikes. They knew that the
National Assembly was on recess and in no position to mount their
usually feeble opposition.
To cap it all up, they noticed that Labour was in the lowest state of
alert since the restoration of civil rule. They had relaxed and taken
things for granted. They had gone palsy-walsying with government. It
is like the rat becoming friends with the cat, or the yam accepting
to be buddies with Mr.
goat. They only end up being eaten! They forgot that Labour s number
one job is "the struggle". The struggle is not meant to take over
governance but to make those in governance to do their jobs better.
It is not meant to derail democracy but to make democracy thrive. It
is not meant to sabotage the economy but to make the economy more
suitable for the citizens of the country rather than the foreign
masters of people in government. It is a struggle to bring governance
"down to earth", where the people reside.
One hopes that Labour has learnt its lessons.

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Man Gets 10 Years for Incest
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Man Gets 10 Years for Incest
Body_Text:
A man who defiled his nine-year-old daughter was yesterday sentenced to 10 years in prison.
Justice Caroline Atima Okello sentenced Ahamada Katende, 41, a resident of Bulongo, Ngogwe sub-county in Mukono district.
Sitting at the High Court at Nakawa, Okello said she was satisfied with the prosecution evidence that Katende was guilty of the offence. On passing sentence, Okello said basing on the fact that Katende admitted that he had two wives, he had no excuse to defile his daughter and deserved a deterrent sentence.
Katende committed the offence between April and June 1999 at Bulongo village in Mukono district.
On June 20, the convict ordered the victim to accompany him to uproot yams in their garden.
After uprooting a few yams, he ordered her to undress. When the girl tried to run, he grabbed her and defiled her several times.
Later, the girl reported the matter to her elder sister who informed the the LC officials who arrested him.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Taers Get Relief From Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Taers Get Relief From Robbers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Traders and buyers at the Mile 12 market in Kosofe Local Government,
who in the past were target of criminals who used to lurk in some
corners of the market waiting for an opportunity to strike, now
conduct their businesses with ease.
In the past 15 months, the market has become less attractive to
criminals.
Reported cases of stealing and robbery which used to be a daily
occurrence in the market has drastically reduced. No small thanks to
the team of a joint military patrol which has been permanently
stationed opposite the market.
Though the primary aim of drafting the soldiers there was to prevent
religious and ethnic violence which at the time was imminent, even
when threat of the clash disappeared, the soldiers are still there
doing the traders and buyers in the market a great service.
Metro Business investigation revealed that before the coming of the
military team, robbers had literally annexed the market, making
shopping for buyers and selling for traders a nightmarish experience,
especially in the early hours of the day and late after the close of
businesse when proceeds of sales were usually snatched from traders.
Market women who spoke to Metro Business said the presence of the
soldiers in the market is a complete blessing to them. According to
them, unlike in the past when they hurriedly close shops for fear of
being attacked by criminals, they now take their time and close
without any fear.
One of the women, Iya Ronke, who deals in canned tomatoes and
vegetable oil, claimed that she has been a victim of attacks from
robbers and that the coming of the soldiers has driven the criminals
far. "The soldiers now act like market monitors. Once they suspect
anybody, they quickly interrogate the person. In fact, they usually
punish people who misbehave in the market and this has helped to
maintain peace," she said.
A police post opposite the market was not enough to deter criminals.
A trader who did not give his name explained that those days when the
soldiers had not come, even when a thief was being pursued, the
policemen in the post would turn their eyes the other way, pretending
that they did not see the thief. "But it is not like that with the
soldiers. A thief dare not run across the market. The soldiers will
go after him even when they are not contacted.
When Metro Business sought to know from some traders what their
response will be like if the team is withdrawn, one of them, a yam
dealer, Bala, said they will organise a protest to get the authority
reverse the decision.
The soldiers, it was gathered, have at various times arrested thieves
in the market and handed them over to the police. Just recently, it
was learnt that about seven armed robbers who had invaded the market
were shot dead by the soldiers.
Mile 12 market has been a target of criminals due to the movement of
huge amount of cash by traders and buyers alike.

Original_Search_Title: Go Placidly Amid the Noise, Haste
Headline: Kenya: Go Placidly Amid the Noise, Haste
Body_Text:
Tomorrow, President Moi begins a year of letting go, of ceding control, of losing power, of saying goodbye. On January 5, 2003 - or earlier - he will become peerless as Kenya's retired President. Nothing could be half as difficult for anyone - because the presidency is a superlative institution.
Kenya's first President, Jomo Kenyatta, was fortunate in many respects because he died in office. His body left State House in an expensive casket under military escort and was then swept into a mausoleum by a national flood of tears.
For a long time after his death, he remained something of a cult figure. The corruption, dictatorship, murder, repression and theft that occurred during his reign remained taboo subjects for close to two decades.
As Kenya bids President Moi farewell and welcomes Citizen Moi, it will also be making a clean break with 24 years of Nyayoism - an era replete with failure, decline, conflict, mismanagement, politics and abuse. Since President Moi will be alive and politically active - as he has indicated he intends to be - there will be no national inclination to be nice or polite to him about his perceived failures and misdeeds.
For quite a while, Citizen Moi will be looking at all his triumphs and achievements sinking into a pool of venomous criticism. The failings of the Moi years will be more obvious than his successes because the novelty of criticising a living ex-President will make us heady for a few years.
Criticism and condemnation will not, however, be Citizen Moi's greatest trouble - he has a thick enough skin. Neither will money. Rumour has it that he is one of the wealthiest leaders on the continent. His money may help him adjust rather quickly to life outside State House if he chooses to establish a presidential library or a Moi Fund for Peace.
That way, he may just create a culture for ex-presidents. And Kenya sorely needs one, considering that in another 10 years, it shall have a young ex-president, and another one in the following period. In a space of 20 years we may have gathered enough ex-presidents to form a club.
The biggest problem the President faces is to break the habit of power. After 24 years as President and 12 as Vice-President, one can never rule out an addiction to power. Ordering people around, giving unsolicited advice, prying into Government affairs as though he is an insider, imagining that everyone should know who he is. These things will be his ruin.
Should President Moi insist on staying in the public limelight after this year, as he has indicated he shall, he will be surprised that people will not always be on their best behaviour when they see him.
He will come into the world expecting that we shall be congregating at village centres to listen to him make empty statements of a general nature. He will be shocked that the only sycophants who will be listening to him will be the layabouts he can buy tea and mandazi at local kiosks. He will surprise himself a great deal when his statements are hardly reported in the media.
He will be calling Mr Amos Wako, whom he appointed and who has security of tenure, to ask about why the youths who disrupted his Kanu rally have not been prosecuted and will be told: "Bring me the evidence."
He will call Dr Sally Kosgei, whom he appointed to head the Civil Service, just to get an inside line on why Mr Paul Muite has been appointed Chief Justice - and she will tell him it is a Government secret. The cheek of it!
Worse, people will no longer be sitting at his hearth, waiting for pearls of wisdom to fall off his tongue. After years of having a view on everything - from missile defence systems to affirmative action - Citizen Moi will have to learn that Kenyans will not be looking up to him for guidance.
When these things happen, Citizen Moi might become querulous and choleric. He may start insulting people openly. Then we shall all say: "See, he has become senile."
The President has signalled that he intends to stay on as Kanu boss. He may also choose to vie for the post of MP for Baringo Central. He may even be appointed Minister for Internal Security. But he will be hearing about the newest Cabinet reshuffle, like the rest of us, on the 1 pm lunch time radio news. He will join the rest of us in assuming the worst about the Government's motives and competence.
Should he consider being an overly powerful party boss, even when he gives Kanu an unbeatable election machine, he must learn to stay in the background. Being party boss is not the same as being President. Citizen Moi will no longer hold the knife and the yam. The sycophancy that has attended his rule will shatter into a thousand shards of indifference.
As party boss, he will have to learn not to cross the President. The new Head of State will doubtless want to prove his or her independence. Should Citizen Moi want to compete for the limelight with the President, the prospect of his turning from a hero of the nation to an enemy of the people becomes very feasible.
These visions must have occurred to the President in the past. But in this his final year, they will intensify. He may become erratic and even unpredictable. The President may suffer some very severe withdrawal symptoms.
The country must help him to understand that since he is the first person to leave State House alive, there are indignities he must suffer which he would not have thought possible. To spare himself all this pain, Citizen Moi is going to have to learn the peace that comes from quietude and modesty.
Then there is the problem of his forthcoming idleness. President Moi has a lot of energy that must be channelled into positive activity. There are several openings: like splitting the Sudan into two countries, installing a government in Somalia and teaching "CGV 102 Politics in Africa" at the University of Nairobi.
Unpleasant as the thought of going away is, frightening as the possibility of not being in the thick of political action may be, President Moi has little choice but to start making psychological adjustments to that approaching reality. Then, and only then, shall the country and the world begin to look at him as a nationalist and a statesman.
So, as he begins this difficult year of passing on the baton, there is solace for him in the words of the anonymous monk who wrote the poem Desiderata: "Go placidly amid the noise and haste, and know what peace there may be in silence-"
Writer's email: kwamchetsi@email.com

Headline: Zimbabwe: Zvakavapano Rules the Roost

Harare — FUNGISAYI Zvakavapano is ruling the roost at Ngaavongwe Records with her latest eight-track album Makomborero featuring such songs as the title track, Moyo Wangu, Svitsa Tsvene I and II, Ndomutevera, Nguva Yekutenda and Garai Neni (hymn and instrumental).

Headline: Angola: Basic Commodities Prices Shoot
Body_Text:
Benguela — Prices of basic commodities have gone up over the last few days on the informal market in the cities of Benguela and Lobito (centre), Angop learned..
This is blamed on the rise in the American dollar which is being traded at the rate of Akz 73.00.
The main markets of Benguela and Lobito have recorded a steep rise in prices of such commodities as rice, milk, sugar, cooking oil, maize flour, soap and fruits like orange, lemon, banana, onion and sweet potato.
As an example, a kilogram of maize flour, the main staple food in central Angola, is being sold at Akz 45.00, against the previous 30.00. Imported goods like rice, sugar, beans and beef have also recorded a significant hike in price.

Original_Search_Title: Spruce Dolce Vita Brings Taste Buds to Life
Headline: Zimbabwe: Spruce Dolce Vita Brings Taste Buds to Life
Body_Text:
Harare — This week the Financial Gazette introduces a new column, The sweet taste of life, which will give readers an insight into Harare's numerous eating establishments.
A taste of the sweet life, however fleeting, is a promise made to be kept as one enters the post-modern glass and steel doors of La Dolce Vita in Avondale.
Dining out in Harare is no longer a casual affair, and for those of us with ever-dwindling disposable incomes, a careful choice of restaurant must be made.
Eating out was in days gone by a weekly event. However, it is now reserved for celebrating a special occasion and one is even more concerned with satisfaction and value for money when seeking a gastronomic experience.
Judging by the ever-increasing numbers of diners and revellers, it would seem that La Dolce Vita offers both.
With a keen sense of anticipation, George and I navigated
the crowded Avondale car park, strode purposefully past a buzzing St Elmo's, and climbed the staircase up to La Dolce Vita. Co-owner Marco greeted us warmly before we ascended a spiral staircase up to the cocktail bar for an aperitif.
Master barman Saith started out at the Victoria Falls Hotel as a 19 year old and honed his cocktail-mixing skills at the Monomotapa Hotel, before joining the team at La Dolce Vita almost five years ago.
We mulled over the enticing menu of long drinks, shooters and cocktails and eyed the selection of whiskies hanging temptingly overhead. Red Label Johnny Walker Scotch Whiskey is available at $3 500 a tot, and the ever-popular J&B Whiskey at $1 800. Shooters, such as Bob Marley, start at $550, and long drinks like Malawi Shandy start at $420.
Fascinated by the exotic list of cocktails, I was initially tempted by a Cuba Libre at $910, but settled on Saith's speciality, the Mai Tai, a refreshing blend of dark and light rums and fruit juices, priced at $550.
George unerringly ordered two consecutive Zambezi beers, costing $385 each. Drinks in hand, we gazed briefly out from the open windows at the dark night sky and into the brightly-lit car park below, as busy and populous in the evening as during the day.
The chances of encountering a friend or colleague at La Dolce Vita are great, and we spent an entertaining half hour with two such acquaintances, discussing the future of the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange and our plans for the festive season, before descending into the restaurant for dinner.
George and I were escorted to our table, beautifully set with crisp linen, white china and sparkling glassware, by a relay team of ever-courteous and efficient waiters. We helped ourselves from a bounteous platter of bread sticks and crisp Italian bread while briefly scanning the starter menu.
George's generously proportioned fresh rocket salad served with parmesan shavings and nuts was declared the best ever and well worth $1 390.
I savoured every mouthful of my carpaccio (fine slivers of raw meat) served with fresh greens and parmesan shavings, perfectly dressed with olive oil and balsamic vinegar. This salad is currently priced at $1 635.
Since the char-grilled lamb chops were unavailable, George requested his second favourite dish of chicken breasts cooked in a tangy lemon sauce. This was served with well-cooked sautÃ©ed potatoes, white rice and an Italian version of ratatouille.
George enjoyed the vegetables so much that a second serving was requested and speedily served by our acquiescent waiter. At $2 420, this dish is in the lower price range of main courses.
My beef fillet served on polenta and topped with mustard and parsley sauce was beautifully presented and served with rice, potatoes and vegetables.
The quality of the beef being served in Harare restaurants at present varies from good to indifferent to unacceptable, but mine was first grade and perfectly cooked, as requested.
Since George and I are both a little concerned about our respective cholesterol counts, I will probably leave off the mustard sauce next time. At $3 150, considering increases in the price of beef, this does not seem over-priced.
Having a sweet tooth, I always look forward to choosing a dessert, and after a brief moment of indecision, I ordered a cremÃ© brulÃ©e served with feather light tuiles.
This dessert was allegedly invented by students at Trinity College in Cambridge, who burnt the cream, providing the prototype for the delicious classic now enjoyed everywhere from Acapulco to Avondale.
Sadly, it did not come up to expectations, and I must continue my search for the perfect creme brulee in Harare. All desserts at La Dolce Vita are priced at $1 200.
La Dolce Vita is an up-market chic place to enjoy food served with style. And you don't have to dress up in your smartest gear to feel at home.
We said goodnight to our friendly host and waiters, and set off for home, pondering what further gastronomic experiences awaited us in Avondale's restaurants.

Headline: Zambia: Inflation Swells By 2 Pc
Body_Text:
The monthly inflation rate has gone up by 2.0 percentage points as at end of November 2002, the Central Statistics Office (CSO) has said.
CSO director Buleti Nsemukila said in the latest inflation release yesterday that the monthly inflation rate was recorded at 3.8 per cent representing an increase of 2.0 percentage points on the October rate of 1.8 per cent.
Monthly inflation rates for the metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were registered at 4.4, 2.9 and 4.0 per cent respectively.
According to Dr Nsemukila the annual rate of inflation stood at 25.3 per cent as at November and this rate was 1.5 percentage points higher than corresponding annual rate of 23.8 per cent at October.
Annual inflation rate for the metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 29.7, 21.2 and 25.6 per cent respectively.
The higher inflation levels as observed in November were largely attributed to rising food prices especially mealie meal, maize grain, cereals and cereal products, meat, fish and kapenta. Others are fresh vegetables, nuts and fruits, tubers like sweet and Irish potatoes, beans, poultry products, sugar, cooking oil and fats, milk and milk products.
Dr Nsemukila said the index for food reflected a relatively large increase of 5.7 per cent in November compared with an increase 2.5 per cent recorded the previous month.
Measured on a 12 months basis, food inflation stood at 34.4 per cent gaining 2.7 percentage points on the October rate of 31.7 per cent.
Other contributing factors to the higher November inflation levels were increases in the cost of fuel, house rentals, household goods, clothing and foot wear, cleaning materials, personal care products and services.
Comparatively, the average price of a 25kg bag of roller meal increased by 4.1 percent from K33.597 in October to K34.986 in November while the price of a 20 litre tin of maize grain increased by about eight per cent.

Having a sweet tooth, I always look forward to choosing a dessert, and after a brief moment of indecision, I ordered a cremÃ© brulÃ©e served with feather light tuiles.
This dessert was allegedly invented by students at Trinity College in Cambridge, who burnt the cream, providing the prototype for the delicious classic now enjoyed everywhere from Acapulco to Avondale.
Sadly, it did not come up to expectations, and I must continue my search for the perfect creme brulee in Harare. All desserts at La Dolce Vita are priced at $1 200.
La Dolce Vita is an up-market chic place to enjoy food served with style. And you don't have to dress up in your smartest gear to feel at home.
We said goodnight to our friendly host and waiters, and set off for home, pondering what further gastronomic experiences awaited us in Avondale's restaurants.

Original_Search_Title: Darare Releases a New Album: Hakuna Chombo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Darare Releases a New Album: Hakuna Chombo
Body_Text:
Harare — Killian Darare and the Pilgrims have recorded an eight-track album titled Hakuna Chombo (No Weapon) with Disc and Tape Studios.
The album with such songs as Ndicharumbidza Zita RaJehovah, Jesu Ndimambo, Chibvisa Kutya Kwose and Fight the Good Fight of Faith has stormed into the gospel charts.
Darare whose voice sounds like the late Brian Sibalo also has Dzidzisai Vana, the title tack, Makanaka Baba and Jesu Ndanga Ndakurasa on the album.
Another arrival to the Ngaavongwe stable is Ishmael Chaparamhosva and the Hwedza Kings with their album Chipo Changu recorded at the Shed Studios.
It's a six-track with cuts like the title track, Mwana Wangu Iyeye, Emelina, I Love Rhumba, Lovely Darling/Ruva Rangu and Samuel.
Corner Studios has a new release Kudenga Kwakanaka, an eight-track debut album by Christlike Choir from the United Methodist.
Saint Peters Choir of Mbare is riding high at Ruffcuts Entertainment with Mwari Ngoni featuring songs like Salamu, the title track, Mununuri Jesu, Mangwanani Manheru, Thumel' Moyo and Jesu Handina Simba.

Headline: Zimbabwe: Sondayi, Chingaira Release New Album
Body_Text:
Harare — Tinos  Viriri, Alan Mpanduki, Gaylord Gwatidzo, Collin Sondayi and Lenin Chingaira, also known as Slice, have released a new offering titled Nhasi Nhasi recorded in October this year at Lifetown Studios.
It includes 11 songs, the title track, Helele, Tiki Ndaiwana, Rombe Ndiyani, Malaika and Romeo and Juliet which already receiving airplay on radio.
Ellison Simbanegavi of Gospel Train Records released a debut album Inyasha consisting of songs such as Tirikuchema, an appeal to God to save everyone from the Aids pandemic, Mwari Vanokuda, Ndinovatenda, Mumawoko Enyu, Makanaka and the title track that entered the 3FM Hitsville charts.
The well-composed and sung album was recorded last year and promises to be a hit for the gospel music lovers.
Glory to God: Alpha and Omega is another debut album from Moses Chikumbu alongside the GGC Voices. Recorded in July 2000, the album features Tariro Muna Jesu, Hatina Nguva, Kuda Kwamwari, Musiki, Tanga Waudza Jesu, Makatirangarira, Glory and Huyai Tiende.
Lucky Chikukwa and ZCC Mbungo Stars have an offering called Mwari Tongai recorded at Mosi-Oa-Tunya Studios with six songs: the title track, Chitsidzo, Ngatiyananei, Sekuru, Dhongi, Ndikubatei Muromo and Pamuchinjikwa.
Mharidzo is the album by Tawanda Mutyebere recorded at Shed Studios featuring six songs including Jesu Chiyedza also available on video, Ndiye, Johane, Chinangwa, Namata Wedenga and Kubarirwa Jesu.

Headline: Zimbabwe: Miss Malaika Album Released
Body_Text:
Harare — Starting this week, Spin That Wheel will inform people on the music by artists whose albums have entered their respective stable's charts.
I start by mentioning the Miss Malaika album, a product of Ruffcuts Entertainment entitled Miss Malaika (A Beautiful Sister) which has been released.
It is a compilation of music from different Zimbabwean artists including Chiwoniso Maraire, Matthew Kaunda a.k.a. Mateo, Willom Tight, Patience Musa, Mary, Xiomowa, Brown Sugar, Major Playaz and Kurruption.
South African based Zimbabwean Mizchif and J. Blu from the United Kingdom are also featured.
The 12 track album consists of such songs as Ndiripano, Runako, Tsvarakadenga, Baby Boy, Tutti Fruit, Look Out, Zimbabwe, Thula Thula, Sango and Running Late.
According to the executive producer of Ruffcuts Entertainment, Marco Machona, the album is available at Gramma Records.
"The compilation will definitely make these up-and-coming artists gain the much needed recognition not only in this country but all over Africa," Machona said.
It is going to be launched sometime before the Miss Malaika finals set for December 14.
"All the 28 contestants of the Miss Malaika beauty pageant will be given the album, which they are expected to take home so that it gets airplay in their countries," he said.
Patience Musa from the same stable has released her debut album With Love recorded in May this year featuring six tracks such as Tinotenda, Sango Rinopa Aneta, the popular Zvirinani and Zvapfuura, Siyana Neni and Runako.
Zvirinani is a motivational song that advises people to be self-reliant while Runako talks of the love that some people have.
It is especially dedicated to her sisters.
