Headline: Uganda: 7 Kony Camps Razed in Sudan
Body_Text:
Kampala — Army Commander Maj. Gen. James Kazini was all smiles in southern Sudan, after his forces registered a major victory against Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army last Friday.
Kazini, who flew to the area to give his soldiers a pat on the back for a job well done, told The Monitor that "wherever Kony goes, we shall get him; even if it is underwater".
This was after the UPDF overran seven LRA camps deep in southern Sudan. The army took control of the camps on Kipyei and Tomo hills after engaging the rebels in one of the fiercest battles in recent times. Twenty-four rebels were killed in the two-hour battle and weapons and foodstuffs captured.
Kazini said the army had suffered 10 minor casualties but this could not be independently verified.
UPDF combat intelligence officials at the frontline told journalists that Kony was wearing a white kanzu (tunic) and was conducting prayers when the army struck. A lone hunter who saw the UPDF troops shortly before they hit the camp reportedly tipped off Kony.
When The Monitor got to the camps, corpses of LRA fighters were scattered around the site. Most of the bodies bore multiple bullet wounds.
Scattered gum boots, military uniforms, cooking utensils, raw pumpkins, beans and maize were hurriedly abandoned by the fleeing rebel forces.
Some saucepans containing maize and boiling beans were left on the fire.
Kony was reported to have fled deeper into the north-western part of Tomo hills. A number of mud and wattle houses in the camp were razed by UPDF bomb fire.
The camps also contained hectares of sweet potato gardens. A live goat was abandoned caged close to the best building in the camps, reportedly the one Kony lived in, with good sanitary facilities in the middle of heavily forested terrain.
The UPDF said this terrain slowed down their hunt for Kony.
Majors Michael Ondoga and James Rubahika commanded the battle.
Kazini praised his soldiers for hard work and high discipline.
On allegations by some politicians that Operation Iron Fist is a big failure, Kazini said the work is difficult, but the army is doing everything possible to protect civilians.
Operation Iron Fist is the code-name given to the latest sustained offensive against the Kony rebels.

Original_Search_Title: Sushi Spot Offers Delicious Fare in a Zen Setting
Headline: South Africa: Sushi Spot Offers Delicious Fare in a Zen Setting
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — WAKAME Corner of Surrey Place and Beach Road, Mouille PointTel: (021) 433-2377
FROM the winding staircase painted in pale lime green to the huge skeleton of a Coelacanth dangling above the kitchen and the conical lampshades hanging from a low ceiling, the decor at the newly opened Wakame sushi joint says: Zen.
Spacious and airy, the interior, complete with a view of the sea, immerses one in a relaxed yet upbeat atmosphere.
Starters include seared sancho pepper-crusted fillet of beef with mixed leaves and coriander oil; oysters with green mango, papaya and daikon salad, served with limes; and Asian broth with prawn and ginger dumplings, bok choi, bean sprouts and spring onions.
Main meals look equally delicious: sesame-crusted tuna with ponzu-flavoured bok choi and deep fried sweet potato, or lamb shank coated in cumin and coriander with roasted garlic, mashed potato and green chilli gremolata. There's also the Dragon roll made of eel and avocado.
The venue is noisy (with wooden floors and minimalist settings the sound to ricochets off walls), but that is pretty much the only fault. Our food was delicious. Fresher, more tasty and succulent sushi I have yet to encounter.
Go for a combo platter. I recommend the assorted sushi deluxe platter, which has eight nigiri, two Californian rolls and three maki. The only problem is trying to look elegant while making sense of a maki lost in a soup of wasabi and soy sauce. I eventually adopted the "dip and hope nobody is looking" approach.
The line fish comes highly recommended; a large portion of Cape salmon looked wonderful.
Desserts look interesting; ginger tiramisu, lemon grass and palm sugar brulee, and chocolate truffle cake are on the menu.
A full wine list is available, or you can take your own bottle. Main meals are R60-R72, and the platters cost are R65-R155.
Wakame is open seven days a week for lunch and dinner.
Suzanne Joubert

Headline: Uganda: Sugar Weakens Sexual Strength
Body_Text:
Kampala — EVERY time Gawaya Tegulle has to take a cup of tea, he puts three tablespoons full of sugar in a 300ml cup. No matter how much colleagues tease him about his sugar intake, his response always is: "what is your problem?"
Sugar is good because it is our fuel. Like a car moves with petrol, so is sugar to human beings. Without sugar, the brain cannot function, the muscles cannot contract-all metabolic processes that need sugar cannot function.
Apart from being a source of energy, sugar is a source of heat, which keeps us warm. Our body systems need a particular temperature to operate. If it is too low, you lose your consciousness and die.
However, taking much sugar as Tegulle's, is unhealthy.
Professor Marcel Otim, an expert on hormones, says: "Taking ordinary sugar is suicide. For a 500ml cup, one to two teaspoons will help to give you the ideal taste."
"Taking too much sugar is not healthy because it causes you problems with your teeth; it will make you fat and as you progressively become fat, you get diabetes," he adds.
Dr Amooti Kaguna, the Mbarara District Director of Health Services, identifies two types of diabetes: Inherited and adult-onset diabetes.
"The latter is caused by poor diet and obesity. When one takes too much sugar, it piles in the blood and turns into fats. These fats lead to complications like blindness or reduced vision and high blood pressure. This can lead to heart failure," Kaguna explains.
Prof. Otim says the sugar levels in the body must be between 70 to 120mg. When it falls below 70 and gets to something like 50mg or less, you lose consciousness and someone can die. When the sugar is above 120, insulin must come in and push it down.
If the sugar goes beyond 120, it will come out in the urine. However, Prof. Otim says that insulin cannot work well in a fat tissue (fat person).
Anybody above 30 years of age should have his or her sugar level checked once every month.
Professor Otim says that usually the hormones in the body are geared towards keeping the sugar levels constant, but they can be overwhelmed.
Whenever you eat much sugar, it is stored. The sugar is stored in the liver or in the muscles. If the glycogen stores are depleted, then you begin to break your proteins. In this case you lose weight.
Apart from ordinary sugar, the biggest sources of sugar are matooke, sweet potatoes, beer and fruits. Sugar intake should therefore be balanced with other things. Whereas 50% of the food intake should be carbohydrates, 10 to 30% should be fats and 10 to 20% proteins.
Other than have one food item in plenty, Prof. Otim, says the trick always is to have a mixture. This will help you avoid having too much sugar.
For those who are prone to becoming fat, those with a sweet taste and diabetic patients, sweeteners like canderel can do.
Charles Wazaarwa, the director of Makerere University International Development Institute at Ruharo community centre in Mbarara, advises people aged 50 years and above in particular, to take canderel.
"Always put one tablet of canderel in a cup of tea. One tablet of canderel is equivalent to one teaspoonful of sugar and is enough for a cup of tea," says Wazaarwa. Canderel can be got from supermarkets.
He also urges people with blood groups A and AB, to desist from sugar because it weakens their sexual strength.
He advises people to check their blood groups and respond to sugar accordingly.
Published on: Monday, 14th October, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Local Avocadoes for US $8m Contract Depends on Consistency of Supplier
Headline: Uganda: Local Avocadoes for US $8m Contract Depends on Consistency of Supplier
Body_Text:
Kampala — A Memorandum of Understanding, in which a Ugandan firm will supply various food items worth $8m per year to a US firm, was signed on Tuesday, between US based importer John Nuwamanya-Matsiko and Ugandan fruit exporter John Kavuma.
The signing was held in the Uganda Export Promotions Board (UEPB) boardroom in Kampala.
Nuwamanya-Matsiko is a Director of USAfrica International Limited, which supplies fruits for two wholesale outlets in the US. Kavuma is the Chairman of the Federation of Associations of Uganda Exporters (FAUX). The first consignment will consist of avocadoes.
"This deal is worth $8m on a repeat basis, provided Kavuma can supply the avocados without the storage temperatures going above 40 degrees fahrenheit (about 5 degrees celsius). That is what will make or break the deal.
"It is necessary to put the fruits in a cold storage chain right from the garden, in the transportation, right through to the freighting. We have agreed to pay for all the freight charges as part of the price we pay," Matsiko said.
He explained that he had actually wanted seven to twenty forty foot containers of fruits per week for five months from January to May. The period when Florida and Californian producers in the US are not in marketing.
"The period January to May is when we have a window of opportunity which we can explore to get our fruits into the US market. The market is opening up and I think the big question of freight will be eased as more airlines try the direct US route" he said.
Kavuma said FAUX has been going around identifying growers of avocados. Other items Matsiko wants to import include sweet potatoes, hot pepper, ginger and mangoes.
He said the available avocados they have identified on the market, make up only a total of four tonnes per week.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Master Art of Asking Stupid Questions
Headline: East Africa: Kenyans Master Art of Asking Stupid Questions
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kenyans like asking stupid questions and expect clever answers. That is
why a Kenyan will meet you sweeping and ask you, "Oh, so you are sweeping,
eh?" He asks the question in a manner that suggests that he expects you to
say, "Yes my good sir, I am sweeping and this thing in my hand is actually
called a broom,"
The right answer to that question should of course be, "No I am not
sweeping. What you see in my hand is actully a hoe and I am in the process
of digging up sweet potatoes for my dinner."
Another Kenyan sees you reading a book and he asks, "What are you
reading?" That Kenyan does not care that you are enjoying the book and that
he has no business interrupting that moment when the story is just about to
take a surprise turn. He asks that question in a manner to say that he
expects you to tell him the plot and the characters of the story.
The temptation is to tell the man, "I am reading nothing. I am just
counting the number of words in this book and if you don't mind, let me go
on with my business."
Later, you are washing you car and your neighbour who has always seen
the car asks, "Is this car a Toyota or a Mazda." The word Toyota is
prominent on the bonnet and behind and he can read that but because he is a
Kenyan, he asks that stupid question.
He asks the question in a way that says he expects you to tell him,
"Yes, this can is a Toyota and it was originally made in Nagoya. It was
driven by a Japanese called Samura Moto for two years. It has a 1300 cc
engine and four cylinders."
The temptation is to tell him, "No a Toyota does not look like this. In
fact, the word Toyota on the grille is a mistake. This is a Tata salon made
in Gulu. It has a 2000 cc engine and also Turbo charged. I am preparing it
for Muhangi to use in the next rally."
No one can ever mistake a Moi motorcade because it is preceeded by
escort cars that seem to be driven by drivers who smoke illegal
things. However, that will not stop a Kenyan from asking another, "Is that
Moi passing?" He expects such a reply as, "Yes, it is him. He was dressed in
a gray suit and a carnation on his lapel. He was escorted by racing drivers
with their sirens blaring."
You are tempted to tell him, "No that was not Moi passing. It was
actually the Sultan of Brunei who has borrowed Moi's motorcade to do a bit
of touring. You see, the Sultan of Brunei is so poor that he has to depend
on charity to move from place to place."
The most irritating question is asked when the lift stops at a
floor. The direction indicator button is showing very clearly that the lift
is going up but that will not stop a Kenyan from asking, "Is this lift going
up or down?"
The fellow expects you to tell him, "Although the indicator button says
that the lift is going up, it is actually going down. You see, these
machines have a habit of changing their minds and that is why its button
says that it is going up."
You are really tempted to tell him, "No this lift does not go up or
down. It just goes sideways."
On another occasion you meet Kenyan whom you had last seen twenty fours
before and he asks you, "So you are still there?" He expects you to tell
him, "Oh yes, this is actually my body, the very one that I had yesterday."
In reality you feel like telling him, "No I am not there or here. The
person you are seeing is my shadow. I will however be there this afternoon."
In short, when a Kenyan asks a stupid question, I feel like giving a
stupid answer. I
By the way, is this The Monitor newspaper that you are reading?

Headline: South Africa: Show US Your Form
Body_Text:
Hilary Fine
A rather pompous and arrogant Englishman once expressed the following
view of football and females: "Why do you women even bother, you can never
fully appreciate football. You don't even understand what the offside rule
is all about."
This Englishman went on to describe how football is "poetry in motion"
and in order to grasp its beauty one needs an intricate knowledge of its
form, structure, rhythm, metre and verse. Things that he obviously believed
were beyond the capability of the female sex.
Not every man holds such sexist views, but many see soccer as serious
business. But women are avid followers of the sport and are keenly watching
the events of the World Cup unfold. The women encountered watching soccer in
bars and lounges around Johannesburg not only understand what the game is
about, but also seem to relish the spectacle on a number of levels that many
male followers do not.
Many women have a deep regard for the stylish incantations of the
crowning glory of footballers. Indeed, there is something lyrical about the
rhythm of flowing hair -- for which the Italian and Brazilian players are
noted.
Some women identify with this vanity and are even jealous of the fact
that a man should possess such silky strands."I wish I had hair like that,"
I heard a woman spectator remark of Emmanuel Petit's luscious locks.
Perhaps the marketers at L'Oreal should tailor-make a celebrity
endorsement campaign for the World Cup. I think many women would take notice
of advertisements where soccer's Samsons did a sexy hair flick and cooed the
tagline, "L'Oreal -- Because I'm worth it".
"Skill isn't enough; to be a footballer worth your balls you need good
hair," chirped a female colleague.
It may seem superficial, but image is important. Even those favouring
short styles understand this. The Japanese highlighted spiky "dos" and the
previously shaven David Beckham grew back his mop as a spiky Mohawk after
angering his long-standing sponsor Brylcreem.
But hairstyles are only the fluff. They are simply an attractive
accompaniment to the main course -- the meat and potatoes. The game attracts
a visceral appreciation of the player's skill and prowess on the field.
Everybody -- male and female -- gets hot under the collar for the main
event. The game is a truly visceral experience, primal even, which sets
pulses racing. There is even something tribal about those little victory
dances players do after scoring. It's an emotional rollercoaster of joy and
tears that is served up in those 90 minutes (plus extra time) and national
pride is at stake.
And, of course, many women find it the perfect time to appreciate
"poetry in motion". Every player on the field sports a fine pair of
ogle-worthy legs.  Run boys, run!
But after the final whistle, men slip in to rerun mode and nurse their
beers. While they are still mulling over the main course, some female fans
are awaiting dessert. It's not that women don't appreciate the main meal,
but there is always time for something sweet.
When players exchange strips with opposition members a sea of
well-defined torsos adorn our television screens. While this may simply be
seen as an admirable display of sportsmanship, many of us see the game as a
prelude to this smorgasbord of stomachs and sculpted shoulders that is the
swapping of shirts.
"It's the best part," remarked one drooling female colleague, while
another fanned herself mumbling: "This is a workout for my hormones."
Seldom is there such palpable female sexual interest in sports. But
soccer seems to be different. One female colleague explained it like this:
"You can't exactly get hot over the sight of rugby players, they're not
exactly pretty are they?" But footballers seem to be that perfect
combination of good looks and manliness -- something to really get excited
about.
But this revelry in sporting eye candy does not cut both ways. Umbro
once ran a billboard campaign featuring a very attractive young female model
in the team strip of an English football club. The copy read: "Who says
women look better with their kit off?" Witty as this was, it proved
fortuitous.  Men seem to like to keep sports and sex in strictly separate
arenas.
When Brandi Chastain, midfielder for the United States women's soccer
team, scored a game-winning penalty in the 1999 World Cup, she celebrated
like the boys -- by removing her shirt. It is obvious from the outcry that
followed the sight of her bra that men are perhaps not as ready to enjoy
sport on another level.
Last week CNN ran a viewer poll asking, "What hopes do you have for the
World Cup?". A female caller said: "I wanna see Beckham swap shirts with
Ronaldo." This only proves that my limited sample is not alone in their
regard of a perfect rhyming couplet.
And just for the record -- a player is offside when he receives the ball
from a team-mate and there was only one opponent between him and the goal
when the ball was struck.

Original_Search_Title: Tourists Dine in Owino, Surprise Vendors
Headline: Uganda: Tourists Dine in Owino, Surprise Vendors
Body_Text:
KADILO, a tour guide with one of Kampala's tour companies surprised
Owino Market vendors when he led a group of five tourists, two female and
three males to Owino market where they dined.
The tourists are said to have hired Kadilo to help them tour Kampala
city and Kadilo decided to take them to Owino the 'black man's market,' as
he identified it.
By then it was lunch time. Since they were hungry and had spotted people
eating food, they asked Kadilo to identify a clean hotel so that they could
feast on African food for the first time.
Kadilo identified Mama Nakato's restaurant where they sat down and
ordered for lumonde (sweet potatoes), fish) and matooke (banana stew). They
enjoyed the meal and even asked Kadilo to write down for them the foods they
had eaten.
The tourists paid Maama Nakato $20 and thanked her for the delicious
meal. Since then, customers have been praising Mama Nakato for her delicious
food which can even attract bazungu.

Original_Search_Title: Speech delivered by George Ninneh Flahn: Focusing On the Future
Headline: Liberia: Speech delivered by George Ninneh Flahn: Focusing On the Future
Body_Text:
Atlanta, Georgia
Editor's Note: Over the Memorial Day of 2002, the Grand Gedeh
Association in the Americas held its Tenth Annual Convention in Upper Darby,
a suburb of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Below is the full text of the
Keynote Address delivered by Dr. George Ninneh Flahn, the Guest Speaker for
the occasion:
Mr. President and Officers of the Grand Gedeh Association in the
Americas, Elders and Members of the Board, the Chairman and Members of the
Convention 2002 Committee, Distinguished Guests, Fellow Grand Gedeans and
Fellow Liberians, Ladies and Gentlemen.
I want to thank the Convention 2002 Committee for selecting me to be the
Keynote speaker for this Convention. It is an honor and a privilege for me
to be here.
I bring you greetings from your brothers and sisters, the Grand Gedeh
Diaspora in the United Kingdom and Europe. I bring you special greetings
also, from the officers and members of the newly formed Grand Gedeh
Association in the United Kingdom. Yes, Mr. President, upon the receipt of
your letter to Grand Gedeans in Europe in January 2002, asking us to join
with your association and contribute towards the re-building of our county,
a group of Grand Gedeans living in the United Kingdom met on the 4th of
March 2002 and the Grand Gedeh Association in the Unite Kingdom was
borne.
Yours truly was elected the first Chairman, Miss Shirley Kaydea,
Secretary, Miss Sarah Rue, Treasurer, and Miss Jehmu Zulu, Researcher. Some
prominent members of our association include, Mr. Amos Bohn, former
Superintendent of Margibi County, Mrs. Anna Kaydea, Mr. and Mrs. T. Gaye
Zulu, to name a few.
My association wants me to assure you that we stand united with you in
helping to rebuild Grand Gedeh County. Each member of our association will
contribute to the Grand Gedeh County Reconstruction Fund established by
you. Your treasurer will be hearing from our treasurer shortly. We hope that
eventually, the Grand Gedeh Association will extend throughout Europe.
Mr. Chairman and members of the Convention 2002 Committee, in keeping
with the theme of the convention, which is "Deh-Dele, Zohn Cheh", translated
in English as "A New Beginning for a Better Tomorrow," the title of my
speech is "FOCUSING ON THE FUTURE."
We all know that the civil war has destroyed our county and country. Our
people, the Grand Gedeans, continue to be gravely affected, whether at home
or in refugee camps across West Africa. Our proud nation has been reduced to
a desolate people depending on handouts. This cannot continue indefinitely,
and I believe we the Grand Gedeans in the Americas and Europe, the lucky
ones, have a significant role to play in bringing about a change for our
people. As we become involved with the hustle and bustle of day to day
living in these countries, it is easy to forget who we are and we need
avenues like this convention to remind us of who we are, where we have come
from and why we are here.
Never forget where you come form. We can change our names, we can change
our nationalities, but we cannot change our place of birth. That is our link
to our heritage. I have a British Passport, but my place of birth, which is
Zwedru, is written in that passport. My wife was borne in Paisley, Scotland,
and my children were borne in Stourbridge, England. When we travel together,
an immigration officer can easily tell that my place of birth is a little
strange and different from the rest of my family. He would then ask me
"... . Where is Zwedru?" To which I will reply, "Zwedru is in Liberia, West
Africa."
About two years ago while traveling from Amsterdam I told my wife if any
immigration office ask me again about where Zwedru was " I will cook their
soup." She was completely confused, and asked what I meant. I said, "Wait
and see." As luck would have it, we met a young English immigration officer
who did not disappoint me and proceeded to ask me,"... where is Zwedru?" And
I said, "Zwedru is in Scotland, in the Highlands." He said, "Really?" "Yes,"
I said, because I knew most English people know very little about
Scotland. Of course, my wife told him that I was just kidding.
So you see my friends, our place of birth is one of those things that
will remain with us until we die. We therefore owe it to ourselves to invest
in its development and prosperity. Never forget the place of your
birth.
Here, we can learn from the Jews. The Diaspora began with the Jews since
they were the first group of people to be displaced by war on a large
scale. But no matter what country the Jews found themselves, they were first
and foremost Jews, and their adoptive countries were secondary. Hence the
names... . Jewish Americans, Russian Jews, English Jews, etc. Every Jew,
whether born in Israel or outside of Israel, always aspires to return to the
motherland. I believe we have to have the same frame of mind in order for us
to make a meaningful contribution towards the development of our county and
country. The only distinction I would like to make from the Jews is that we
have to learn to live in peace with our neighbors, something that has eluded
the Jews for many years.
As we focus on the future of our county and country, let me remind you
that the development and rebuilding of our county will depend on us. We
cannot expect outsiders to do it for us. As we look around the world today,
there are conflicts in every corner of the world, from Asia to Eastern
Europe, from Central America to South America, and of course, across the
whole of the African continent. The International Community is busy frying
bigger fish, like combating International Terrorism, and dealing with
International refugees. Since the end of the cold war, we no longer occupy a
strategic position; therefore our interest is not high on Uncle Sam's
list.
I believe that the time has come for us to engage ourselves in a new
process of nation building, not from the narrow perspective of sectionalism,
but through a broader approach which focuses internally on civil society,
and externally on creating or strengthening regional mechanisms. It is not
charity that our people need but rather our solidarity. Solidarity is not
just a moral value. It is often said that good leaders are
visionary. Vision, in my opinion, also means to realize that solidarity
brings stability, and that only through stability we will achieve
prosperity.
Let me share a story with you that has an American flavor and which I
believe we can all identify with:
On Thanksgiving Day many years ago, a young family awoke with a sense of
foreboding. Instead of looking forward to a day filled with gratitude, they
were preoccupied with thoughts of what they did not have. At best they would
scrape together a meager meal on this day of "feasting". If they had
contacted a local charity group, they would have had a turkey with all the
trimmings, but they didn't. Why? Because they, like many other families,
were proud people. Somehow they would make do with what they had.
The difficult situation led to frustration and hopelessness, then to
irreparable, harsh words between the mother and father. The eldest son felt
devastated and helpless as he watched the people he loved most become more
and more angry and depressed.
Then destiny intervened... a loud and unexpected knock at the door! The
boy opened it and was greeted by a tall man in rumpled clothing. He was
grinning broadly, carrying a huge basket brimming with every conceivable
Thanksgiving delight: a turkey, stuffing, pies, sweet potatoes, and canned
foods-everything for a holiday feast.
The family was stunned. The man at the door said, "This is from someone
who knows you're in need and wants you to know you're loved and cared
for". At first the father of the family did not want to take the basket, but
the man said, "Look, I'm just a delivery person." Smiling, he set the basket
in the boy's arms, turned to leave, then called over his shoulder, "Have a
great Thanksgiving!"
In that moment, this young man's life was forever changed. With this
simple act of kindness, he learned that hope is eternal, and that people,
even "strangers" really do care. The sense of gratitude he felt moved him
deeply, and he swore to himself that some day he'd do well enough to give
something back to others in a similar way. And by the time he was eighteen
years old, he had begun to fulfill that promise. With his scant earnings, he
set out to purchase groceries, not for himself, but for two families he had
learned were in dire need of food. He then drove to deliver the groceries,
dressed in an old pair of jeans and T-shirt, intending to present the gift
as if he were a delivery boy. When he arrived at the first dilapidated
house, a Latino woman who looked at him suspiciously greeted him. She had
six children, and her husband had abandoned the family only a few days
before. They had no food.
The young man offered," I have a delivery for you, ma'am." He then went
out to his car and began to carry in bags and boxes overflowing with food: a
turkey, stuffing, pies, sweet potatoes, and canned foods. The woman's jaw
dropped. The children, when they saw the food being brought into the house,
let out shrieks of delight. The young mother, who spoke only broken English,
grabbed the young man by the arm and started kissing him all over, saying,
"You gift from God! You gift from God!
"No, no" the young man said. " I'm just the delivery boy. This is a gift
from a fried." Then he handed her a note that said, "This is a note from a
friend. Please have a wonderful Thanksgiving; you and your family deserve
it. Know that you are loved. And someday, if you have the chance, please do
well enough to do this for someone else and pass on the gift."
How many of us cannot remember a time when we did not have food, no
money to pay school fees or buy books and uniforms, until someone came to
our aide - maybe an uncle, an aunt or other relatives, a teacher, a Peace
Corps volunteer, a missionary, maybe a God Pa or God Ma, or even a stranger
helped us along the way.
When I think of the young people of Grand Gedeh County who are eager to
learn but have no money to pay school fees or buy books, or even find food
to eat, I see in them future doctors, lawyers, university lecturers, future
political leaders, all waiting for help to develop their full potentials. I
believe we can make a difference in their lives, just how someone made a
difference in our lives to help us get to where we are today.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, I believe the Grand Gedeh
Association in the Americas is well placed to bring about a meaningful
change in the lives of our people. I sincerely hope that all Grand Gedeans
will heed the call and contribute to the Grand Gedeh County Reconstruction
Fund, and help to make a difference for our people and pass on the
gift.
Let me close by leaving you with an inspirational verse, which has
inspired me through the years:
"I may pass this way but once. Any good therefore that I can do, let me
do it now. For I may not pass this way again."
Thank you very much for listening. May God bless Grand Gedeh County and
Liberia.
About the Speaker: Dr. Flahn is a Staff Surgeon at Russells Hall
Hospital in Dudley, the United Kingdom, and a Fellow of the Royal College of
Surgeons of Edinburgh. He is the current Chairman of the Grand Gedeh
Association of the United Kingdom
P.O. Box 450493
Atlanta, GA 31145
Website: www.theperspective.org
E-mail: editor@theperspective.org

Headline: Uganda: Porter in Court Over Defilement
Body_Text:
A porter at Nkonjeru Convent Sisters in Mukono appeared before Nakawa High Court judge, Justice Carolyne Okello Thursday for trial on defilement.
The statement of offence says that the accused, Israel Kiwewa being employed as a porter at Nkokonjeru TTC in Mukono had unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 18 years, contrary to section 123 (1) of the Penal Code Act.
The girl told court that on Feb. Feb. 5, 1999, the Sisters sent her to collect sweet potato vines to feed pigs from the TTC's garden.
She said when she was picking the vines, Kiwewa grabbed her and raped her.
She reported to the Sisters who in turn reported the matter to police.
The victim's father told court that the medical examination at Kawolo Hospital had confirmed his daughter had been defiled.
The case was adjourned to April 23 for further hearing.

Original_Search_Title: He Has HIV, She is Negative, But They Are Getting Married
Headline: Uganda: He Has HIV, She is Negative, But They Are Getting Married
Body_Text:
HE has HIV; she does not. But these two are set to wed in church this Saturday, March 16.
For the last 11 years, Benard Nzabandora, 46, and Esther Nanyombi, 35, have been cohabiting well knowing that they are in a discordant relationship.
They knew they had a problem in their midst, accepted it, and not at any one point in time did they let the problem tear them apart.
Instead, they decided to play it safe and plan for the future together. A future they have finally decided to crown with a wedding.
The two will walk down the aisle at Naddangira Catholic Church in Wakiso district at 4.00pm and exchange vows before Reverend Bruno Serunkuuma.
Nzabandora first knew he was HIV positive in August 1989, when he carried out an HIV test at Mulago Hospital. He was not sure about his status because at that time he did not know what it meant.
All the same he joined The AIDS Support Organisation (TASO).
In 1990, the AIDS Information Centre (AIC) opened at Baumann House. Nzabandora went for another test, and the results were no different. He was positive.
Three months later, he went back to AIC for another check up. This time, he put all the uncertainty at the side. It was true. He was positive.
The challenge before him was how to let the secret out to the wife. At TASO, they had preached about safe sex and there was no way he could just all of a sudden introduce the topic to the woman he had only lived with for three years.
For each of these years, Nanyombi had delivered two children and was pregnant.
Nzabandora quit the home, and stayed in Kampala most of the time, occasionally checking on his family.
In 1991, their young child was ill. Nzabandora was worried. He took the child for a blood test. The child was HIV negative.
It was at this point that he planned with a counsellor to visit their home in Kakiri, Hoima Road, and talk to Nanyombi.
The counsellor focussed on the child's illness and advised Nanyombi to have a blood test. She was negative.
Nanyombi went with Nzabandora for the results. They declared the discordant results to them and counselled them on living together without Nzabandora infecting Nanyombi.
"I got so scared about the results, I didn't know what that meant," reminisces Nanyombi.
"I was not sure about my status until I went back for another test and it confirmed me HIV negative," she adds.
Nzabandora explained to Nanyombi that he had to leave the home because there was no way he would just introduce "safe sex" in the home. This won him Nanyombi's heart.
Nanyombi felt abandoned during the time when Nzabandora stayed in Kampala but could not share her concern with anyone. She kept at home for the sake of the children.
"I feared sharing with anyone that I was not having sex. I just told my elder sister that my husband came in once in a while," she says.
Once the two had their status known, it was time for them to enjoy their conjugal rights. They resumed their sexual life in 1993, only this time, it was protected sex.
"Our sex was not as frequent as it used to be. We began having sex once a week or twice a month, but with condoms," shares Nzabandora.
"The only trouble I find with sex is that I easily get tired. The body becomes weak and I feel worn out. Because of that, right now we have spent the last eight months without sex,"
he adds.
Nanyombi misses sex but she has convinced herself not to think about it and instead think about her children and her two step-children who are in S6 and S4.
Nzabandora has not lived on any anti-retroviral drugs but he is keen on treating any opportunistic infections. He eats vegetables and any food fresh from their garden. But hates any food that disorganises his tummy like sweet potatoes.
He has kept in sound health save for the frequent attacks of pneumonia and the TB he had in 1999.
"Pneumonia disturbs me a lot and that is what makes me uncomfortable. This is because I always move on a motorcycle to do my work. To prevent it, I try to put on a jacket, even under the sun," says Nzabandora.
Nanyombi looks much healthier. "She used to be slender but now she's put on weight. I think she always gets the fatty and juicy bits to herself," Nzabandora jokes, prompting a wide smile from Nanyombi's dimpled face.
Grace, a counsellor at AIC, says about the two: "That is our couple that has really coped. The man is hardworking and that is what is wearing him out. The woman is happy. One might be tempted to think that they are lying about their relationship but they have children. We have seen them."
The couple is so endeared to each other. Don't they have any problems? They laugh and Nanyombi volunteers: "There are times when we have problems at home and even call in a close friend to intervene but our misunderstandings are never HIV related."
Nzabandora cannot hide his appreciation for his wife: "I am grateful to my wife. I want to assure you that if it was her who was positive, I believe I would have got another woman and left her. I thank her for being there for me."
Looking forward to the wedding, Nanyombi says: "I am happy. Even if he died now, I will be a lawfully wedded wife. Relatives cannot easily chase me away from the home."

Headline: Uganda: Ex-Minister Promises Potato Market
Body_Text:
Former state minister for gender, Mrs. Albina Opio has promised to secure better market for sweet potatoes widely grown in Teso region.
Mrs. Opio, who was recently elected woman councillor for Kachumbala sub-county, in Kumi, said she would use contacts she made as minister and resident district commissioner(RDC) to look for markets in other parts of the country.
Opio, also former NRC woman representative for Kumi, trounced four other women to win the Kachumbala seat.
She said that she opted to contest for the lower council elections, because it was at the grassroots that the community required much assistance for development.
Opio served as RDC in Kabale, Iganga, Katakwi and Kapchorwa.
"Kapchorwa district alone could provide a very lucrative market for the popular yellow potatoes from Teso region, now that the road is being tarmacked," Opio said.
She said that Kabale was a potentially large sweet potato market and her people stood to gain.

Headline: Zimbabwe: Eating Out - Tremendous Taverna Athina
Body_Text:
Harare — ABSOLUTELY noone's indispensable. This was proved when I visited Taverna Athina, for the first time
since Mano Babaliokis left for the Gulf.
Three new partners, Greek and non-Greek, were all away, said manager
Roger Woodward-who with ready smile and Falstaffian figure, looks and
acts like a restaurateur.
He led me to my table, passing a formally laid arrangement for 20
couvertes, unwinding at the friendly bar. There were perhaps 30
diners already enjoying good Hellenic cuisine, customers still
checking in.
"It's a quiet night," young Woodward commented. Serving 60+ on a
"quiet' mid-month night speaks volumes for the regard diners-out have
for Athina.
Other than prices little has changed. New management stuck to Mano's
proven recipes, staff seemed the same, decor unchanged. Laminated
menus now have current prices magic marker-ed in, updated expensive
reprinting; a chalk-board reflects specials. Lighting is a little too
discreet for size of menu typefaces, not helped by ceiling fans
causing a flickering I found disturbing; one, I which creates a
setting in which epileptics could throw serious wobblies.
Food is Balkan with a few cosmopolitan favourites. Last time I
lunched there, braised oxtail casserole was one to die for, but it
wasn't a good plan to tackle Athina's generous portions with much
cerebral work to be done later.
Greek starters included skordalia or houmos (chickpeas mashed with
garlic) $335. Dips: tarama, dazdziki, melizanosalata were $460 as was
lunza (pork marinated in wine with black pepper and coriander),
afelia (cubed pork treated similarly), dolmathakia sakanaki (sounds
Japanese, but deep fried feta), keftethes, fried melizano (brinjal)
with garlic and loukaniko (Greek sausage).
Deep fried mushrooms, chicken giblets or livers were under $500;
haloumi, crumbed, deep fried mussels with tomato salsa or souvlakia
(grilled cubed beef with lemon wedges) around $550.
I'd forgotten how huge Athina's salads were. Greek cost $425, but
would feed two as a starter. There was a stunning array of green
olives and slightly over-salty feta. A genuine Greek salad contains
no lettuce. This had a few leaves hidden under onion, tomato and
sweet pepper. Bread was a large basket of good, hot crusty
"continental" type.
I ate half the salad as an appetiser with bread, sipping a chilled
refreshing article of a moderately intoxicating leat nature ($180).
Prawns, linefish, calamari, sole, hake and kingklip were all
available but subject to the quotation many punters are disinclined
to request. On the blackboard, butterfish was $1 040; "salmon" $2
060.
I'm partial to "mincey" things: spaghetti bolognaise,
cottage/shepherd's pie, mince and tatties, etc. Best known Greek
example is moussaka where good minced steak is layered with potatoes,
eggplant, topped with rich Bechamel sauce. I was determined not to
talk myself out of the dish, one Teresa and I rarely cook. It was
$865 for a large individual bowl.
It needed just a little black pepper; with plenty of delicious salad
left to accompany the moussaka, not a morsel remained.
Other Greek specialities similarly priced are soudzouvakia,
pastitsio, yemista rice), and dolmathes. In Greece this would
comprise savoury mince stuffed in vine leaves; we use spinach).
Kebabs were $1 065; Greek mezes $1 715.
While enjoying the moussaka, I studied long and hard before deciding
against house speciality: Mano's chicken. Marinated, basted, charcoal
grilled it costs $945, as do chicken peri-peri, schnitzels, diavalo
(marinated in lemon/black peppercorns) and garlic chicken. Chicken
cordon-bleu or Kiev with chips and a selection of young vegetables)
were $1 120.
Grills ranged from $950: cubed pork fillet in mushroom sauce, pork
chops or Vienna schnitzel; to $2 095: rack of spare ribs. Plain
grilled fillet was $1 040, with various sauces from $1 100 to $1 140.
Grilled lamb cutlets sauce were $1 100, pan-fried veal lemonato, $1
015.
Lager was splendidly chilled. Local white wines were $600-$930 except
Vigneron's Selection: $1 100. Domestic reds $730-$1 040, rose $600.
Imported reds included a litre of "Versus" $3 300; standard bottles
varied between $3 590 to $6 570: South African whites ranged from $2
000 to $5 150. Graca was $3 300, JC Le Roux $5 500; wine by the glass
$220, by carafe $700, corkage and a selection of young $300).
Puddings are from $260 to $350. Two handsome scoops of a
sophisticated after eight flavoured mint and chocolate ice-cream were
enjoyed at the lower price; a pleasant cappuccino was $140.
Bottom line: enormous Greek salad, moussaka, pudding, coffee and two
Pilseners was $2 050. Service, despite demanding "shipping orders"
from 20 hungry and thirsty punters at the next table, was slick and
professional.
A sudden sneeze, whilst picking garden peas for supper two days
earlier, had severely put my back out. I left Athina doubled up, like
Quasimodo, after a very enjoyable meal during which I'd totally
forgotten the discomfort!
Three-and-a-half stars at mid-August 2002.
Taverna Athina, 129, Union Avenue, Harare. Tel 731222/735525. Closed
Saturday lunch, all day Sunday.

Headline: Malawi: Gwazantini Hospitalised
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Alice Gwazantini who is facing murder charges for allegedly scalding
to death her maid Ntchayi Jakisoni, is currently admitted at
Mwaiwathu Private Hospital where she will undergo an operation to
remove a cyst in her stomach this week.
Confirming the development in a telephone interview on Saturday from
Zomba, Malawi Prisons Public Relations Officer Tobias Nowa said
Gwazantini was initially hospitalised for asthma some two weeks ago.
"But she later started complaining about stomach pains hence the
operation," he said.
However, authorities at the hospital are keeping Gwazantini's illness
under wraps.
But a reliable inside source revealed that Gwazantini is admitted in
a private ward, Akuzike where she is waiting for a gynaecologist to
remove the cyst.
The source further revealed that Gwazantini has been taking
pain-killers and vitamins in readiness for the operation.
"At the moment she is just fine. She has been eating and chatting
with her guardians. But we don't know how she will be after the
operation," the source said.
Nowa also disclosed that security has been tightened at the hospital
since Gwazantini is an accused person.
"The hospital [Mwaiwathu] has its own guards who have been placed on
higher alert. We have also supplied our female security guards to
ensure maxmum security," he said.
Nowa further said as a security measure, nobody is allowed to visit
Gwazantini in her ward, save for her family members and close
relatives. The source revealed that so far only Gwazantini's spouse
has been visiting her.
Gwazantini hit the headlines last August when she allegedly doused
boiled water on Jakisoni for eating a sweet potato which was not
meant for her.
Jakisoni fought a losing battle with life at Queen Elizabeth Central
Hospital for nearly three weeks until she eventually died on August
18.
A public outcry ensued and led to the incarceration of Gwazantini on
August 17, a day before Jakisoni died. She has since been denied bail
and her case is in the High Court.

Headline: Uganda: Bundibugyo Gets Village Bank
Body_Text:
A village bank; Abanyarwenzori Mountaineering Association (AMA) Micro-Credit and Savings Trust has been opened in Bundibugyo district.
The bank is aimed at instilling a culture of saving in the local community.
Members will obtain loans from the bank at an interest of three percent per month.
Deposits will attract an interest of two percent per annum.
The director of the project, Bahaati Azoori, said recently members are required to pay a membership fee of Shs 8,000.
"After getting 100 members, we shall convene a general meeting in January 2002 to elect a Board of Trustees which will be responsible for formulating policies and appointing staff," he said.
Azoori said members will also be given improved varieties of matooke, sweet and Irish potatoes and passion fruit.
He said Shs 15m has been injected in the bank as they await more funds from donors who are expected in the district soon.

Headline: Zimbabwe: Improved Margins Lift Delta
Body_Text:
Harare — IMPROVED margins lifted Delta's historical cost attributable earnings
346% to $6,431 billion in the six months to September.
The group said the below inflation increase in turnover of 71% was
misleading since it had actually increased by 209% when the impact of
the demerged units was taken into account.
Robbie Mupawose, group chairman said: "When making comparisons with
prior years, turnover which has increased somewhat less than
inflation-when viewed in isolation-has actually increased by 209%
when the impact of the demerged units is taken into account.
Attributable earnings increased to 733,7 cents from 167 cents-which
was broadly in line with market expectations.
Operating income grew 245% to $9 billion while dividend per-share
rose 300% to 200 cents compared with last year's 50 cents.
The increase came in spite of a fall in beer sales, although this was
by less than the company had forecast. Lager sales had eased 6%
against company expectations of 8% while sorghum beer sales fell 8%
against Delta's forecast for a 10% drop. These smaller than expected
declines were partly offset by an absence of change in sales of
carbonated soft drinks against company expectations of a rise of 8%.
Mupawose said the second half of the year would be "better than the
first half due to the holiday season peak activity and the hot
weather".
He said in spite of the inflation rate and economic crisis, the group
was poised to perform well in light of the "consumer expenditure that
has remained firm, buoyed by the low interest rates which discourage
savings and the impact of remittances by Zimbabweans working across
the border".
Delta has also embarked on a stocking programme to counter the rising
inflation. "Because of inflation and deterioration in exchange,
necessary capital expenditure will be accelerated and where possible,
stock holding will be increased," said the group chairman.
Most of the major projects requiring capex had been completed and all
forex exposures had been completed, CEO Joe Mutizwa added.
The group had spent $3,3 billion on capex, largely for maintaining
and expanding operations and had anticipated keeping up this spending
in the second half.
Delta was optimistic it would not have a problem sourcing forex in
the future.
Mutizwa added that the group would be launching a rebranding campaign
over the next six months to leverage its corporate and consumer
brands.
Delta shares closed at $145 on Thursday losing $45 (23,7%) on the
week.
What the story means: Delta shares may have suffered along with the
rest of the market, but supplies of raw materials are guaranteed to
the end of the financial year in March. Beer volumes have dropped,
but not as much as the company had anticipated. Margins look set to
increase further for Delta's largely inelastic products. Price
controls do not affect their products.
Most analysts reckon Delta is a solid buy at current levels.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Improves Margins to Buck Lower Volumes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Improves Margins to Buck Lower Volumes
Body_Text:
INSPITE of a decline in beer volumes and flat sales of softdrinks, Delta Corp has reported a 346% rise in historical cost attributable earnings to $6,431 billion in the six months to September as a result of improved margins.
Delta financial director Rob Maunsell told an analysts' briefing that beer sales had fallen by less than company forecasts. Lager sales had eased 6% against company expectations of 8% while sorghum beer sales fell 8% against Delta's forecast for a 10% drop. These smaller-than-expected declines were partly offset by no change in sales of carbonated soft drinks (CSD) against company expectations for a rise of 8%.
Maunsell said the trend in CSD sales was downwards, but part of this was due to glass availability. However, he added the unit now only comprised 15% of profits.
In the six months ended September, Delta reported 71% increase in turnover to $34 billion. However, once the demerged units were taken out, turnover was up 209%. Operating income rose 245% to $9 billion, but when adjusted for the demerger, it was up 403%.
EPS amounted to 733,7 cents (2001: 166,9 cents), which was broadly in line with market expectations and achieved the company's aim to achieve last year's full year EPS of 741,6 cents in the first half. Maunsell said the group had declared a "modest" dividend of $2 a share (2001: 50 cents) because the current period was the group's peak cash requirement.
Along with a weaker market, Delta shares closed at $147,90 on Wednesday, down $57,10 on the week.
Maunsell said the the operating margin - operating income to net sales - shot up to 32,8% from 20,4% as a result of margin recoveries and the shedding of overheads. CEO Joe Mutizwa said the group headcount was down by 6% and overheads remained well controlled. "This is only the beginning ... we are confident will be able to show more savings," he said.
Mutizwa predicted a strong third quarter as a result of bonuses and funds remitted from overseas. However, the fourth quarter was expected to be "softish" but the company was still "bang on target" for the full year. The group did not anticipate a "dramatic" second half like last year when 75% of earnings were generated in the second half as a result of an exceptional fourth quarter.
While the company had cash on hand of $1,887 billion at the end of the period, the group had "spent the lot" in the first week of October and was back in overdraft, Maunsell said.
Some $3,6 billion was spent on working capital ahead of peak period building up stocks and raw materials such as barley. Stocks on hand increased by 132% to $13,3 billion.
"We been pretty well cash neutral for most of this year," Maunsell said. "The glass bottles we imported from Dubai were all prepaid for," he added.
Mutizwa said the company had imported enough bottles to see the group through to the end of the financial period. As far as other inputs went, Mutizwa said the company has secure adequate supplies of maize and barley until the March year end and were "confident" of overcoming problems with sugar supplies.
Imports as a percentage of cost of sales amounted to 40-45%, due largely to the importation of vehicles and spare parts, management said. Most of the major capex projects requiring capex had been completed and all forex exposures had been completed, Mutizwa added.

Headline: Namibia: Lightning Kills Man
Body_Text:
A BOLT of lightning killed Martin Ndjimba (30) at Omupulungushu
village in the Ohangwena Region in the North on Tuesday.
NamPol Liaison Officer in the North, Sergeant Samwel Hamukonda, said
Ndjimba was in his mahangu (sorghum) field when he was struck by
lightning at about 14h00.
A companion, Junias Thomas (40), sustained serious injuries.Thomas
was hospitalised at the Eenhana State Hospital.
More than 10 lightning deaths were reported to Police in the North
during the last rainy season.
Villagers from Onepandaulo village, one of the areas most hit by
lightning in the North, have appealed to Government to investigate
the cause of lightning and install devices to shield them from the
devastating power of nature.

Original_Search_Title: United Breweries Left With Two Shareholders
Headline: South Africa: United Breweries Left With Two Shareholders
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — UNITED National Breweries, formerly National Sorghum Breweries, has ended up with only two shareholders after buying out the minorities and converting the company into a private company from a public entity.
Indian-based United Breweries and Durban based Marriott Merchant Bank, hold 74,99% and 25,01% respectively. This eliminates the broad black shareholding which the sorghum brewer had. The company, dubbed by the Black Economic Empowerment Commission in its report as the first real empowerment of black people, began when black investors led by former National Sorghum Breweries chairman Mohale Mahanyele bought it from government for R45m.
The brewer raised its funding from individuals who included township distributors.
United National's president and chief operating officer, Rajan Ranganathan, said on Friday the acquisition was in its interests as their returns on investment had not yielded any dividends until 1999.
The Johannesburg High Court earlier this year sanctioned the scheme of arrangement in which United National was able to buy R46m worth of minority shareholders' interest (65,6%) in the company from 9000 shareholders, including the Premier group, Gensec, the Transvaal Municipal Pension Fund and Nedcor Investment Bank, at 100c a share.
To fund the scheme in part, it was proposed that Nedcor Investment Bank subscribed for the preference shares amounting to R36m.
Ranganathan said United National Breweries' board was restructured to have five members from United Breweries. Mariott was not keen to have a seat.

Original_Search_Title: Case of Villagers' Desperation And the Law
Headline: Kenya: Case of Villagers' Desperation And the Law
Body_Text:
Something curious, yet so uplifting, is alleged to have happened recently in a village called Kaharo in Maragwa District. It is now the subject of a court case. The defendants are a group of respectable middle-aged mamas who have been charged with malicious damage to property. The ringleader had her case recently changed to assault.
They are accused of taking the law into their hands by raiding a den of bootleg booze and turning the place upside down. Let me start by saying that it is not my intention to intrude into the legal technicalities of the case. The matter is before a court of law whose burden it is to determine whether the women are innocent or guilty. Suffice is to say that when the matter blew into the open, the local Protestant church organised a campaign of support for the women. Soon, FIDA got attracted to the case. The organisation's deputy executive director, Ms Judy Thongori, has now taken charge of the women's legal defence.
The accused could be any of the ordinary peasant women you find in villages ravaged by the consumption of illicit brews. Many such women, tired of seeing their husbands and sons waste into vegetables because of dangerous bootleg liquor, are increasingly taking matters into their hands.
It is a statement of the potency of these brews that the hapless drunks whom the angry women encounter in their raiding sorties are in no condition to put up a fight. In one of those queer twists, it so happens that these wild brews are sold in licensed establishments. Incredibly, too, the people who manufacture these brews can show permits for it. The authorities, in their wisdom, have seen it fit to leave things this way. It sounds absurd, but you could actually get charged with a crime for interfering in the sale of these brews.
To be sure, instances of angry mothers taking robust action of this sort have become a common feature in most areas in Central, Eastern and parts of Rift Valley provinces where these brews are sold. But the practice of those at the receiving end has been to politely give way until the mothers' rage subsides, then get briskly back to normal business. Never before have I heard anybody seeking to punish such mamas.
The brews come with exotic names like kulta, jet, sorghum sake and kairasi. There is even a brand called vatican. But whatever the name the brews go by, the common denominator is that they are uniformly lethal. Wives quietly but grimly relate to those they trust about how their men have become cabbages when it comes to certain obligations husbands are required to perform. But the sheer tragedy is to see strong and fit men progressively reduced to emaciated wrecks who are no longer productive or useful.
Families are getting wrecked. Women are increasingly being left as the sole bread-winners and also as the people who carry out all the productive labour in many village families. Something is getting rotten in the social fabric of village life. And it is no exaggeration when a huge share of the blame is put before these devilish brews.
To their credit, churches have been quick to understand the peril these brews pose to society and have taken the lead in the fight against them. At a conference the NCCK held in Nyeri last week, the matter of these brews again came up. There was a great deal of justifiable anger directed at the provincial administration and the government machinery in general for allowing these brews to proliferate. Mr Zachary Ogongo, the PC for Central Province where these brews are alarmingly prevalent, was present at the conference. But rather than own up to the fact that the authorities were being negligent in the face of a genuine crisis, he chose to pass the buck to his junior officers.
It would be nice to say that the government is doing as much as the churches in the fight against these illicit drinks. But it is not. Many government officers working in the provinces are a large part of the problem. Chiefs and assistant chiefs are especially notorious. Yet they can't afford to be complacent unless they get a quiet nod from their superiors, which is the point the participants at the NCCK conference sought to impress on PC Ogongo.
Sure, the Provincial Administration will periodically announce the "banning" of these drinks, and for a while accompany the "ban" with an energetic confiscation of the brews. But such action is short-lived and purely for show. The sellers and distributors are soon back in business.
It is not difficult to see the pattern of tricks that ensures the people involved in this industry of killer brews remain in business. Notice first of all that the brands are never sold in the district they are manufactured. A particular brew consumed in Murang'a is known to be manufactured in Mukurwe-ini in Nyeri. Many others that flood Central and Eastern provinces are known to originate from Naivasha. This ensures that the manufacturers are outside the jurisdiction of the chiefs and DCs where their products are consumed.
In any case, "banning" consumption will remain an empty gesture unless you get rid of the manufacturers. That means the government revoking their licences. It is a simple and straightforward act. And if they go underground, as they must, then mobilise everything you have beyond the Provincial Administration. Bring in the Public Health Officers and the police authorities from the OCSs to the OCPDs. Many of them may be corrupt like hell, but one crucial advantage they have is that they don't need a court order to act on the brewers and sellers.
Otherwise, everything will be left to crusading village women taking their own kind of executive action.
At a recent seminar I attended that was held behind the scenes of the Africa Union summit in Durban, an uncomfortable revelation was made by one of the learned participants. The combined productivity, in economic terms, of the whole of sub-Saharan Africa has yet to catch up with Hong Kong, a tiny city state which is now part of China. And this includes Nigeria and South Africa, the supposed economic giants of the African continent.
E-mail: gwarigi@nation.co.ke

Original_Search_Title: Proposed Revision of Domestic Content Law
Headline: Ghana: Proposed Revision of Domestic Content Law
Body_Text:
Trade and Industry Minister, Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku on Wednesday proposed
a revision of the Domestic Content Law to increase the use of raw materials
in the manufacturing industry from 10 per cent to at least 20 per cent.
Speaking at a national symposium for research scientists in Accra, Dr
Apraku said this strategy would add more value to local raw materials,
create employment opportunities and overall industrial growth and
development.
The Research Staff Association of the Council for Scientific and
Industrial Research (CSIR) organised the symposium under the theme:
"Propelling Ghana's Scientific, Technological and Industrial Growth" to
discuss ways of improving the application of science and technology to
industrial development.
Dr Apraku expressed the regret that breweries import 6.5 million dollars
worth of malt for their operations when they could rely on local
sorghum.
He said industries massively import clinker, starch, leather and eggs
when they could be produced locally and called for the reversal of the
practice to create ready market for such materials.
Dr Apraku identified finance as a major problem in industrial technology
development and urged small and medium scale industries to access funds from
institutions like the Export Development and Investment Fund (EDIF),
Business Assistance Fund and the Trade and Investment Project Fund (TIP)
that have a lower interest rate of 15 per cent as compared to the
traditional banks.
He expressed the hope that those funds would ultimately lead to an
establishment of an Industrial Bank.
Mrs Anna Nyamekye, Deputy Minister of Environment and Science, said
science and technology are major weapons in the fight against poverty and
underdevelopment. However, serious attention has not been paid to investment
in scientific infrastructure and research.
She stressed the need for more funds to be allocated to scientific
training.
Professor Akwasi Ayensu, Deputy Director General of the CSIR, said there
is the need to establish a Presidential Commission on science and technology
to give maximum attention to the development of those disciplines.
Mr Yaw Owusu Addo, Vice President of the Ghana Journalists Association,
called on the media to increase their reportage on science and technology
and move away from over-concentrating on politics.

Headline: Uganda: Talk Peace, Acholi Plead
Body_Text:
'The government doesn't want us to go home, the rebels want us to go home, the people are confused'
Bukenya, the minister in charge of the Presidency was in Acholiland on the directive of President Yoweri Museveni to assess the emerging situation and advise on possible ways of bringing the 16-year old conflict to an end. The New Vision's Okello Jabweli travelled with the minister and now reports.
Bukenya's visit comes against a background of renewed and more daring attacks by the rebels on civilian targets, camps and military detachments.
In one month since crossing from Sudan, the rebels, led by Vincent Otti, have killed over 50 people including 30 UPDF soldiers, abducted scores of people and looted property.
The bandits have left general despondency, trauma and fear in their wake. Against this backdrop, Bukenya visited Alero, Anaka and Purongo displaced persons camps in Gulu last week.
The people of Alero and Purongo are still reeling from recent attacks, which left many of them displaced and destitute.
Throughout the stops, Bukenya said he was in Acholiland to listen to the people on how the war could be ended. The people responded with sincere narratives about their plight and proposals for bringing peace to the region.
In all three places the rallying cry of the people was the same: "We want peace. We are tired of war. We don't want to live in these camps any more. We want to go home."
The camps, comprising round grass-thatched huts were instituted around 1996 as "safe havens" for the people against marauding rebels in the countryside. While some civilians went to the camps on their own, others were forced there by the army. But as Kony intensified his raids on the camps, looting, abducting and killing people, the camps increasingly came under a spotlight, with some people demanding that they be allowed to go home.
The Acholi have been living in terror and fear for much of the last 16 years. Everywhere you go all that meets you are war-weary people who are unsure of their very existence.
At Anaka, a woman who only identified herself as Auma said women are suffering more than the men: "We are like polygamous men. When we are here we are with the UPDF, when we run to the bush we find the rebels," Auma said in reference to allegations that UPDF mobile troops rape women.
"The war in Arua ended. Who will end ours? Is it people from outside? Soldiers are just doing business here. When rebels come there are nowhere to be seen," she told Bukenya. Odong Bwoch Okello agreed, saying whenever the rebels strike soldiers simply run away. Auma said Col. (Samuel) Wasswa and Lt. Col. (Walter) Ochora (Gulu LC5 chairman) were peace-makers who brought out some rebels from the bush: "Why did they take Col. Wasswa away? These people who say 'defeat them' are the ones escalating the conflict," she charged. Auma ended her contribution with a funeral song.
At Purongo, Bukenya met Walter Okello, a seven-year old orphaned boy who was abducted June 29 by the rebels together with his 60-year old grandmother Biturina Atto.
According to Mark Okot, the LC3 chairman, Okello was later abandoned at Got ngur where he spent five-days and nights on bare grass, feeding on mud. When he was finally rescued, Okello was simply gaping. He could hardly utter a word. Now, back at the same camp from where he was abducted, Okello is showing signs of recovery even though his pink-ish complexion and the look in his brown innocent eyes suggest that it could take a while before he resumes his "normal life."
Okello's case simply mirrors the plight of thousands of Acholi men, women and children who continue to suffer ignominy at the hands of heartless vagabonds.
The desperation is running deep. William Okot called for the intervention of the Organisation of African Unity (OAU), now transformed to the African Union, to end the war.
Unsure of the safety of the camps and unable to return to their homes, most people now flock to town centres by evening and then trek back to the camps in the morning.
The minister was accompanied on his tour by State minister for Sports Henry Okello Oryem, MPs; Zachary Olum (Nwoya), Jacob Oulanya (Omoro), Jane Akwero Odwong (Kitgum Women) and Norbert Mao (Gulu Municipality). Others on the team included the fourth division commander Col. Guti, Gulu LC5 chairman Lt. Col. Walter Ochora, Adjumani RDC Ahmed Awongo, his Gulu counterpart Max Omeda and Semei Okwir. The Gulu Deputy RDC Awongo accompanied the minister in his capacity as dean of northern RDCs and also chairman of the security committee for the region.
A huge camp to cater for the internally displaced people (IDPs) from the west of Gulu has been created behind Lacor hospital. Other IDPs from outlying areas are camping at the bus park and Owino market area. A huge humanitarian crisis is looming now that relief agencies fear to take food to the camps. At Alero, where the rebels burnt over 1,000 huts, the New Vision saw women preparing to cook burnt remains of their millet and sorghum grain.
The rebels also abducted scores of people and looted dry a nearby health centre. At the time of our visit, health centres, schools and other services in the sub-county were closed. The chairman Alero sub-county told Bukenya that most teachers fled to Gulu town.
But despite the suffering, the tenacity of the Acholi remains. At Alero, Anaka and Purongo people expressed the hope that the war can be brought to an end if only the government and rebels engage in serious dialogue.
Okony Simon Opiro said the Acholi people are in dilemma. "The government doesn't want us to go home, the rebels want us to go home; The people are confused," Opiro said. The politics of the camps is very intriguing. While the government fears that the civilian population in the countryside would be susceptible to attacks from, and also possibly collaborate with the rebels; the people think such a move would help steer clear of the confrontation between the UPDF and rebels. At the moment, UPDF detachments are situated at the camps to protect the people.
"We are on the brink of death. The president should lower his level because this idea of inquiring about our opinions is simply wastage of time. He should just agree to talks (with the rebels)," Opiro said.
Even with all the displacement and suffering already inflicted on the people, the rebels are still breathing fire. A July 5 letter circulated through LCs, Vincent Otti, the LRA Commander leading the current raids warned people to flee from camps or risk their (rebels) wrath.
The letter hand written in Luo warned that the rebels would slaughter 250 people, with knives and shoot 250 others to show their seriousness.
"The army is incapable of defending you. If you think the UPDF is strong? Ask them what happened to them in the Sudan." Religious and political leaders in Gulu told the New Vision that the LRA letter was authentic as it came through the same channels an earlier one Otti wrote announcing the release of 108 women and children.
Throughout his sojourn Bukenya called for ideas and proposals on how to end the war. "We must stop this war: The Acholi culture is under threat. Why should we destroy the rich culture of Uganda? The Acholi people should say enough is enough," he urged.
He said while government would continue with its mandate of (militarily) protecting its people, the President is not opposed to peace talks with rebels provided the rebels stop killing people and burning huts: "The medium for these talks could be religious and cultural leaders. Let us explore this (idea of talks) sincerely," he said. The minister promised that government would beef up security for the camps and appealed to the people to stay in the camps as government continues its drive against the rebels. He appealed to Kony informers within his audience to relay to him one message: "Stop killing people and burning their huts."
On the immediate needs of the people, Bukenya promised to ensure that relief supplies including food and medicine resume. At Alero, Anaka and Purongo, the minister said the convoys would resume almost immediately. However owing, to the prevailing situation most aid agencies are yet to resume full operations for fear of rebel ambushes and abductions.
In the meantime the Gulu district administration and the military have reduced the number of camps, from 32 to 21 in order to concentrate the UPDF presence in them. Semei Okwir, deputy RDC, says with a reduced number of camps the UPDF will now be able to deploy more troops to better protect the people.

Headline: Botswana: Minister Lobbies for Drinking Ban
Body_Text:
Selebi Phikwe — Botswana may become a teetotaller's paradise if its trade, industry,
wildlife and tourism minister gets her way.
Pelonomi Venson is travelling the small southern African country to
solicit support to have beer and other alcohol declared illegal.
"Most accidents in Botswana are caused by drunkenness," Venson said.
"It has also been discovered that most rape cases are committed by
people who have consumed alcohol."
The minister also blames the breakdown of families on alcohol abuse,
"even if alcohol is made of sorghum it is not a food".
Botswana police report that out of every five fatal road accidents
three are caused by drunken driving.
The government has already enforced tough alcohol consumption laws
that prohibit bars and other outlets from selling liquor after 11pm
on week nights and 12am over weekends.
Venson's ministry is responsible for issuing liquor licenses but says
they have become reluctant to issue new licenses since January.
Government ministers have previously unsuccessfully campaigned to
have the alcohol content of the country's alcoholic beverages
reduced.
Venson and her sympathisers efforts to ban alcohol will most likely
be frustrated by the fact that the national brewery, Kgalagadi, is
the only successful manufacturing venture in the country and the
largest employer.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Killed At Least 300 Sudanese Villagers, Says UPDF
Headline: Uganda: LRA Killed At Least 300 Sudanese Villagers, Says UPDF
Body_Text:
Members of the Ugandan rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and their leader, Joseph Kony, have killed up to 300 civilians in southern Sudan in the past week, in retaliation for their refusal to support the insurgent group, according to a senior Ugandan army spokesperson.
Shaban Bantariza, the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) Director of Information and Public Relations, told IRIN on Friday that the LRA had in the past week been "carrying out ritual killings in different places", similar to the earlier alleged massacre of 60 mourners in the Agoro Mountains, not far from the Ugandan border.
"Kony has been killing in different places," he said. "The numbers are soaring. Right now we know they can't be less than 300."
A Sudanese diplomat in Kampala, the Ugandan capital, and officials of the rebel Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army (SPLM/A), which operates in southern Sudan, have said, however, that they could not verify whether these alleged incidents had actually happened.
George Garang, a spokesman for the SPLM/A in Nairobi, the Kenyan capital, told IRIN on Friday that the movement had little to say by way of clarifying the issue.
"We have no information on what is happening other than that there have been civilian deaths. As to who is killing them, we really don't have any idea," Garang said.
Bantariza said he had on Thursday sent a group of local and international journalists to the areas where the incidents reportedly happened to "verify the truth for themselves". According to him, Kony had turned against the southern Sudanese civilians for refusing to support his movement with food and cattle.
"The simple reason why Kony is doing these things is because he is annoyed and desperate," he said. "The villagers have plenty of food and animals, which Kony wants, but they have refused to sustain a Ugandan rebellion. So he is trying to subdue them and eat their food."
Earlier this week, local and international media organisations reported that, on 26 April, a group of armed LRA fighters had intercepted a funeral procession and shot dead all 60 mourners. Quoting Bantariza, the agencies said the attack had taken place in the Agoro Mountains, which straddle the Sudan-Ugandan border and where the rebels had taken refuge from the Ugandan army offensive, being carried out with permission from Khartoum and dubbed "Operation Iron Fist".
"Kony attacked a funeral procession of about 60 people, forced them to cook the corpse in sorghum and eat it. They thought that they would survive if they obeyed, but the rebels shot and killed all of them after they had eaten the corpse," Reuters quoted Bantariza as saying.
Siraj al-Din Hamid, the charge d'affaires at the Sudanese embassy in Kampala, the Ugandan capital, told IRIN on Tuesday he had not yet received any official response regarding the incident from the Sudanese government.
"I can't confirm it yet," he said. "I only saw reports of it in the newspapers. I haven't yet received any reaction from Khartoum."
"If this is true, it is a horrible and horrendous crime which justifies the [Uganda and Sudanese army] activities against Kony and the LRA," Hamid added.
Until recently - and with support from the Khartoum in retaliation for Kampala's alleged support for the SPLM/A - the LRA has been waging a low-intensity war against President Yoweri Museveni's government in northern Uganda. The war, has resulted in severe humanitarian consequences in northern Uganda, where the LRA has abducted about 12,000 children and caused the displacement of hundreds of thousands of people, according to humanitarian sources.
[see also
However, recent months have seen significant improvements in relations between Sudan and Uganda, resulting in the withdrawal of Khartoum's support for the LRA and the signing of a defence protocol in March this year which has allowed the UPDF to pursue LRA inside Sudanese territory.

Headline: Sudan: Kony Kills 60 in Khartoum
Body_Text:
UNDER pressure from UPDF operations in southern Sudan, Joseph Kony and his Lord's Resistance Army rebels on Friday massacred at least 60 Sudanese who were in a funeral procession of an elder of the village in Agoro mountains, the army said yesterday.
"At least 60 people were shot point-blank after being forced to cut to pieces the corpse they were carrying. Before killing them, Kony rebels surrounded them and ordered them to cook the carcass in sorghum," army spokesman Major Shaban Bantariza said yesterday.
Bantariza said, "The helpless civilians wanted to buy their lives and please Kony by eating the human body. But shortly after being forced to eat at gun point, Kony ordered them killed."
He said Sudanese fleeing from the scene told the UPDF that Kony accused the people in Agoro and Acholi hills of collaborating with the UPDF against him.

Headline: Kenya: Man Jailed for Stealing Sorghum
Body_Text:
Kabale Grade One magistrate, Amos Kwizera Wednesday sentenced John Mureza, a resident of Kabale Municipality to 4 months in prison for stealing two bags of sorghum worth Shs 40,000.
Prosecution said that on Feb.22, Mureza, 27, while at Kigongi A cell in Kabale Municipality with intent to defraud, obtained 2 bags of sorghum from Agnes Busingye promising to pay but he did not.
Mureza pleaded guilty but prayed for leniency saying that he is a first offender.
He was fined Shs 50,000 or 4 months in prison on default. Court also directed him to pay the complainant Shs 40,000 compensation immediately after his sentence.
