The same court acquitted one Kyerere, a resident of Upper Bugongi Kabale Municipality of charges of malicious damage of property.
Prosecution alleged that on Oct 13, 2001 at Rushambya cell in Kabale Municipality, Kyerere unlawfully damaged the fence of Patrick Mbabazi.
He denied the charges and due to lack of evidence, he was acquitted.

Original_Search_Title: SAB's Chips Become a Hostage to Someone Else's Fortune
Headline: South Africa: SAB's Chips Become a Hostage to Someone Else's Fortune
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — It looks very much as though SA Breweries has got itself into a hole.
Probably not a very big financial hole but certainly one that is
potentially embarrassing. The story revolves around its hotel and
casino interests, its known desire to dispose of these and the war
now being waged by its surrogate, Tsogo Sun, and the nascent Rhino
Casino Consortium. Shareholders should use their chips judiciously.
For some years now, SAB has made it clear that it regards itself,
first and foremost, as an international beer maker. It has redoubled
its efforts to expand, notably into Eastern Europe and the Far East
(China) and, by so doing, to reduce its reliance on South Africa. In
the process it has deliberately and systematically sold off many
assets which it now considers "non-core." It is a long, impressive
and sometimes not very well-executed list: OK to Shoprite in 1997
(for R1 together with the liabilities); the unbundling of Afcol and
its merger with Pat Cornick; Levisons and Deans to Esic; Da Gama to
Daun & Cie; Amrel to an institution-led consortium; Lion Match to
Fasic ? all in 1998, a busy year. Scotts, John Orr and Kids First to
Esic; Conshu to Daun; Plate Glass Shatterprufe; the merger of
Traditional Beer with National Sorghum; Ceres to Pioneer Food - all
in 1999.
While all that was going on, SAB listed in London and changed its
domicile to the UK. Bevcon, which held 27% of SAB and was owned by
Johnnic, Anglo American and Liberty Life was unbundled.
Not much that can be described as non-core is left. SAB can
presumably get on with its primary business of brewing and selling
beer around the world. But wait -  there remains the hotel and casino
businesses, all concentrated in this country. In March 2000, SAB's
chief executive, Graham Mackay, said the company was considering
bringing in a strategic partner. This is shorthand for an exit plan.
The problem is that it hasn't happened and, from a reading of the
latest situation, it won't for some time.
As I read it, the problem lies in Tsogo Sun, into which it has pumped
a good deal of cash and intellectual capital and in which it holds a
50% stake. Two years ago, SAB wanted to merge Tsogo with its hotel
interests and list the new company. But that fell apart when the
market turned down. Since then, Merrill Lynch, famous for its immense
internationally-based business, has hawked SAB's hotel and casino
interests in just about every corner. The answer, so far, has been a
fat negative.
SAB's partner in Tsogo is an empowerment consortium led by Fabcos. It
hopes, logically I suppose, that if it waits long enough it will be
able to raise the money it requires to buy SAB's hotel and casino
interests for a bargain basement price.
But Tsogo has problems of its own. Its casinos don't appear to be
flourishing.
Those in Nelspruit and Witbank aren't exactly printing the expected
profits.
Its investment in the Durban casino has been substantially diluted by
Johnnic's successful courtmail actions. The jewel in its crown,
Montecasino in the heartland of Gauteng's affluent suburbs, simply
cost too much - it is said around R1,8bn.
And it is worried about the emergence of a small competitor on the
West Rand, Rhino. In one way or another and with varying degrees of
success, Tsogo has opposed the Rhino licence over the last two years.
When the Gauteng Gambling Board finally issued the Rhino licence -
six years in the making - I warned that the last word hadn't been
heard. Sure enough, Tsogo applied for a judicial review last month. I
now have a copy of its initial pleading - all 800-odd pages of it.
It is a remarkable document on a number of counts, first off for what
it doesn't contain. In a letter written by Tsogo chief executive,
Jabu Mabuza, to the Gambling Board on August 27 last year (omitted
from Tsogo's pleading to the court in which he also admits that
Montecasino isn't offering its gamblers the desired win percentage),
he argued that the Rhino application for a temporary licence in
another shopping centre (Hillfox) "encroaches significantly into our
gaming market."
In any event, Mabuza argued that the distance between Hillfox and the
West Rand communities would reduce the chances of uplifting them. He
thought (then) that further growth would only be "realistically
achievable?with the grant of a licence for a casino on the West Rand
in the location of one of the original sites." The Gambling Board did
as Mabuza asked.
He has now changed his mind it seems. He doesn't want a licence to be
awarded at all on the West Rand. It is quite an about-turn and it
leads to the second issue.
It is that SAB now finds itself hostage to its black empowerment
partner in Tsogo.
The tactic Tsogo is applying is one which, in recent months, I have
dubbed "courtmail" - the process of using law and regulation to
hinder, hamper and harass a competitor. The information available
about the Montecasino operation is that its monthly revenue is
somewhere in the area of R60-R65m. Tsogo believes that Rhino's impact
on Montecasino could be as high as R15m. That is probably a large
chunk of Montecasino's profits.
So no wonder it complains. But the fact is that it was always the
Gauteng Government's intention that a casino should be established on
the West Rand.
It's no good complaining about it now - and, anyway, presumably this
was factored into Tsogo's business plan when it made all those
strenuous efforts to capture the province's premier casino site.
I understand that Tsogo Holdings has approached a variety of
lenders/underwriters with a view to securing the funding it needs to
buy out SAB/Southern Sun. I haven't seen the document on which it
bases its discussions but I hope it says the same thing Tsogo has now
told the court.
The battle between Tsogo and Rhino is one thing. What's more
fascinating is how Tsogo has handed Rhino an unbeatable hand. As long
as it keeps on making Rhino appear the joker in the pack, then for so
long SAB will find it increasingly difficult to shed these "non-core"
interests to an outside investor. Who will buy now with Rhino about
to steal Montecasino's cherry?
It isn't hard to see in all this the quandary facing SAB. It's
wholly-owned subsidiary, Southern Sun, which it wants to get rid of
anyway, is in thrall to its partner in Tsogo. Its partner has cast
covetous eyes on its assets. So, when Tsogo plays the legal card to
the very limit, in whose interests is it acting? Southern Sun's (read
SAB) or that of its black empowerment constituency?
Meanwhile, the Rhino Consortium can sit it out ? if that is what it
wants. Time is of little consequence to its controlling shareholder,
US-listed Century Casinos. It can go the logical route of
highlighting to the court the glaring inconsistencies in Tsogo's
application - though it does have other alternatives which I needn't
spell out.
The sectors to suffer in this argument are SAB's shareholders and the
various West Rand communities which have staked so much on Rhino's
establishment. But I don't suppose anyone will shed tears for them -
after all, they're poor.

Headline: South Africa: "Sinful" Habits Become More Expensive
Body_Text:
Each year, the "sinful" habits, including cigarettes and alcoholic
products are targeted with higher taxes.
For the 2002 fiscal year started at the beginning of March, taxes on
tobacco products will increase by 12%. So a box of 20 cigarettes will
cost an extra 34c a packet, a total excise tax of R3,51 per packet.
Tax on wine goes up by 8%, while that on spirits and sparkling wine
will be 10% greater. By bottle of spirits, the increase is R1,08,
bringing the total tax contribution to R11,84 per 750ml bottle.
Government ultimately wants to fix the tax contribution of alcohol
products as a percentage of their retail price.
Beer drinkers are slightly better off than those that drink spirits ?
tax on beer and cider products go up by 8%, an extra 3,2c a can. For
the lower end of the market, however, the tax on sorghum beer doesn't
go up. This is out of a fear that taxing sorghum could lead to people
consuming more possibly unhealthy substitute products.
But for the teetotaller, there is ever better news. The remaining 6c
duty levied on soft drinks has been abolished (as of 1 April),
resulting in an estimated revenue loss of R135m to Government.

Original_Search_Title: Alcohol And Tobacco Prices Go Up
Headline: South Africa: Alcohol And Tobacco Prices Go Up
Body_Text:
Pretoria — The cost of social habits such as smoking and drinking went up this
afternoon.
Presenting the much-awaited budget speech in Parliament today,
finance minister Trevor Manuel came down hard on these indulgences.
Beer and ciders have been raised by 8 percent, bringing the proposed
excise duty to 43.6 percent per 340 ml can or an average increase of
3.2 cent per can.
However, the price of sorghum beer and sorghum flour will not be
increased.
Last year sorghum beer and sorghum flour duties went by five percent,
which according to Mr Manuel, was below the rate of inflation.
He announced that duties on spirits and sparkling wine would increase
by 10 percent.
'This translates into a total excise of R11.84 per 750ml bottle of
spirits, or a R1.08 increase per bottle,' he explained.
Minister Manuel also announced that taxes on tobacco products would
increase by an average of 12 percent - double the increase last year.
This increases total excise burden to R3.51 per packet of 20
cigarettes, or an increase on 34 cents per packet.
According to the minister, the 'sin taxes' will pay the country
R798-million.
The tax burden on fuel has fallen from an average of 45 percent of
the pump price in 1998 to about 30 percent in 2001.
To encourage environmentally-friendly diesel fuel development, a levy
at 70 percent of the general fuel levy rate would apply to the
consumption of these alternative diesel fuels.

Original_Search_Title: High Court Blesses Share Buy-Back Plan
Headline: South Africa: High Court Blesses Share Buy-Back Plan
Body_Text:
THE Johannesburg High Court has sanctioned the scheme of arrangement in which United National Breweries would be able to buy R46m worth of minority shareholders' interest in the company.
The ruling follows the company's announcement in December that it had offered to buy out its 9000 minority shareholders who held 66% of the company's shares.
The balance is held by India's United Breweries Holdings, which bought into the former National Sorghum Breweries in 1994.
United National Breweries CEO Rajan Ranganathan said yesterday the directors had resolved to offer the buy-back to minorities to provide the large base of small shareholders with an opportunity to realise their investments.
He said the shareholders were being paid out from a trust account since the beginning of the week.
The scheme entailed United National Breweries buying all 46-million shares held by minorities, who included the Premier group, Gensec, the Transvaal Municipal Pension Fund and Nedcor Investment Bank, at 100c a share.
Ranganathan said for the scheme to succeed and get the company on the right footing, United National Breweries had found a strategic partner in Durban-based Marriott Merchant Bank.
With the scheme being approved now, Marriott would subscribe for United National Breweries shares, enabling Marriott to own 25,01% of the issued ordinary share capital. United Breweries Holdings would own 74,99%.
To partly fund the scheme, it was proposed that Nedcor Investment Bank subscribed for the preference shares amounting to R36m.
Ranganathan said earlier that in view of market volatility a listing on the JSE Securities Exchange SA was not feasible. The company showed its first profit, R504000, in the results for the year to December 31 1999. The showing was repeated at the end of 2000 with a profit R18,5m.

Original_Search_Title: 'We Cannot Have a Good Time Without Alcohol'
Headline: Africa: 'We Cannot Have a Good Time Without Alcohol'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Amid the tremendous cultural diversity of the people of Africa, there
may be discerned some consistent trends in alcohol consumption. In
many countries, young males are more likely to drink than young
females.
Presently, the use of alcohol among Africans, on average, is fairly
low compared to the more developed regions. Many young drinkers in
Sub-Saharan Africa believe that drinking is essential to having a
good time, and that their purpose in drinking is to get drunk.
In both genders, the use of alcohol increases with age, says the most
recent WHO Global Status Report entitled Alcohol and Young People
released last October. Current use of alcohol among Africans on
average is fairly low compared to the more developed regions.
Interviews with young drinkers have found, particularly in
Sub-Saharan Africa, that they believe that drinking is essential to
having a good time, and that their purpose in drinking is to get
drunk.
These beliefs, the report says, are reflected in drinking patterns.
In Zimbabwe, for instance, university students who do drink are much
more likely to drink to intoxication than their British counterparts.
There is also some evidence that those drinkers who identify with
Western cultural symbols are more likely to drink heavily.
In Ethiopia, the report states, a 1989 survey of 519 high school
students in the capital city of Addis Ababa found that 9.2 percent
"consumed alcohol heavily" according to individual admission.
The prevalence of current alcohol use among university students in
northwest Ethiopia in 1988 was 31.1 percent. Alcohol was also
frequently used in combination with tobacco and khat.
In Kenya, a 1989 survey report of 2,059 secondary school students
found that 12 percent of males reported drinking chang'aa (local
brew) against three percent and 0.5 percent respectively for females.
A three-year study completed in 1995 of alcohol and other drug use in
secondary schools and teacher training colleges nation-wide surveyed
2,381 students. Of these, 42 percent had taken beer or wine, 31.5
percent had used spirits, and 22 percent had drunk chang'aa.
According to a 1992 baseline health study of households in the
Lesotho Highlands, 16.9 percent of respondents 15 to 29 years of old
reported drinking. Of children between the ages of 5 and 6 some two
percent reported alcohol use.
Traditional beer was the most commonly consumed alcoholic beverage.
The majority of respondents indicated they drank alcohol monthly or
less often, although two children reported daily alcohol use. A
national survey among secondary and high school students reported in
1989 that 24.2 percent of the total sample drank alcoholic beverages.
A 1989 report of an interview survey of 1,133 school students from 11
to 22 years of age indicated that about half of the students (54
percent of the boys and 42 percent of the girls) had drunk alcohol at
some point in their lives.
Drinking was positively associated with age, sex (male), drinking by
friends, higher family income, and drinking in the family, the WHO
report states. No indication of widespread alcohol abuse was found,
but about half of the students believed that moderate drinking is
impossible and that the fun of drinking is to get drunk.
Five studies in Namibia from 1991 to 1992 have reported that 20
percent of school children and 75 percent of out-of-school youth
abuse alcohol over weekends. One of the studies on 12 sixteen-year
olds conducted in Katutura and Khomasdal schoolchildren found that
alcohol was the most used substance by young people.
Of the boys, 22.9 per cent drank alcohol, while 12.6 per cent of
girls reported drinking. Rates increased with age. In addition, 35.5
percent of the young people surveyed agreed with the statement "a
youngster cannot enjoy a party without alcohol".
In Nigeria a number of school and college surveys have found alcohol
use to be common among students, with many drinking students having
that their first drink in family settings.
In June 1988 a questionnaire survey of 636 undergraduate students at
the University of Ilorin in Kwara State (Nigeria) found that 77
percent reported lifetime alcohol use (81 percent of men and 68
percent of women).
In response to a 1988 survey of 1,041 senior secondary school
students in Ilorin, 12 percent reported in 1988 that the preceding
decade had witnessed increasing alcohol use among young people, but
longitudinal data were unavailable to support this. The same study
reported that around the age of eight many respondents had been given
alcohol, usually during family festivities.
In South Africa, during multistage random sampling of 443 black urban
and rural current drinkers aged 10 to 21, it was found that 4.4
percent of urban males, 1.9 percent of urban females, 7.8 percent of
rural males and 1.8 percent of rural females were drinking at risky
levels (defined as an average of 3.4 beers per day, equivalent to
yearly consumption of 25.61 litres of absolute alcohol).
According to the report, a 1991 study of 16 Swaziland secondary and
high schools established that 22 percent of girls and 7.5 percent of
boys stated they were regular drinkers, and 16 percent of girls and
20 percent of boys started drinking by the age of 15.
In 1989, 68 percent of second-form students (about age 13) from three
schools in the working class urban area of Mbabane reported never
drinking alcohol, 25 percent were " rare" drinkers and three percent
said they drank frequently.
A random survey of more than 2,500 Zimbabwean schoolchildren between
the ages of 12 and 21 found that 32 percent of boys and 28 percent of
girls between the ages of 12 and 14 had tried alcohol.
Lifetime prevalence of alcohol use was 31.1 percent for people 14
years of less, 39.5 percent for 15 to 16 years olds, and 46.1 percent
for 17 to 20 years olds. Alcohol was the drug most commonly used by
all the schoolchildren, and was more common among male than female
students, and in private than public schools, largely due to the
socio-economic differences between the two student populations.
Of those 14 years old or less, 7.6 percent had tried alcohol before
they were 10 years old. Students with more Western attitudes and
tastes were likely to exhibit a global cultural orientation, which in
turn was related to greater consumption of alcohol, and of clear,
barley- based, as opposed to traditional sorghum-based, beer.
A comparison made in the early 1980s between Zimbabwe and British
University students found that fewer Zimbabwe (particularly females)
drink than those who do drink more and drink to intoxication
frequently.
The point of the matter is that alcohol of whatever form is a drug,
and the WHO and the rest of humanity abhor drug abuse. Substance
abuse is detrimental to health, especially the health of the youth. u

Original_Search_Title: Court to Scrutinise UNB Buy-Back Offer
Headline: South Africa: Court to Scrutinise UNB Buy-Back Offer
Body_Text:
A HEARING on the offer of a scheme of arrangement to buy out minority shareholders by United National Breweries (UNB), formerly National Sorghum Breweries (NSB), will be heard in the Johannesburg High Court tomorrow.
The court hearing follows the company's announcement in December that it intended to buy out minority shareholders who totalled about 9000, and held 66% of the company's shares.
However, the scheme does not come unchallenged, as an anonymous letter sent to Business Day from a disgruntled shareholder showed .
According to the scheme, UNB was to buy all 46-million shares held by the minority shareholders which included the Premier group, Gensec, the Transvaal Municipal Pension Fund and Nedcor Investment Bank at 100c a share.
The concerned minority shareholder said UNB should be paying more than the 100c that shareholders paid in 1991.
He said that it was unfair for UNB to buy the shares at the same price as 11 years ago, taking into consideration that the company had turned around and was now making profits.
UNB CEO Ranjan Ranganathan said that the scheme was being conducted in accordance with the procedures laid down in the securities regulations code on takeovers and mergers, and that evaluation of the shares was done by Nedcor Investment Bank (NIB) and independent firm Deloitte and Touche.
"In fact our projected earnings a share came to less than R1. This is a fairly reasonable offer to the loyal shareholders who hung in there when the times were tough for the company."
Ranganathan said that if the scheme of arrangement had been done while the company was in red the shareholders would not have received anything because their shareholding was worth nothing at the time.
He said that to avoid putting shareholders under undue risk and further debt, a strategic equity partner had been introduced in the form of Marriott Merchant Bank, a Durban-based financial services company.
On approval of the scheme, Marriott had agreed to subscribe for UNB shares, which entailed it to hold 25,01% of UNB's issued ordinary share capital. United Breweries (UB) Holdings, the Indian brewer that bought into NSB in 1994, would hold the balance.
To partly fund the scheme, it was proposed that NIB subscribed for the preference shares amounting to R36m. Implementation of the scheme would result in UNB purchasing 66% of its issued share capital for a total cash consideration of R45,9m.
On implementation of the scheme and the Marriott subscription, UB Holdings and Marriott would hold 74,99% and 25,01% respectively of UNB's issued ordinary share capital.
"The scheme remains conditional upon the sanction of the court. Any scheme member of UNB is entitled to appear in court and put forward their views on the scheme without necessarily incurring legal expenses," said Ranganathan.

Headline: South Africa: Ties Boost Local Technology Firms
Body_Text:
Providing skills and global expertise
AS the world's largest producer of computer software and a major player in the global information technology arena, India is forging partnerships in SA to boost local skills and expertise.
SA-based Consilience Technologies, operational since March last year, is a joint venture between Tata Africa (part of India's multinational Tata conglomerate) and the J & J group in SA.
This group, founded by Jay and Jayandra Naidoo, has a twothirds shareholding in Consilience.
Tata Africa has nominated Tata Infotech, another of the Tata group of companies in India, to work with Consilience in SA as a technology partner.
Hasmukh Gajjar, CEO of Consilience Technologies, says the company's objective is to become an enterprise systems integrator with a strong thrust on developing a company with local capacity and resources.
He says projects will be implemented through a combination of creating local capacity backed by Tata's project management and know-how.
"Indian companies have a great deal of depth and human resources and bring the best of India's cost-effectiveness and its global experience to the SA environment."
Consilience, which will be targeting both public-sector and corporate clients, has alliances and partnerships with local technology suppliers, including multinationals such as IBM and Microsoft.
In other fields of activity, India's UB Group, with brewery, resort and hotel interests in SA, remains India's largest single investor in the country to date.
United National Breweries (UNB), a separate company within the UB Group, has a 95% share of the formal sorghum beer market in SA, representing about one third of the total SA sorghum beer market.
In 1995 the UB Group bought a 30% stake in the ailing National Sorghum Breweries, effectively taking over the management in 1997.
Since 1999 the company has been operating profitably, says UNB president Rajan Ranganathan. It changed its name to United National Breweries last year.
He says UNB shareholders have approved a scheme to buy out the company's minority shareholders at a fair valuation.
Listing is no longer a feasible option, given the market situation and the difficulties being experienced in the sorghum beer industry.
A buy-back scheme will provide an exit route for minority shareholders who invested in the company in 1991.
This would leave the company with three shareholders the UB Group with a 75% stake, Marriott Merchant Bank, with 25% of the equity and Nedcor Bank continuing to hold its preferred share.
Tourism between India and SA is another growth industry.
Yogesh Jain, director of the India tourist office in Johannesburg, says that between January and October last year an estimated 17000 South Africans visited India, while more than 22000 visitors travelled to SA from India.
About 90% of all visitors said they had travelled for holiday purposes.
Jain says South Africans will find products and services in India to be reasonably priced.
He says India offers an alternative destination with the richness of its cultural and scenic diversity and value for money.

Headline: Rwanda: Leaders Hopeful for Peace in 2002
Body_Text:
Presidents Paul Kagame of Rwanda, Joseph Kabila of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Pierre Buyoya of Burundi have ushered in the New Year with messages of peace and reconciliation.
At the inauguration of a new flag, anthem and emblem in Rwanda on 31 December, Kagame called upon Rwandans to seek what could unite them in the search for patriotism.
By contrast with the old Rwandan flag, which was predominantly red and featured a pruning knife, heavily reminiscent of the 1994 genocide, the new flag features horizontal green, yellow and blue stripes with a yellow sun in the right hand corner.
The colours represent hope for prosperity, work, peace and happiness, while the sun stands for unity, transparency and the fight against ignorance. By contrast with the old national anthem, and in keeping with the official Rwandan policy of not favouring any particular ethnic group, the new anthem makes no reference to Hutus, Tutsis and Twas, the country's three main ethnic groups.
On a similar note, the country's new emblem - an ear of sorghum and a coffee branch, symbolising prosperity, and two shields symbolising patriotism - features the slogan "Unity, Work, Patriotism".
In his new year's message, Kabila told his countrymen that 2002 would be a year of peace and reconciliation. Referring to the areas of the country under foreign control, he hailed his people's spirit of resistance, and said the DRC would continue to fight for sovereignty and territorial unity so that "all will be able to circulate freely...and hold the much-loved fundamental values of freedom and unity for the Congo".
In a mixed message, Buyoya hoped for peace and economic development in Burundi in 2002. He invited all Burundians to become involved in the process of restoring peace, saying that the process had reached the point of no return with the establishment of a transitional government. Despite using the word "peace" 15 times in his speech, he added that 2002 would also be marked by the fight against "the enemy", and that no territory would be under rebel control even if assisted by negative forces in the subregion.
Meanwhile, Burundian presidential adviser Colonel Leonidas Nijimbere told IRIN that the government hoped to hold cease-fire talks by the end of January. Previous attempts to convene the talks had been "bogged down by disagreement" between Gabon and South Africa on the venue and date, a Burundian political analyst, who asked not to be named, told IRIN. Gabonese President Omar Bongo and South African Deputy President Jacob Zuma are directly involved in the cease-fire mediation process.
Before Pierre Nkurunziza ousted Jean-Bosco Ndayikengurukiye as leader of the Conseil Nationale pour la defense de la democratie-Forces pour la defense de la democratie (CNDD-FDD), Ndayikengurukiye had sought Gabon's participation. However, the analyst added, Jean Minani's faction of Frodebu (the pro-Hutu group, Front pour la democratie au Burundi), which supports Nkurunziza, did not want Gabon as the venue, because this would lead to Jean Bosco's participation in the talks.
"We would like Gabon to quit the mediation process," an aide to Jean Minani told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Traders Want Security Assistance to Retrieve Trapped Goods
Headline: Nigeria: Yam Traders Want Security Assistance to Retrieve Trapped Goods
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yam dealers in Plateau State have sent a Save Our Souls (SOS) plea to the state government to assist to retrieve their trapped goods, particularly yams, in the troubled spots of the state.
A spokesman for the dealers, Mallam Salisu Nayalwa, told journalists in Jos,the Plateau State capital, that their yams and other commodities worth over N20 million have remained trapped in Shendam, Longtang North, South and Klasa council areas.
He explained that they fled for their lives, living behind their properties and goods when hostilities broke out in villages they went to purchase goods.
Nayalwa said they need the state government assistance to retrieve the goods because it is unsafe for them to go back to the villages with security escorts with killings still going on in some of the places.
He named villages still involved in fighting as Mabudi, Wadata, Dadin Kowa and Gwiwar Kogi. He said while normalcy seemed to have returned to the headquarters of the councils areas, some of the towns were still being ravaged by Fulani and Taroh militias.He appealed to security agents to assist them, pointing out that their goods were being destroyed by the warring tribesmen.

Headline: Uganda: Gazette Nakivubo Swamp
Body_Text:
THE MINISTER of State for the Environment has warned that continuing encroachment on the Nakivubo wetland is threatening the health of Kampala residents.
The wetland has shrunk from 4.8 square kilometres to 1.3 square kilometres. Yet even this remaining area is under assault from slum dwellings, KCC plot al__cpLocations, and the new Nakivubo channel that has brought the point of discharge closer to Lake Victoria.
Matters have been made worse by illegal cultivation of yams as they cannot retain water like papyrus.
The Nakivubo or Bugolobi swamp filters all the sewage from National Water and Sewage Corporation as well as receiving often-polluted drainage from Kampala's industry and road run-off.
The decline of Nakivubo swamp could eventually be very costly to Kampala. Already the NWSC is saying that an additional $10 million per year in chemicals will be needed to purify Kampala's drinking water because of the reduction in size of the swamp.
However all is not lost and it is not too late to rectify matters.
The Ministry of Lands, Water and Environment are making an excellent start by preparing to declare the swamp a protected area. However the legal instrument gazetting the swamp must include all areas of existing illegal encroachment.
The ministry must go one step further and organise for the Nakivubo channel to discharge through multiple reticulated outlets into the swamp. This will reduce the pressure on the swamp and make it easier for the swamp to absorb and retain water. The cost will be minimal compared to the annual extra cost of chemicals for NWSC if the modifications are not made.

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Lagos — Questions why the police are holding on to the corpse six years after she was allegedly killed by a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, wants killer cop brought to justice
She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary last weekend: "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advice of the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Nigeria: One More Job for Balogun
Body_Text:
Lagos — As Mr. Tafa Balogun, the newly appointed Inspector-General of Police settles down to ensure the provision of security of lives and property in Nigeria, he has an urgent assignment in Osun State, from where he hails.
Fortunately the job at hand does not need any tedious preparation that logistics could hinder. It is not an assignment to chase armed robbers so there is no need to complain that the enemy has more sophisticated weapons. The assignment only needs to be carried out with a human face as a way to re-invigorate the public confidence in the Nigerian Police.
The nature of the assignment to be carried out in Osun State this time around would require only services and not force and such service will bring more glory to the police than a thousand armed policemen using force could accomplish. The assignment has to do with an incident of May 27, 2000 at Ilesa, in osun State. Not many Nigerians who were aware of the incident would remember now but the victim of the ugly police brutality is still going about in pain both mentally and physically.
It is the case of Mr. Joshua Olumakinde Siyanbola, a welder with Abuletagba Social Equipment Rental Services, who was shot by a now dismissed policeman named Oluwatoyin. The story, according to the victim, who now walks on an artificial leg goes thus. Siyanbola was returning from work at about 1pm on a Saturday. The poor welder in a pair of jeans trousers and a white long sleeve shirt was carrying a polythene bag containing yam porridge which he bought for his family at home. Since he did not have enough money, he had to trek. Perhaps the spirit of the Children's Day Celebration made him to think of taking something extra home for his children. So when reached the Okesa area of the town he stopped. The place does not only host a branch of Afribank, it also accommodates a lot of petty traders. The women sell roasted yam, corn, banana as well as sweets, kolanuts and some other related items. Siyanbola decided to buy N5 worth of biscuit out of the N15 he had on him. Just as he took the decision to make his children happy, a figure in an "Agbada" attire landed in front of him and identified himself as a policeman. Siyanbola, who was initially agitated by the sudden appearance of this figure, was relieved when he discovered that he was a person put in charge of security. Even if his sudden "visitor" was an armed robber or thief, Siyanbola thought he would not have lost anything spectacular except his porridge and perhaps, the remaining N10 and sachet of biscuit. Besides, no armed robber really chase cheap things like those he was carrying so he listened to the policeman.
The cop promptly ordered him to disclose what was in the bag. When emptied only the yam porridge and a piece of cloth could be found. It was re-packed and the policeman asked how much Joshua had to which he replied N10. Though disappointed by that meagre amount, the cop still felt "half a loaf is better than none" and demanded for the N10 but the man would not let it go. According to him, he tried to convince the policeman that N10 was too small for a very important personality like him and that he would bring a "reasonable" amount the following week. To convince the cop that he meant business, he said many people in the area knew him and as such he would come and see him as promised.
However, Oluwatoyin would not take any of such "pranks" as he thought Joshua was playing. he threatened to shoot if he failed to release the N10. Siyanbola had to think over it. He had only N10 and if he should release the money, it would mean that aside the porridge, there would be nothing again to eat for the day. However, the wish to stay alive prevailed and according to him, as he was trying to take the money from his pocket, his uninvited visitor pulled the trigger and pumped bullet into his left leg. The assailant took to his heels and the traders who were mostly women, nearby also ran for their lives. The victim who said he was shot around 1pm was not taken to the hospital until about 3pm. The cop had gone back to the police station to report that he shot a thief. By the time his colleagues got to the scene, they discovered that the Joshua was not a thief but a popular man also known as "Pappy" in the area. Afribank donated N10,000 the following Monday and the police at Ijamo station bought some drugs.
The leg was eventually amputated and the erring cop dismissed. After walking on crutches for more than a year, the Rotary Club of Ilesa bought an artificial limb for Siyanbola at a cost he put at N150,000 and he said he spent about N50,000 myself from donations I received. It became a serious media issue not only at home but also at the international community.
Prior to his retirement, Mr. Musiliu Smith, Balogun's predecessor, according to Siyanbola, sent emissaries to visit him with promises to help "but up till now, no money had come from the police and I have to feed myself".
He further told THISDAY that: "I appreciate all that people have been doing for me to appeal to the police. The lawmakers went to Abuja because of this but there was no result. Before Chief Bola Ige died, the Alliance for Democracy (AD) Senate Leader, Chief Mojisoluwa Akinfenwa, took my case to him but to no avail".
Siyanbola lamented, further that "I have been living on peoples goodwill and donation" and added that he had to withdraw his two kids from school.
He also claimed to have approached the state governor, Chief Bisi Akande directly during a meet-the-people programme at Ifewara "but the governor told me that he was not in charge of the police and that it was the Federal Government. He equally said he would refer my case to the social welfare department but up till now I have not seen any help".
"My hope is now on God and the police to compensate me for being a victim of gunshot without any offence. I cannot blame the governor. It is true that the police belongs to the Federal Government but I will appreciate help from anywhere it comes" , he said.
Siyanbola concluded that he was advised to hire a lawyer. "But I have no money to do that, I only want to appeal to police authorities to bail me out of my financial mess" adding "I learnt the Sergeant that shot me has been dismissed but how will that benefit me and put food on the table. How will my children go back to school with his dismissal?"

Headline: Nigeria: Witness to Bedlam
Body_Text:
Nigerians do not know how to behave when disaster, natural or man-made, strikes because we have never been taught what to do in situations like this. Hundreds of people died because they did not know what to do other than to run
It promised to be an interesting evening at Onigbongbo, a small high density settlement by the army cantonment, Ikeja,   Lagos  . Oscars was hosting the monthly Ikpilogwu family union meeting on Sunday, January 27. We had been served pounded yam with a particularly delicious ukpo/abasi soup. Obande, the president of the union called the meeting to order. Kola nuts were broken and served. The meeting was committed to God and to the gods - the latter being our ancestors - to make sure nothing went amiss. We settled down to business. Oscars read the minutes of the previous meeting. Contents debated and approved. The promise of the evening was about to be broken. We suspected nothing.
At about  5.15 p.m. we began to hear what sounded like an exchange of small arms fire. Not a strange sound in   Lagos  . One often hears that kind of sound when either armed robbers are operating or are engaged in a shoot out with the police. It was coming from around Oshodi, not far away from where we were. We dismissed the sound and got on with the meeting. The sound did not go away. It became louder and more diffused. Evidence, we thought, that the police and the armed robbers were probably evenly matched gun for gun and neither side was willing to surrender to the other. We were wrong. Proof of that came soon.
A loud blast. The kind of sound that gives you the momentary impression that your head has been blown off. We were jolted. From the room, we could see the cantonment. Skido pointed out that some women in the barracks were running. All of us crowded in the doorway. No one was running. Or more correctly, we did not yet see them. Some children and women with babies strapped to their backs were more or less strolling in the barracks. We went back to business.
Oche, a serving soldier, had a worried look on his face. Something told him we were wrong in our assumption. He was going to the barracks to put on his uniform, he said. He was not to be caught dead in mufti. We persuaded him that it certainly was not a military activity. He sat back, unwilling to argue further but clearly unconvinced by us.
Not quite three minutes after that, there was a particularly loud blast. Never heard anything like it before. The house shook to its foundation. No rain of bullets. Some luck. This was getting weird. We rushed down to the street. The narrow street was already crowded with people who had poured out from their houses. Questions were asked of no one in particular. Then we saw the thick black smoke billowing from what appeared from where we were to be the back of the army barracks. Trouble had come in black smoke. Soon the street was teaming with anxious men, women and children. Fear and confusion were about to take over, shattering the peace of the evening and ending the meeting. Think of all the cold beer we were about to miss. Ye gods.
No one knew what was happening. Everyone knew there was trouble. Eshi, a serving soldier, offered the view that the armoury had been bombed. That meant bigger trouble. Probably, enemies or dissident soldiers had taken on the military establishment. He quickly assessed the situation and concluded that Onigbongbo was no longer safe for us. It was evident. We could see hundreds of people running towards the gate of the army barracks. Skido offered to drive me home. As he fumbled with the security lock of his car, another loud bang shook the neighbourhood. We got in. He drove by his house in the neighbourhood. His wife, carrying their little daughter, ran to meet us on the road. Skido told her to run with the neighbours if they were running. Our mistake. We realised that rather late.
We drove to   Ikorodu Road  . Skido is a young man with some pluck. He decided he would take me home through Oshodi. I said it was dangerous. The blasts were coming from that direction. No problem, he said. Another big bang settled the argument. We headed for Ojota on the wrong side of the road. At the best of times, we, Lagosians, do not obey traffic rules. On this day, my driver, Isiaka, would have been shocked to find that even I did not remember that there were traffic rules left in the books.
Bedlam. Chaos. Not a pretty sight. Hundreds of people had already crowded every inch of the road. Some were running. Some commercial drivers, too shaken to hold the steering, did the most sensible thing. They simply jumped out of their vehicles, contributing to the madness and the traffic bottleneck.
An opportunity for a quick naira or two presented itself in the midst of the rather helpless situation. From nowhere, in my untutored view, some young men emerged with planks. I soon saw why. A motorist wishing to cross from one side of the road to the other needed the planks to help him climb the road divider. He paid the young men and was soon on his way across the road. Several motorists needed and used the services of the plank men.
At Ojota, the first wave of guilt hit me. Here was I being spirited to safety by Skido who had left his family behind. He groaned and said we made a mistake by not taking his family away with us. I agreed. We were helpless. We could not turn back. The road was clogged. It was getting progressively worse back where we came from. The bombs were now in full and frightful blast. We were stuck. No movement. Only panic and confusion. Women were running everywhere, shouting, weeping and asking to jump into any available vehicles. They took off their head scarves and their spare wrappers as if not wearing them would quicken their escape from the looming trouble. At the moment, these prized items of a beautiful Sunday outing had become liabilities. They had to be discarded.
Again, Skido remembered his family he had unwisely left behind at Onigbongbo. He groaned the groan of a man caught up in a bad and unforgivable mistake. I too groaned the groan of the guilty. Because of me he left them behind. What if .? Perish the thought.
People were simply running, well, helter skelter. They did not quite know where to. They only knew there was trouble. Instinct told all of us that if there was trouble, the most sensible thing to do was to run. Problem was, we did not know where the trouble really was and how widespread it was in the metropolis. No time to ask questions. Just run. If your shoes or slippers or sandals impeded your speed, discard them. Many did, littering the roads with evidence of panic. If your car was held in traffic and you could not move fast enough from trouble, abandon it. Many did. Evidence that the car can, sometimes, be a hindrance rather than help in certain circumstances. There comes a time when the feet earn the trust of their owners. Weep not, you who own no cars of your own. Unfortunately, some of those who abandoned their cars returned the next day to find their condition totally different from what they left behind. Thieves had cashed in on the situation. How awfully sad.
We tried to make contact with family and friends with the mobile phone. No use. Network failure was all the help we could get when we needed the wonders of the GSM mobile phone most. Some radio stations were off the air. Everyone was frantic. Some information just so our worst fears were not confirmed. None came. No one told anyone anything. Where were the police and military authorities? We wondered in vain. No information. No help. Nothing. Sometimes, help is tardy in its own funny way. We stewed in our panic and confusion. We just kept moving to wherever we felt was a long way from trouble.
Which was how Ekong made it to a white garment church in Otta. Quite a distance from his residence in Mafoluku. He did not know how he got there but once there, he trusted God to deliver him from whoever was making trouble for the country.
A loud blast. A man jumped out of his car. He left the engine running. Another blast. So close. No rain of bullets. We looked around to see who or what was hit. Nothing. The crowd changed direction at the command of no one; briefly faced where the smoking billowed to the sky and then changed direction again, facing Ketu.
We drove on to   Kudirat Abiola Way  , Oregun. Some volunteers, as they so often do in   Lagos  , had taken it upon themselves to bring some sanity to the traffic because no motorist was willing to concede an inch of space to the other. Ironical. Escape consists in movement, not in standing still. Wrong time to preach, wrong people to preach to.
We managed to get through and on to Agidingbi. Same story. A big blast. The houses in the neighbourhood rattled. More people poured to the street. No lone rangers in this confusion. Everyone simply followed where the mass of people flowed. The majority could not be wrong. Even in confusion, we instinctively practise democracy in   Nigeria  .
Something worth recalling. Despite the chaotic traffic situation, there was a surprising abundance of civility among the motorists. Lagosians usually leave their homes and offices fully armed each day with imprecations which they haul at one another at the slightest provocation. This evening, no curses, no swear words. Panic and the looming shadow of the unknown had engendered in us a new spirit of civility and even politeness. It raised the hope that on the day of Armageddon, no Lagosians would be caught dead with their daily ration of imprecations. It takes a crisis.
On to Ogba, Oba Akran,   Agege Motor Road  , local airport and then to the   International Airport Road  . The big blasts had subsided. It has been some one hour and forty-five minutes since they started. The rumbling continued. At the toll gate between the local airport and the international airport, children aged between five and 10, probably returning from a Sunday outing, spread themselves on the ground, tired from head to toe. The vehicles that brought them were parked by the roadside. Some adults stood by the vehicles. They looked vacant. They did not know what was happening. They thought it wise to simply stop and wait. They were wise.
Albert was at a naming ceremony at Otta. Everyone was blissfully unaware of what was happening in   Lagos  . Until a strange man ran into them, asking for cold water. They offered him a sachet of pure water. He drank some and poured the rest on his head. Everyone waited. He then told them that half of   Lagos  had been destroyed by bombs. He escaped all the way on foot to Otta. The long distance runner had won but he had misled the people as so many other people did in phone-in radio programmes this evening of all evenings.
Talk of an evening that promised to be interesting. Some evening!
In the panic and the confusion and the tragedy of January 27, two things were evident. The first is that we all appear to be sitting on the edge of our chairs, anticipating a military take over. That, indeed, was the conclusion of most people. Some radio stations reportedly, rather thoughtlessly that the cantonment  been bombed and that the alleged military action was spreading to other parts of the city pretty fast. Some foreign news media simply added two and two together and got their answer:   Nigeria  was up in political flames again.
We were stuck in the traffic on   Lateef Jakande Road  , Agidingbi, Ikeja. A beat up taxi, fully loaded beyond its capacity, drew level with us. Were it not on a day like this, he would have  paid a toll through his nose to the police or the traffic warden. I heard the driver say: "See now, dem say God no dey answer poor man him prayer. God don answer now. All dis Obasanjo dem jolly-jolly don finish." I found that shocking.
It was worse than cynical. None of us should find it funny. Even when Governor Bola Tinubu of   Lagos  State  appeared on television with the commander of the 9th brigade, not a few people immediately concluded the governor had been arrested by soldiers. One must commend Tinubu. His timely appearance on the tube reassured all of us that the rain of bombs was an accident.
The second point, which is no less worrisome, is that Nigerians do not know how to behave when disaster, natural or man-made, strikes because we have never been taught what to do in situations like this. Hundreds of people died because they did not know what to do other than to run. Running anywhere was better than standing still to be probably picked up like a sitting duck. But it was a grievous mistake and grievously hundreds of men, women and children paid for it. The federal government hardly needs to be persuaded that it is time to do something about this. The sooner, the better. But of course, when the enquiry by the military is over, nothing will change. Call it the curse of indifference.
Newswatch Volume 35 No 6, February 11, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Ethnic Cleansing: Hausa And Fulani in Dilemma
Headline: Nigeria: Ethnic Cleansing: Hausa And Fulani in Dilemma
Body_Text:
The incessant cases of inter-tribal wars being reported at the Taraba/Benue border, that led to the killing of 22 soldiers recently and the Mambila /Fulani war in the Mambila plateau in Taraba State, and many others here in the North that have been recurring from time to time can be traced historically.
While the various governments are trampling on the Hausa/Fulani year after year from 1904 to date, the Christian missionaries are and have all this long been concentrating on the so-called emancipation of the tribes/cum minorities or the Habe districts of the various emirates of the North. First by Christianising them, educating them and economically empowering them with white -cola jobs, in government, in the church and non-governmental organisations. As a result, polluting the minds of the beneficiaries with the notion that the Hausa/Fulani are the people that have denied them all this goodies all along and are not happy that they now could enjoy it.
The current ethnocentric cleansings going on around the nation in which the OPC aims at getting rid of all other Nigerians in Yoruba land, whereas the Bakassi Boys in the east do not want to see any non Ibo selling even tea in the streets of Onitsha and the Egbesu boys destroying the property of genuine investors, the Northern part of the country had no option but to act, and when doing so, contrary to all the evil machinations of the others, the North instead, formed the Arewa Consultative Forum not another terrorist group like all the other regions.
The forum facilitates dialogue within which we relate and cool surging tempers and boiling cauldrons in the region of the North, as led by a gentle man in the person of Alhaji M. D. Yusufu. With the above set-up in motion, some disgruntled ingrates amongst us are still fuelling fires of hate, malaise and ethnic love- lost, causing disruptions to harmony and peace as depicted in Tiv land, Adara and Mambila in Benue, Nasarawa and Taraba states of recent.
While in Benue, Taraba and Nasarawa borders we blame the crises on natural phenomenon, in the sense that, historically, the Tiv being one of the last tribes to arrive on Benue river basin from Congo could not have enough time to establish itself in the basin, and had to continue pushing further into hinterlands of Taraba, Nasarawa and Niger for more land for their yams, beniseed and cassava, this is being resisted by the earlier occupants of same lands in all directions. First Taraba then Nasarawa and soon Niger (as can be seen of Tiv settlements between Gawu-Babangida and Lapai on the road to Lagos from Abuja).
In Mambila plateau in Taraba state, the onus of the matter is the mistake of the Hausa/Fulani in the attempt to settle on the plains of the plateau controlling so much wealth and being in the minority in terms of population, this imbalance created so much envy among the surrounding tribes that they will never allow the Fulani to live in peace until they annihilate the last of them and their vast wealth (hence the killing of all prosperous businessmen any time there is a riot up on the plateau.)
I am convinced that as long as there are communal and religious crises in the federation in this political dispensation, right from the OPC onslaught on Hausa traders in Ketu and on and on except the carnage in Ile Ife and Ogoniland all the others are targeted at the Hausa/Fulani. Either in Zango, Kaduna, Ilorin, Tafawa Balewa, and Kaltungo or in Mambila the victim is always the Hausa/Fulani, irrespective of the colouring the press and government might give the incident and situation.
I here submit that we (the Hausa/Fulani) are neither in power nor near it in any state that we are not in total control and majority, the president today is southern Yoruba man and his vice a tribe from Adamawa state, the Senate President an Ibo man, with our brother Ghali in the seat of Speaker of the House of Representative, even that was not usurped by our majority but was democratically allocated to us by the ruling party.
The individual wealth and various bounties of God in the hands of the Hausa and Fulani man in this country was not by statutory al__cpLocation but the result of hundreds of years of trading right from the historic trans-Saharan trade to modern IT marketing. Please we call on Nigerians irrespective of tribe to endeavour to engage in economic activities and stop perpetrating troubles that can only draw us back the more.

Headline: Nigeria: Understanding the American Mindset
Body_Text:
Wisdom is contained in books. Those who wish to cultivate their intellect and have a very sound mind ought to read always and widely. This is the advice my late friend, Kenule Boeson Saro-Wiwa, a great lover of books himself, used to give to anyone with whom he came in touch, especially if you were a journalist.
"Your profession is an honourable one, and it demands knowledge, courage and curiosity. The pen is mightier than the sword but the pen that wants to be taken seriously has to be well-informed, to have a sense of history and to be of impeccable character".
That again was another of Mr. Saro-Wiwa's advice to journalists.
Even before I got to know Mr. Saro-Wiwa, I used to love books and read them a great deal. What the counsel from Ken did for me was to merely reinforce my love for books.
In pursuit of this first great love, I have just devoured a 789-page tome entitled President Kenney: Profile of Power by Richard Reeves, a most delightful and unputdownable book.
President Kennedy is a brilliant attempt to reconstruct the 1000 days John F. Kennedy, the 35th president of the United States presided over the affairs of that country.
A particular book may minister different things to each reader. To me, this book is a window unto the mind of the American society. It tells me very clearly that Americans are deeply tormented by fear, an acute fear of being thought weak.
Americans are so consumed by their inflated opinion of their place in the world that they are constantly gnawed by fear. Like Okonkwo in Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart, the whole life of the average American is ruled by the fear of his country not being thought as powerful enough.
Americans have been brought up to believe that theirs is the most powerful country of the modern era, the leader and defender of the "Free World", the policeman of the universe.
This kind of thought is deeply buried in the psyche of every American. It is clear to me that every American leader is aware of this psychological make-up of his average citizen and plays on this fear to achieve his political goal.
I had a great laugh when I read of President Kennedy telling his Director of Budget that it was inappropriate to use words like "aid" and "assistance". Better to use "International Security" instead of "aid" and "assistance", "International Security" instead of International Development".
"Strengthen the security of the Free World" was a phrase Kennedy liked because Americans did not like the idea of foreign aid, but were willing to pay for almost anything that sounded like "anti-communism".
What is this column up to today? Is it out to review a profile of Kennedy's enduringly fascinating days in office? Our aim today is to help us understand why President George W. Bush and his fellow hawks in his administration are so bent on attacking Iraq with a twin objective of effecting a "regime change" in that war-tormented country in spite of the near universal disapproval of the rest of the world.
From what Richard Reeves has shown in the book on Kennedy and from what we have known of the United States over the years, foreign wars are a great domestic votes catcher in America. Once the country is engaged in a foreign war, the voters rally round the president.
Kennedy was himself amazed by this trait in the American. Based on the urgings of some of his hawkish aides, he ordered the infamous Bay of Pigs invasion of Cuba. The invasion failed but rather than turn against their president, the Americans, strangely, rallied around him. Kennedy's rating in an opinion poll soared and he wryly remarked: "The worse you do, the better they like you".
Presidents are politicians who are aware that the voter is king. It is the voter who determines whether a politician remains relevant or not. Most American presidents therefore pander to the yearnings of voters most of who do not know the wider implications of war.
Apart from the politician's need to woo voters, arms manufacturers and arms contractors also actively promote the cause of war. Without wars they will be out of business.
There are of course, also naÃ¯ve intellectuals and hawkish advisers who operate from the comfort of air conditioned offices, far removed from the jungles and other theatres of war, who draw up beautiful scenarios of how a smart bomb can take out a "recalcitrant leader" of an unfriendly country in a short and sharp operation.
Military generals some of who will never go to the war front, are another group of persons who are most vocal in calling for war. It is this group that urged Kennedy on to other disastrous Bay of Pigs invasions. It is a combination of these armchair war mongers who persuaded him to pour in men, arms and materiel into South Vietnam, a place in South East Asia that became the graveyard of America's innocence.
Kennedy was a man with a first rate mind. In as much as he was motivated by the need to act tough in order to please voters, he also knew that if he did not act with tact and confidence, his armchair cheerleaders would urge him onto a course that would bring catastrophe on humanity. He once told his close aides that "if we listen to them (naÃ¯ve generals), and do what they want us to do, none of us will be alive later to tell them that they were wrong".
I have gone to great lengths to recount some of these incidents to give us an insight into why Bush's America is calling for war against the so-called "Axis of Evil". It is also to warn Mr. Bush that he may lead his Coalition Against Terrorism into an unwinable war against especially Iraq, a war that could imperil the whole of humanity.
Bush may be tormented by the fact that in the eyes of many Americans and the rest of the world, he is seen as a usurper, he did not really win the presidency fair and square. He may therefore be pushed to engage in a daring move to prove that he is a tough man and a great president.
It is unfortunate that over the years, American presidents love to play politics with the lives of their citizens and the lives of innocent citizens of other countries thousands of miles away. At some point during the Vietnam War, some callous war strategists urged Kennedy to order "human defoliation" of the innocent citizens of Vietnam and he did!
Even more callous was the fact that the president ordered the use of herbicide defoliants - commercial weed killers containing cacodylic acid and several kinds of butyl - sprayed from US planes to clear underbrush within two hundred feet of roads through ambush country.
Food denial was always employed as a weapon of war. The weed killer was used to destroy fields of rice, manioc, corn and sweet potatoes in territories held by the Vietnamese communists.
Greenery was destroyed in over 32,000 square miles of South Vietnam, almost half the country. What cruelty, what callousness.
Millions of live have been lost in the quest of the United States to dominate this past century. Even without waiting for anyone to award them the honour, Americans crowned themselves victors of that century. Remember, Henry Luce, the founder of Time magazine had predicted that the 21st century was America's and it had it.
To what extent will simple folks of other nations be made to suffer so that America can again possess the 22nd century? Has not the world borne enough agonies because of America's insatiable appetite to be the leader of the Free World?
How indeed free is the "Free World" when people are not allowed the right to live their lives free from interference by a "big brother protector"? In the past 12 years, citizens of Iraq have lived under the permanent threat of bombs, air raids and crippling economic sanctions.
It does appear that Bush and the hawks in his administration will not be dissuaded from going to war against Iraq. I think the best we can do in the circumstance is to tell him that just as Operation Desert Storm I cost his father re-election, Operation Desert Storm II will also provide a death nail on his political coffin.
For those who keep wondering why the Americans seem to like war so much, they should now understand that foreign wars are a good tool for domestic political success.

Original_Search_Title: No Christmas to Celebrate This Year
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Christmas to Celebrate This Year
Body_Text:
At the rate with which things are deteriorating in Zimbabwe, I doubt if we will celebrate Christmas at the end of the year. Christmas, as far as the majority of Zimbabweans are concerned, is about having lots of bread, cake, drinks, tea and travelling to the rural areas.
A number of things on this list are in short supply, not to mention that even money is a scarce resource for most of us. There won't be any bread. There will be black tea without sugar (not that one will be on diet but because it's the order of the day in the new Zimbabwe, with the advent of the Third Chimurenga).
As if this is not enough, there won't be any cake since there is no flour. You cannot even bake maize-meal cakes, as there is no maize-meal. How are we supposed to brew beer without chimera (sorghum)?
Speaking of beer, who will think of it on an empty stomach when there is no sadza?
And for those lucky few with maize-meal, whether by chikwambo che Zanu (the party card) or by mere Godly blessings, what relish will they cook, seeing as the price of beef has since gone up. Thinking of buying chicken is being ambitious.
And by the way, how will I travel to the rural areas without any fuel in my sikorokoro (jalopy)? Seeing that spare parts are difficult to come by, I have virtually ignored the condition of my car, risking my life. As long as I can get fuel my vehicle can move, at least.
How about my brother who has to travel to Chipinge by bus but only earns $7 000 dollars working as a gardener. How will he get to the rural areas?
Given all these woes, how can we celebrate Christmas? And by the way, there are no new clothes for the children. At least we, the adults, understand enough to forego new clothes.
Whether Zanu PF or MDC, the message is kana yasvika nguva muchataura zvenyu. (When the time comes, you will admit it).
Abigail Shava Budiriro 3

Original_Search_Title: Jackie, the Judge And the Jailbird
Headline: South Africa: Jackie, the Judge And the Jailbird
Body_Text:
Reputations are at risk as a dispute between business factions heats  up.
A high court judge and the national police chief both face the heat in  a blazing row over a R99-million airports security contract. Accusing  both is a well-connected drugs convict who earlier partnered the judge in  business.
Cape High Court Judge Jerome Ngwenya is accused by the convict of  having benefited from "stolen" shares. Judge Ngwenya's standing may be  compromised by his earlier business relationship with the convict, who was at the  time a fugitive from justice.
National police Commissioner Jackie Selebi is accused by the convict of having ordered his belated arrest as a favour to the judge's brother,  Noel
Ngwenya. This was after the business relationship between both Ngwenyas  and the convict went sour. Selebi's standing may be compromised by his  alleged friendship with Noel Ngwenya, who was arrested last week on multiple  fraud counts and whose security company is subject to a multimillion-rand tax investigation.
The convict is Vuyo Ndzeku, related by marriage to Minister of Housing
Sankie Mthembi-Mahanyele and a long-time business partner of Lungi  Sisulu, son of struggle veterans Walter and Albertina Sisulu. Ndzeku was  arrested in
1988 for mandrax dealing but served his sentence only in 2000. He  maintains the mandrax charge was a set-up, but does not deny earlier links with  the drugs underworld. He said this week: "I am not scared of [Selebi]."
Selebi confirms personal involvement in the saga, but denies his relationship with Noel Ngwenya is as close as suggested or that it influenced his actions: "He is an acquaintance not, you know, a  buddy."
Judge Ngwenya, denying Ndzeku's charges, comments: "Had I time on my  side, I would have written a short novel about Ndzeku."
The dispute is remarkable for the way it has pitted politically  connected business factions against each other. It came to a head last November  when the Airports Company of South Africa (Acsa) cancelled Khuselani  Security and
Risk Management's contract to provide security at the country's major airports, claiming Khuselani had not performed adequately. Noel Ngwenya  is chief executive of Khuselani.
The contract, worth R99-million over three years, was awarded 18 months earlier and its premature termination remains the subject of  arbitration proceedings. But Khuselani's axing unleashed a torrent of whispered allegations against opponents - of corruption and worse, but hard to  prove - from sources allied to both camps.
The Mail & Guardian reported at the time that, days after Acsa  cancelled
Khuselani's contract, Acsa chairperson and prominent businessman  Mashudu
Ramano was hauled in by police who questioned his citizenship. It is tempting to regard the police action as harassment, since no charges  were formulated against Ramano afterwards.
Selebi was accused by Acsa officials at the time of having pressured  them not to terminate his "friend" Noel Ngwenya's contract. Selebi was  quoted responding that police "top management suggested [to Acsa] that it  would make sense to keep the incumbents in place until such time as everybody  had been checked out and other measures had been put in place".
Now the long arm of the law is reaching in the opposite direction,  driven primarily by tax officials. The Sunday Times reported at the weekend  that revenue service and police officers raided Khuselani's offices last  week to gather evidence in a R5-million tax investigation against the company.  Noel
Ngwenya was arrested the same day on seemingly related multiple fraud charges. He was released on bail of R500 000 - a measure of the  gravity of the charges.
Khuselani was founded in 1996 by Ndzeku, Sisulu and Jerome Ngwenya  (then still a practising attorney - he was appointed judge only two years  ago).
Noel Ngwenya, at the time employed by state arms firm Denel, also  played a role in the company. Copies of share certificates from November that  year reflect Ndzeku, Sisulu and Jerome Ngwenya as the joint owners. "It  worked well; there were no problems," Ndzeku says.
The problems were to come later. Judge Ngwenya says: "They [Ndzeku and
Sisulu] were to add value to the business because of their connections  and profiles. This never happened but we incurred the stigma and trouble  that persecutes me to this date."
At the time the partners came together, Ndzeku had just lost a final  appeal against his drugs conviction. Ndzeku was arrested in Cape Town in 1988  and charged with dealing in thousands of mandrax tablets, none of which was found on him. He was sentenced in 1992 to nine years, although the  effective term was three years.
The Appeals Court dismissed Ndzeku's final plea in November 1995 and confirmed his conviction. The judgement was "reported", meaning it was accessible to the legal community.
Judge Ngwenya says he knew Ndzeku had a "problem" as Ndzeku could not  be registered with the Security Officers' Board, which oversaw the private security industry. But he maintains he was not aware of the full  details until much later: "Had I known about his past criminal record I would  not have had any dealings with him. Upon my discovery of the true facts I  cut all ties with him."
Ndzeku maintains Judge Ngwenya knew all along: "He did know about my  case -
I wanted him to help me [as attorney]."
Ndzeku at the time was not only a convict, but also technically a  fugitive from justice, as he failed to report for his sentence and authorities,  for reasons still unclear, neglected to arrest him. But he made progress in  high society. Already in January 1995 he married Phuthi, the daughter of controversial sorghum beer king Mohale Mahanyele, who 18 months later  wed housing minister Mthembi-Mahanyele. Luminaries at Ndzeku's wedding reportedly included Adelaide Tambo and former Zambian president Kenneth
Kaunda.
One former business partner of Ndzeku's says: "He could open doors. He  was connected. He could take you to minister level, to premier level."
During 1998 Noel Ngwenya, having left Denel, formally joined Khuselani,  of which he is now CEO. That same year Ndzeku and Judge Ngwenya helped  found
Swissport South Africa in conjunction with the Swissair group.  Swissport has the security-sensitive job of handling passengers, baggage, cargo and aircraft at South African airports. Judge Ngwenya became Swissport chairperson, with Ndzeku as his alternate director.
But the relationship between the Ngwenyas on the one hand, and Ndzeku  and
Sisulu on the other, deteriorated during the course of that year. Judge
Ngwenya says it was during this time that he chanced upon details of
Ndzeku's conviction from published law reports. He wrote to Swissport
International in August 1998 announcing the withdrawal of Ndzeku from  the
Swissport South Africa board.
The letter read: "Mr Ndzeku is currently a fugitive from justice and  was convicted of a serious matter and has failed to remedy the situation, despite having been afforded both assistance and an opportunity to do  so."
In January 2000, Selebi personally ordered Ndzeku arrested for his 1992 drugs conviction.
Selebi says: "I got a report suggesting that Ndzeku, who had been  convicted of dealing with narcotics and who had been sentenced to nine years, had  been evading arrest After some investigation we learned that Ndzeku was  working for Swissport I got very concerned and disturbed that a convicted  fugitive was working at the airport. That is how I got involved in the Ndzeku matter."
Judge Ngwenya's letter suggesting Ndzeku was removed from Swissport in  1998, and Selebi's statement suggesting Ndzeku's "presence" at Swissport  played a role in his 2000 arrest, seem not to match.
Ndzeku charges his belated arrest was designed to benefit the Ngwenyas.
"Because Jackie [Selebi] was Noel [Ngwenya's] friend, he started to  remove me."
Selebi denies he knew Noel Ngwenya at the time of Ndzeku's arrest: he maintains he first met Ngwenya later that year. "[The arrest] had to do  with someone who had committed a crime; it did not have to do with my  friendship with anybody."
