Ndzeku's release on parole after just 83 days - on the grounds that  he qualified for various remissions and amnesties, and that five-sixths of  his effective three-year sentence could legally be converted to  correctional supervision - led to an outcry from Selebi and others. Ndzeku was rearrested, but in July that year the Pretoria High Court ordered his release.
During Ndzeku's spell in prison two things happened. First, Jerome  Ngwenya was appointed to the Cape High Court Bench. To comply with the  prescription that judges not be involved in business, he handed over his  directorship in
Khuselani to his twentysomething daughter, Thandekile Ngwenya, and  placed his shares in a trust of which she is the beneficiary.
Second, Khuselani landed the R99-million airports security contract, significantly boosting the company's value.
Ndzeku, on his release, went on the warpath against the Ngwenyas. In a  sworn affidavit, Ndzeku claims to have discovered early last year that all  his and
Sisulu's shares were, between 1997 and 2000, irregularly transferred to  both
Ngwenya brothers and to the trust of which Thandekile Ngwenya is beneficiary.
Ndzeku's affidavit continues: "I have never transferred, sold or in any  way whatsoever made over my shares to Jerome Ngwenya or anybody else. I  state that the register of members' share accounts as reflected in the share register is false and purports to reflect what must be fraud."
Ndzeku says he pressed charges against both Ngwenya brothers over the
"stolen" shares. The Johannesburg police commercial crime unit this  week confirmed it had investigated the charges and forwarded the docket to prosecuting authorities for a decision. Judge Ngwenya calls Ndzeku's  charges
"spurious and false".
Ndzeku and Sisulu appear to be at least partially behind other actions against Khuselani and the Ngwenyas. A complaint has been lodged with  the
Judicial Service Commission against Judge Ngwenya. A commission representative confirms there was a complaint, but will not disclose  details and says it will not be investigated pending finalisation of related  civil procedures.
Ndzeku says he was the one who handed information to the revenue  service that sparked the tax investigation against Khuselani and the Ngwenyas.  "I gave the receiver all the information."
For now, authorities seem to be taking seriously at least some of the convict's complaints. Whether the Ngwenyas and Selebi can withstand the  heat remains to be seen.

Original_Search_Title: New Flag, Anthem To Mark Rebirth of War -Torn Nation
Headline: Rwanda Born Again
Body_Text:
Across Rwanda on Monday a new national flag was unfurled to the accompaniment of a new national anthem composed in the name of reconciliation in the wake of the country's 1994 genocidal war.
The new flag, with horizontal green, yellow and blue stripes and a yellow sun in the top right-hand corner, was hoisted at noon in solemn ceremonies across the tiny central African country.
Activity in Kigali came to a halt as the flags went up at major intersections, and many residents were seen listening to the new anthem "Rwanda Nziza" (Beautiful Rwanda in the Kinyarwanda language) over transistor radios.
In the new flag, the green symbolizes hope for prosperity, yellow stands for work, blue for peace and happiness, and the sun for "unity, transparency and the fight against ignorance," according to the government.
Red was one of the principal colors of the old flag, which many people associated with blood and memories of the 1994 civil war in which upwards of a million people, mostly members of the Tutsi minority, were killed.
"This day will remain unforgettable in the history of Rwanda," President Paul Kagame said at a ceremony here attended by tens of thousands of people including his entire cabinet.
"Here at last are symbols for all Rwandans," Kagame said after being presented with the country's new emblem, an ear of sorghum and a coffee branch, symbolizing prosperity, and two shields symbolizing patriotism, with the slogan "Unity, Work, Patriotism."
The old emblem included a pruning knife, one of the weapons used in the genocide.
"There's nothing about Hutus or Tutsis in these symbols," one man said. "The old flag belonged only to the ruling (Hutu) elite ... The new one is nicer, but also has more meaning than the old one."
An elderly gardener agreed, saying: "Finally now all Rwandans will build their country together without distinguishing between ethnic groups, unlike previous regimes."
However, a Kigali resident who described himself as a radical Hutu said he was nostalgic for the old Hutu regime, defeated at the end of the genocide by a Tutsi-led force that is now in power. "It's the burial of the last vestiges of the power of the majority," he said.
The new symbols, adopted last month by the transitional National Assembly, replace ones that had been introduced at independence from Belgium in 1962.
